She couldn't find him, became alarmed, and at dusk they had still not found him...' Henri looks up. 'Victor, are you always to be unhappy? I can offer you no consolation. Your disaster is irreparable. What do you intend to do?'

xoptx 'Please read all of it.'

xoptx 'Tell me I'm still mad - that this is a dream!'

xoptx 'I must set off at once.'

'They went home but William wasn't there, so they returned with servants and torches and searched all night. At five in the morning, they found his body...' Henri's voice catches. 'They found his body. He had been strangled.'
'How do they know?'
'Victor - '
'How, Henri?'
'The marks of the killer's hands on his neck, your father says. The police are looking for man of great size and strength. I'm sure they'll catch him.'
He pauses frowning as he scans the next part.

xoptx Tell him to read the rest.

xoptx That's enough.

'He writes: "Elizabeth was very earnest to see the corpse. At first I attempted to prevent her but she persisted, and entering the room where it lay, hastily examined the neck of the victim, and clasping her hands exclaimed, 'O God! I have murdered my darling child!' At this she fainted, and was restored with extreme difficulty. Weeping and sighing, she revealed that that same evening William had teased her to let him wear a very valuable miniature that she possessed of your mother. This picture is gone, and was doubtless the temptation that urged the murderer to the deed. We have no trace of him at present, although our exertions to discover him are unremitted; but they will not restore my beloved William!
' "Come home, son; you alone can console your cousinElizabeth. She weeps continually, and accuses herself unjustly as the cause of his death. Her words pierce my heart. We are all unhappy, but will not that be an additional motive for you, my son, to return and be our comforter? Your dear mother! Alas, Victor, I thank God she did not live to witness the cruel, miserable death of her youngest darling.
' "Come, not brooding thoughts of vengeance against the assassin, but with feelings of peace and gentleness that will heal, instead of festering, the wounds of our minds. Enter the house of mourning, Victor, with kindness and affection for those who love you, and not with hatred for your enemies." '

xoptx Not vengeance? Is he mad?

xoptx You must leave immediately

Not mad, no. Just a better and more Christian soul than you or I can imagine ourselves to be.
Henri puts out his hand to me. 'Victor... What can I possibly say to comfort you?'

xoptx 'There is nothing.'

xoptx 'Your companionship is my only comfort.'

Henri comes with me to order the horses, endeavouring all the way to raise my spirits.
Henri wants me to talk out my grief, but I prefer to cherish it as a bitter black pill. I know he is alarmed at the brisk manner in which I organize my departure, but I have no concern to spare on him. We walk to the coaching inn where I can hire a cabriole to drive myself home. On the way, Henri makes a futile attempt to raise my spirits.
'Poor William. Dear lovely child, he now sleeps with his angel mother. Whoever saw him bright and joyous in his young beauty must weep over his untimely loss.'
'You knew him better than I, Henri. When I left home he was not much more than a baby.'
'Then your sorrow is doubled, my friend, for the killer has stolen your chance of knowing how sweet a child your brother was.'
Perhaps unreasonably, I feel a surge of cold, anaesthetized anger at his words. He seems to doubt the extent of my grief.

xoptx Ask Henri how he would cope with such news.

xoptx Tell him your own suspicions about the killer.

xoptx Say nothing.

'Come with me, please, Henri, to order the horses for my journey. I'll take a pony trap so I can drive myself, rather than wait for the coach.'
'Of course.'
As we hurry through the streets, he tries to raise my spirits.
'As an only child, I can never know such sorrow as you now feel,' he replies with infuriating sympathy. 'There is but one consolation, and that is that he is at rest. He does not now feel the murderer's grasp. Earth covers his gentle form and he knows no pain. He can no longer be a subject for pity. His family and friends are the greatest sufferers.'
'What of those maxims of the Stoics?' I say. 'That death is no evil, and that the mind of Man ought to be superior to despair.'
Henri shakes his head. 'Even Cato wept over the body of his brother.'

Make haste now.

I explain what is my greatest fear, that it was my own deed that doomed my little brother. I don't know if Henri understands. He only says, 'It's natural for you to think that. Weren't thoseThose were Elizabeth's own words when she set eyes on William's body?.'
He has learned not to hear when I talk about the monster. Perhaps I could speak now with the eloquence and clarity of bereavement, and finally make him believe, but it is not to be. The ponies are hitched to the cart. The coachman hands me a whip.

It's time to go.

'Of course, I have no brothers or sisters,' says Henri, 'so I can only hazard a guess at the depths of your emotion. There is but one consolation, and that is that he is at rest. He does not now feel the murderer's grasp. Earth covers his gentle form and he knows no pain. It isn't William whom I pity. You, his family and friends, are the greatest sufferers.'
I hear him, but whatever turmoil is inside my heart can find no expression in words. I order a two-wheeled cabriole. The ponies are soon hitched. I take the whip and climb up.

God speed you homewards.

Bidding Henri farewell, I set out through streets thronged with people whose bright eyes are again turned on the future. But for me the future holds no shining promise. All I can see in the wide countryside dissolving towards a blue horizon is the shadow of inevitable night.

END OF CHAPTER ONE


CHAPTER TWO
CHAPTER TWO
The Light of My Own Vampire

Though I had no answer to Henri's words of solace, nonetheless they are impressed on my mind and, as I have time to reflect on them now, I once again have cause to appreciate the kind understanding of my dearest friend. Other than these thoughts, myMy journey is very melancholy. To begin with I crack the whip, longing to console my family. But the further south I get, the longer I dally over meals and the changing of horses.

xoptx Why?

xoptx Do you encounter any difficulties?

I hear your sarcasm clearly enough. Let me count them for you. What state will my father be in, blaming himself, as he must, for William's death? And his only other hope for the Frankenstein name, myself, the refugee from his own conscience, guilty of crimes against God and Reason. Elizabeth perhaps still wishes to marry me, in accordance with my parents' wishes when she was first brought into our house. Yet she cannot know what changes have befallen me, whom she always loved as a brother. And if she finds me changed too much, would she still desire marriage from a sense of duty? I think she would, and that may be the most terrible of all my fears.
So then, I could start counting on the other hand, but there's a clutch of reasons to be going on with. Satisfied?

xoptx Not good enough. They need you.

xoptx Spend a few days here, then.

Do you wonder? This countryside is the same through which I travelled to university, full of hope. Ahead are the dim outlines of my ancestral homeland. These are sights that evoke the feelings of youth, as if six years were a day, yet everything might be altered.
See, here is Lausanne already. The journey is almost over. How peaceful these ancient streets are. The rattling of the carriage wheels on cobblestones. A few old men pausing over coffee and chequers to watch me alight. The peal of church bells. Oh, do I have to go on? I would like to stay here for a few days.

xoptx Out of the question!

xoptx All right, but what good would that do?

Nobody even asks to see my travel permits. To think that I delayed my departure for months waiting until these were in order, when I might just as well have torn pages out of the newspaper for all that any of the checkpoint guards are bothered.
Amazing, here is Lausanne already. I could be home in a few hours, if I drove on through. How peaceful these ancient streets are. The rattling of the carriage wheels on cobblestones. A few old men pausing over coffee and chequers to watch me alight. The peal of church bells. Perhaps I'll stop for a while. I could tarry here a few days before going on to Geneva.

xoptx Out of the question!

xoptx What makes you feel that way?

One sudden and desolating change has taken place, but a thousand little circumstances might have by degrees worked other alterations. And those, although they came about more tranquilly during the years I've been absent, might be no less decisive.
The truth is, I dare not advance. I tremble at the thought of a thousand nameless evils, although I am unable to define them.

xoptx So often the way with nameless evils.

xoptx Then stay a while.

xoptx You can't be serious.

You're right. My father and my cousin have no respite from grief - why then should I? Whatever my own feelings, I must overcome this curious reluctance to move forward to confront my fate, and drive on at once to join them.
The road runs by the side of the lake, becoming narrower as I approach my native town. Where before they were hidden in the haze of distance, now the black sides of Jura and the bright summit of Mont Blanc are as solid and real as nursery toys.
I weep at the sight. Dear mountains! Clear streams! My own lovely lake! How do you welcome your wanderer? Your summits are clear. The sky and lake are blue and placid. Everything is at peace.

And here is Geneva.

I remain two days at Lausanne in my painful state of mind. I walk along the lake shore. The waters are placid, all around is calm. The snow-capped mountains, those palaces of nature, are not changed. All that astounds and expands the spirit, and fills us with awe, is in those cloud-piercing icy pinnacles. The weight of millions of years is up there. If there is a God, you will find him in the high unbreathable air of the Alps, my friend, not in some dusty chapel that smells of dead stone and incense.

Now, on to Geneva.

Thus restored, and in a better frame of mind to give succour to my loved ones, I hitch the ponies to the cart and continue the last few miles towards my home.
Yet, as I draw near, grief and fear again threaten to overcome me. I set out too late in the day. Already it's getting dark; the mountains are disappearing in the gloom. The whole panorama to the south appears a vast and dim scene of evil.
I foresee that I am destined to become the most wretched of human beings. Already, in my early twenties, my life seems drained of purpose. My only feelings are of tragic grandeur and artful decay. It seems that I drive through the empty world at the end of time.

You're nearly home.

It is dark when I arrive at the outskirts of the city. The gates are already shut, obliging me to pass the night at Secheron, a village just a mile or two away. The sky is serene, the night quiet, yet I cannot sleep.

xoptx Because William's murderer is out there.

xoptx Perhaps a stroll would help?

Across the lake lies the park of Plainpalais. That's where William was abducted and murdered. I must see it for myself.
As I can't pass through the town, I am obliged to cross the lake in a boat. It is a short voyage. Fortunately I am not lost in pitch darkness as there is lightning playing on the summit of Mont Blanc in the most beautiful figures. Hear that thunder, just a few seconds behind the flash? A titanic storm is sweeping down on us.

You must find shelter.

Across the lake lies the park of Plainpalais. That's where William was abducted and murdered. I must see it for myself.
As I can't pass through the town, I am obliged to cross the lake in a boat. It is a short voyage. Fortunately I am not lost in pitch darkness as there is lightning playing on the summit of Mont Blanc in the most beautiful figures. Hear that thunder, just a few seconds behind the flash? I know the signs. A titanic storm is sweeping down on us and it will be here in moments.

xoptx Find shelter.

xoptx Climb a hill to get a better view.

As I expected, the storm approaches rapidly. Clouds blot out the stars. Fat, slow raindrops start to fall, soon increasing in violence until it is like standing under an icy waterfall. I can see nothing but the occasional flicker of celestial light through a black deluge.

xoptx Danger is near! Turn back.

xoptx Find shelter.

The ferocity of the storm increases every minute. I have lived so long in the city, I forgot the terrible savage beauty of nature unleashed. Peals of thunder burst with a terrific crash over my head, echoing from the surrounding peaks. Vivid flashes of lightning turn the lake into a vast sheet of fire. Then for an instant everything is swallowed in inky darkness, until the eye recovers from the flash.
There's a hut. I saw it briefly. Just a pile of stones with the remnants of a roof. It may at times be a shepherd's home, but its lonely aspect puts me in mind of a tomb. I fear to enter.

xoptx Nonsense, you're getting soaked.

xoptx Trust your instincts. Don't go in.

Nonsense! I know these Swiss storms. I exult in them. As I expected, it approaches rapidly. Clouds blot out the stars. Fat, slow raindrops start to fall, soon increasing in violence until it is like standing under an icy waterfall. I can see nothing but the occasional flicker of celestial light through a black deluge

At least get out of the rain.

Lightning splits the sky as I enter, outlining the bare rafters. The interior is darker than the day before Creation, and the skeleton of the roof offers no shelter from the rain, but at least the piled stones of the wall keep out the wind.
As I sit shivering in the darkness, I become aware of another sound besides the clattering of the storm-rung sky. Somebody sits by the ruined hearth, whispering in the darkness. I seem to catch the name 'Frankenstein' spoken with bitter hatred.

xoptx Run!

xoptx Ask who is there.

I am not alone. The last flicker of lightning from the other side of the lake was enough to show a figure in that clump of trees. I cannot see for this rain.
'Come out! Show yourself!'
Another flash - directly overhead this time. The boom of thunder is enough to flatten me. I see the figure clearly stamped by the lightning's glare. It has been two years, but I recognize it as surely as beholding myself in a looking glass.
That gigantic stature, and the deformity of its face like something peeled from a dissecting tray, are unmistakeable. It is the wretch, the filthy demon, to which I gave life. The tang of ozone in the air brings back every detail of that night when the sparks arose it from itshim as surely as beholding myself in a looking glass.
That gigantic stature, and the deformity of his face like something peeled from a dissecting tray, are unmistakeable. It is the wretch, the filthy demon, to whom I gave life. The tang of ozone in the air brings back every detail of that night when the sparks arose him from his birthing pool.
'What are you doing here?'
The flickering light dies. He is just a shadow against the clouds.

xoptx Run!

xoptx Grab him.

Stumbling and skidding, I descend the slope, fearing at every second to feel the creature's grip close on my neck. I'm covered in mud - blood too; I fell and gashed my leg. Where is the boat? There! Half awash, but I must cast off.
The oars! This one paddle will have to do. Where is he? There, outlined in rain as though a devil caught in rails of fire. Why is he watching me like that? What brought him here... to the very site of my brother's death?
Oh God. Oh God.

He cannot get you now.

It easily eludes me in the dark. How ..? Of course, I cultured an extra layer of cells on itsHe easily eludes me in the dark. How ..? Of course, I cultured an extra layer of cells on his retinas to impart night vision. So there should be -
Yes, there! Two blue glowing eyes turned towards me. But another flash reveals him moving at incredible speed. See, he's scaled fifty feet of that cliff in the space between two bolts.

xoptx Quickly, after it!

xoptx It's got away.

xoptx Quickly, after him!

xoptx He's got away.

There's a hut. Just a pile of stones with the remnants of a roof. It may at times be a shepherd's home, but its lonely aspect puts me in mind of a tomb.
As I enter, lightning splits the sky, outlining the bare rafters. I clasp my hands - in prayer? in fear? - and cry aloud, 'William, dear angel! This is thy funeral, this thy dirge!'
And a voice from the interior of the hut replies, 'Or yours, Frankenstein.'

xoptx Run!

xoptx Ask who is there.

I blunder back out into the storm.
The figure moves closer. Lightning fills the interior of the hovel with a momentary furnace-glare. The face looks back at me, yellow eyes and bared teeth in a face like a skinned animal.
'Adom!'
'You!'
The fiend thatHe whom I created stands before me for a moment, haloed redly in fiery light. Then impenetrable darkness swallows everything.
'Did you kill William?'
There is no reply. I reach out, groping towards it, but my hands sweep at empty air. I can hear the door swinging in the wind. Another lightning flash shows that ithim, but my hands sweep at empty air. I can hear the door swinging in the wind. Another lightning flash shows that he has fled.

xoptx Pursue it.

xoptx Pursue him.

xoptx A narrow escape.

Its guilt or mine? For this is the being that I cast among mankind. I endowed itHis guilt or mine? For this is the being whom I cast among mankind. I endowed him with the will and power to effect purposes of horror. William died because of my foolishness and pride.

Everything will seem better in the day.

How can I make you conceive the anguish I suffer during the remainder of the night? I do not mean the wind and rain. Those I hardly notice, as my imagination works over and over to create scenes of evil and despair.
I revolve in my mind the events which I had until now sought to forget: the whole train of my progress toward the creation of this fiend. Alas! I turned loose into the world a depraved wretch who delights in carnage and misery. For I have no doubt now that it murdered my brother.
I must consider the being that I cast among mankind in the light of my own vampire, my own spirit let loose from the grave, and seemingly impelled to destroy all that is dear to me. My only question now: was William's death itsvillain. Alas! I turned loose into the world a depraved wretch who delights in carnage and misery. For I have no doubt now that he murdered my brother.
I must consider the being whom I cast among mankind in the light of my own vampire, my own spirit let loose from the grave, and seemingly impelled to destroy all that is dear to me. My only question now: was William's death his first crime?
And see, the sky is getting lighter. Daybreak. But it gives me no solace. Night is eternal now.

How does ithe find purchase on a sheer cliff? And these rocks - slippery with mud. I can't keep up. Can't even see. Ah - !

Victor!

The fiend soon reaches the summit of Mont Salève. Is it my fancy, or does it pause there to look down and wave, like a comrade departing on a journey? And then itThe creature soon reaches the summit of Mont Salève. Is it my fancy, or does he pause there to look down and wave, like a comrade departing on a journey? And then he's gone.

It must have murdered your brother.

He must have murdered your brother.

Indeed, for nothing human could have destroyed that fair child. It is the murderer. I cannot doubt it. The mere presence of the idea is an irresistible proof of the fact.
How can I make you conceive the anguish I suffer during the remainder of the night? I do not mean the wind and rain. Those I hardly notice, as my imagination works over and over to create scenes of evil and despair. I revolve in my mind the events which I had until now sought to forget: the whole train of my progress toward the creation of this fiend. Alas! I turned loose into the world a depraved wretch who delights in carnage and misery.
I must consider the being that I cast among mankind in the light of my own vampire, my own spirit let loose from the grave, and seemingly impelled to destroy all that is dear to me. My only question now: was William's death itsHe is the murderer. I cannot doubt it. The mere presence of the idea is an irresistible proof of the fact.
How can I make you conceive the anguish I suffer during the remainder of the night? I do not mean the wind and rain. Those I hardly notice, as my imagination works over and over to create scenes of evil and despair. I revolve in my mind the events which I had until now sought to forget: the whole train of my progress toward the creation of this villain. Alas! I turned loose into the world a depraved wretch who delights in carnage and misery.
I must consider the being whom I cast among mankind in the light of my own vampire, my own spirit let loose from the grave, and seemingly impelled to destroy all that is dear to me. My only question now: was William's death his first crime?
And see, the sky is getting lighter. Daybreak. But it gives me no solace. Night is eternal now.

What happened ..? I fell - struck my head. But I couldn't have fallen all the way here to the bottom of the cliff. I was sixty feet up, it would have killed me.

xoptx The monster saved you.

xoptx Providence spared your life.

Yes, you're right. There's my coat, caught up there on an outcropping rock. By sheerest luck I must have dropped onto that ledge, and it climbed back and carried me down to the bottom of the cliff. And here are its footprints in the mud to prove it. But why would the monster do that? Save my life?

he climbed back and carried me down to the bottom of the cliff. And here are his footprints in the mud to prove it. But why would the monster do that? Save my life?

xoptx To assuage his guilt.

xoptx Because he has other plans for you.

Yes. Whatever crimes I have committed - and they are legion - fate now gives me a sign. I will have a chance to set right my wrongs, as Professor Waldman said. In gratitude, I spend the rest of the night in the open air, allowing the cold and rain to purify my soul for the struggle ahead.
I revolve in my mind the events which I had until now sought to forget: the whole train of my progress toward the creation of this fiend. I see that I turned loose into the world a depraved wretch who delights in carnage and misery. For I have no doubt now that it murdered my brother.
I must consider the being thatvillain. I see that I turned loose into the world a depraved wretch who delights in carnage and misery. For I have no doubt now that he murdered my brother.
I must consider the being whom I cast among mankind in the light of my own vampire, my own spirit let loose from the grave, and seemingly impelled to destroy all that is dear to me. My only purpose now is to lay that spirit, to drive a stake into its heart even at the cost of my own life.
And see, the sky is getting lighter. The town gates will open soon.

He? It! If the Devil himself took shape, he could not be so abhorrent a monster as that which I have unleashed upon the world. And having begun his crimes with the murder of an innocent lad, an angel of a boy whose sweet nature was admired by all, what horrible sins can he now be contemplating?

You must fight back.

How can I fight back? I am powerless. Do you conceive the slightest part of the anguish I am suffering during the remainder of this night? I do not mean the wind and rain. Those I hardly notice, as my imagination works over and over to create scenes of evil and despair.
I revolve in my mind the events which I had until now sought to forget: the whole train of my progress toward the creation of this devil, which I must consider in the light of my own vampire, my own spirit let loose from the grave, and seemingly impelled to destroy all that is dear to me.
Now see, the sky is getting lighter. Daybreak at last. But it gives me no solace. Night is eternal now.

END OF CHAPTER TWO


CHAPTER THREE
CHAPTER THREE
The True Murderer

The gates open at dawn, and I hasten through the familiar streets to my father's house. I anticipated this homecoming so long, yet now I hardly take note of my surroundings. What should I say of last night's encounter?

xoptx Tell them you saw William's killer.

xoptx You must not mention the monster.

That was my first thought. But when I reflect on the story that I have to tell... a being whom I myself formed and endued with life... a chance encounter at midnight among the precipices of an inaccessible mountain. Remember how Henri attributed every mention of the monster to my nervous fever? Knowledge of that illness would give an air of delirium to a tale already quite improbable. Of a certainty, if anyone were to tell me such a story, I would take them for mad.
Besides, the superhuman nature of the monster would elude all pursuit, even if I were so far credited as to persuade my relatives to commence it. And then of what use would be pursuit? Who could arrest a creature capable of scaling the overhanging sides of Mont Salève? By this reasoning, I am resolved to remain silent.

Here is your father's house.

You're wrong. While it is at large, no-one is safe. Oh, I realize I can hardly tell them that the murderer is a being that I myself fashioned and endued with life, and thathe is at large, no-one is safe. Oh, I realize I can hardly tell them that the murderer is a being whom I myself fashioned and endued with life, and whom I had not seen in nearly two years until last night's chance encounter among the precipices of an inaccessible mountain. I am mindful of how Henri treated every mention of the monster as a symptom of my nervous fever. But I can describe meeting a dangerous lunatic in Plainpalais, not far from where the body was found. That will be enough to organize a manhunt.

xoptx If only it were a man.

xoptx If only he were a man.

xoptx Look up. You're home.

I am better aware than any man of the difficulties. I designed the monster with superhuman strength, agility and endurance. ItHe needs little food, is indifferent to discomforts of heat and cold. You saw evidence last night of its heightened senses. You saw evidence last night of his heightened senses. All of these things will make it hard to apprehend. Yet consider the obstacles it faces also. No one would give so hideous a thing refuge. It cannot hide or disguise itself. It has no friend but hatred. For these reasons, I think it All of these things will make it hard to apprehend him. Yet consider the obstacles he faces also. No one would give so hideous a thing refuge. He cannot hide or disguise himself. He has no friend but hatred. For these reasons, I think he cannot remain at large for long.

Here is your father's house.

Still only five in the morning. They will still be abed. I'll go round to the kitchen yard.
'Ah, you there. Thierry, isn't it? Remember me?' I have to make a conscious effort not to address him as 'citizen'.
'Master Victor! We expected you yesterday.'
'Well, here I am. On foot and soaked, as you see. Perhaps you will be so good as to make up the fire in the library. And bring me some hot chocolate.'
'Of course. The family is not yet about, sir. Shall I wake them?'

xoptx 'Tell Miss Lavenza I am here.'

xoptx 'You can get my father.'

xoptx 'No, I'll wait for them to rise.'

Oh, that fire is good. I hadn't realized how the cold had got into my bones overnight.
How strange to be back home. Six years have elapsed like a dream, but for one indelible trace, and here I stand in the same spot where I last embraced my father before setting off to university.
The room seems smaller than I remember, of course - everyone says that, but it's true all the same. And how odd to gaze on the familiar objects of childhood - that carpet with its pattern of peacocks and foliage, the birds' eggs my father collects in this case, the spines of the books like old friends. You know, it is good to be home but at the same time there is something dreadful about it. As though the child we once were had become a ghost trapped amid all these memories.

Here is Elizabeth.

Hearing the swish of fabric against the door frame, I turn and see her standing there. She wears a white gown tied with a bright red sash. The gown is indistinct as smoke in the half-light, but her rich auburn hair catches the glow of the fire and seems to shine.
'I told Thierry not to wake you.'
'I asked Thierry to call you.'
'I was already awake. I heard you arrive.'
'How did you know it was me?'
'I knew.'
As she comes nearer, I see how she has changed. Six years ago she was a pretty, good-humoured girl whom every one loved and caressed. Now she is a woman in stature and expression of countenance. An open and capacious forehead - that's said to betoken an understanding nature, joined to great frankness of disposition as I can see in her hazel eyes. How expressive of mildness they are, and candour, though raw with recent sadness.

xoptx 'How have you been, Elizabeth?'

xoptx 'How is father?'

xoptx 'Tell me about William, if you can bear it.'

'This is the same spot where we last embraced,' says my father, advancing into the library with sorrowful dignity. 'Victor, let me look at you. Six years! Oh, you are a man.'
He smiles, but there is pain in his eyes. 'I feel guilty even at such understandable pleasure as it gives me to see you again.'
'You should not, Father.'
'Grief is a greedy emotion. It would like to blot out all others, yet in fact is the least capable of doing so. Oh, Victor, I wish you had returned three months ago, when my joy could have been unalloyed.'

xoptx 'I'm sorry I stayed away so long.'

xoptx 'How is Elizabeth?'

'No, no. Young men need to spread their wings. I'm sorry this room is so dreary. Is that the best fire Thierry could manage? And he hasn't lit any lamps. You didn't want to come home to a silent, half-dark mausoleum, I'm sure.'
I see the stricken look on his face as he realizes what he's said. Strange how the bereaved often seem almost ashamed when a chance remark slips out to reveal what is obsessing their thoughts.

Ask after your cousin.

'Inconsolable, poor girl. She blames herself - that stupid business with the brooch. I can't very well tell her that people often have rather more sordid reasons for assaulting young children than to steal the contents of their pockets. And of course the arrest of the murderer has given her no comfort.'

xoptx 'It has already been arrested?'

xoptx 'He has already been arrested?'

xoptx 'I met the murderer last night!'

How strange to be back here in the same spot where I last embraced my father before setting off to university. The room seems smaller than I remember, of course - everyone says that, but it's true all the same. And how odd to gaze on the familiar objects of childhood - that carpet with its pattern of peacocks and foliage, the birds' eggs my father collects in this case, the spines of the books like old friends. You know, it is good to be home but at the same time there is something dreadful about it. As though the child we once were had become a ghost trapped amid all these memories.
Come nearer. These pictures over the mantelpiece are hard to see in the twilight. See this one? My poor late mother. Of course she never wore rustic garb nor, I imagine, did she ever kneel by her father's tomb in such an agony of Biblical despair. That's what is taken for art among the well-to-do Swiss. But you know, there's something here. That pale handsome cheek and air of dignity and beauty, those are her. You cannot pity one so strong and full of love.
Oh, and this little portrait... William, of course. He -

Elizabeth is here.

'He is very low. William's murder has rekindled his grief at the death of your mother. Victor, you come to us now to share a misery which nothing can alleviate. I'm sure every mile of your journey has been filled with dread at having to face us all. Yet your presence will, I hope, revive your father, who seems daily to be sinking under his misfortune. I cannot give him the consolation that his only remaining child can.'
'I am sure that he regards you as much his child as I am, dear Elizabeth.'
'Still, you are his son, Victor. Don't stand too aloof. Since they now have the murderer, as they suppose - '
'The murderer has been caught?'

xoptx How can that be?

xoptx Ask her who it is.

'I can still see his sweet face... He was so full of gaiety and loving mischief as he ran off to hide. Oh, Victor, death seems an impossibility! How can he be dead, when I can smell his hair and feel that little pink cheek?'
'He is with us in spirit.'
Tears are flowing down her cheeks. She does nothing to wipe them away. 'In heaven? But I remember him too vividly. How can death erase all that? Each moment I think the door will open and he'll come running in.'

xoptx Explain the stages of grief.

xoptx Embrace her.

'I have some experience with the bereaved families of patients, Elizabeth. It may comfort you to know that it is often difficult for them to accept their loved one's death, especially if the death was sudden and unexpected. The answer is simply that the mind is not accustomed to catastrophic change, such as the abrupt removal of a person, a change of identity or behaviour, or a traumatic relocation. Our sole prior acquaintance with such things comes in dreams or works of fiction. Hence, when such a thing truly occurs, we say, "Surely I am dreaming?" '
She wipes away the tears with a swift brushing movement of her sleeve. She seems almost angry. 'I see you have learned a great deal,' she says.
'Oh yes. It is often helpful to know that one's most violent emotions in times of crisis are simply a reflex reaction shared by the rest of humanity.'
'I see that it would be. I'm sure you apply that same analysis to your own feelings.'
I start to nod, until it occurs to me that I never do.

xoptx Ask how your father is bearing up.

xoptx Ask Elizabeth how she has been.

'Darling Elizabeth! I know it is unbearable. Think how I feel, returning to this household after so long away. I am consumed with guilt. If I had been here, perhaps I could have done something.'
'What could you have done?'
'At least I could have held you, darling, and shared your grief.'
She dries her eyes and lays a tender hand on my cheek. Her fingers are still damp with the tears. 'You are here now.'

xoptx 'How is my father?'

xoptx 'How have you been, Elizabeth?'

'Oh, I am much the same as ever.' She makes a gesture as though brushing cobwebs from between us. 'I wish you had come three months ago, Victor, then your homecoming would have been joyous, rather than this hushed greeting in a silent house.'
'I wish I had come three years ago.'

xoptx Talk about your engagement.

xoptx Talk about William.

'You rarely mention it in your letters, Victor. And you have been away a long time. I almost dread to ask you whether your years in Paris have brought about a change in heart.'
The brisk tone she uses serves to underline, rather than disguise, her depth of feeling. What should I say?

xoptx 'It was Mother's dearest wish that we marry.'

xoptx 'I've missed you, Elizabeth.'

'I can still see his sweet face... He was so full of gaiety and loving mischief as he ran off to hide.' She shakes her head. 'How could anyone be so wicked as to take that blameless life?'

xoptx Tell her you know who murdered him.

xoptx Ask what is being done to catch the murderer.

Ask what is being done to catch the murderer.

'Elizabeth, my darling, the murderer is my responsibility.'
Her eyes flash in the flickering firelight. 'Why should you say that, Victor? She's just a servant.'
'I don't understand. Who is?'
'Justine Moritz. Didn't you know they arrested her?'

xoptx 'On what grounds?'

xoptx 'She's not the one.'

'It has already been done, to the satisfaction of the magistrates at least. They believe William was murdered by our servant, Justine Moritz.'

xoptx 'Incredible!'

xoptx 'What evidence do they have?'

'How kind you are. Everyone else believes in her guilt, and that has made me wretched, for I know that it is impossible. And to see everyone prejudiced in so deadly a manner rendered me hopeless and despairing.'
'She is innocent, my Elizabeth, and that shall be proved. Fear nothing, but let your spirits be cheered by the assurance of her acquittal.'
'Oh, I hope so, Victor. Who is safe, if Justine be guilty of this crime? I rely on her innocence as certainly as I do upon my own. Our misfortune is doubly hard to us, is it not, Victor? We have not only lost our lovely darling boy, but this poor girl, whom I sincerely love, is to be torn away by an even worse fate. If she is condemned, I never shall know joy more. But she will not, I am sure she will not - and then I shall be happy again, even after the sad death of my little William.'

Someone else is coming.

'No evidence. Merely misunderstandings. There is the matter of her absence during the search... The missing brooch... It's all nonsense, but the magistrates have convinced themselves of her guilt.'
'They are mistaken, certainly. I know the murderer. Poor, good Justine is quite innocent.'
'Victor! You're back!'
It's my father. Unhappiness is deeply impressed on his countenance, but he visibly musters himself to welcome me cheerfully. As we embrace, I am searching my mind for some topic other than our mutual disaster, which I do not wish to mar this moment, but Elizabeth cannot contain herself.
'Uncle, did you hear?' she exclaims. 'Victor says that he knows who murdered William.'
'So do we, unfortunately,' says my father, the pretence at good humour falling from his face like a mask. 'For indeed I would rather have been forever ignorant than have discovered so much depravity and ingratitude in one I valued so highly.'
'No, uncle, you are mistaken. Justine is innocent. Tell him, Victor.'

xoptx 'That's right.'

xoptx 'Well, perhaps we had better hear all the facts.'

'The murderer discovered! Good God, how can that be? Who could attempt to pursue such a being? It is impossible. One might as well try to overtake the winds, or confine a mountain stream with a straw.'
Elizabeth looks at me in wonder. 'I don't know what you mean. I'm sure it is a mistake, but they're saying it was Justine Moritz.'

xoptx 'Ridiculous!'

xoptx 'What's the evidence?'

'Well, if she is, God forbid that she should suffer as guilty. She is to be tried today, and I hope, I sincerely hope, that she will be acquitted.'
My father has never liked having to change his mind. It takes him so long to make it up in the first place.
'She is innocent, father. This I know for a fact.'
He puts his hand on my shoulder. 'The judge is no fool, Victor. He is a very dear friend of mine, in fact. You may rely on him to discover the truth. And we will not have long to wait. Justine's trial is at eleven o'clock today.'

Go to the trial.

'No doubt the theft of Elizabeth's brooch,' says my father, 'which William had in his pocket, was motive in part. But I believe there must be some rotten core to her character. There always is in cases of such savagery. Murder arises from hatred or other bad lusts, never from simple financial incentives.'

xoptx 'Servants do often resent an employer's kindness.'

xoptx 'I met the true murderer last night.'

I quickly recount the facts of my encounter, while omitting any mention of having created the fiend or even having met ithim before last night.
'You went across the lake in a thunderstorm at midnight?' says my father. 'Really, Victor, I think it more likely that you dreamt that whole bizarre episode.'
'Then why are my clothes wet through?'
'That hardly proves that you met a disfigured giant on the hillside!' He gives me a very odd look. 'At any rate, the truth will come out at Justine's trial today.'

Attend the trial.

My father nods. 'It's true that no good deed goes unpunished.'
Elizabeth, who has listened in silence but, I cannot help noticing, with mounting incredulity, suddenly says, 'As it is so easy to determine her guilt merely through generalizations, I wonder why they trouble with legal process at all?'
Before either I or my father can reply, she runs from the room in tears.
'Your cousin has few companions,' he says after a thoughtful silence. 'Naturally Justine was careful to ingratiate herself, to the extent that Elizabeth perhaps forgets she was paid to be her friend. At any rate, we will find out the truth today.'
'Today?'
'Yes. Justine goes on trial this morning.'

Go to the trial.

'It? Don't try to demonize malefactors, Victor; it is important to understand the humanity in them that has gone wrong.'She. It's Justine Moritz. I'm sorry, son, I forgot you didn't know.'
'Justine? Why would she murder William? Surely nobody believes that.'
'No one did at first. But several circumstances came out that have almost forced conviction upon us, and her own behaviour has been so confused as to add to the evidence of facts a weight that, I fear, leaves no hope for doubt. But she will be tried to-day, and you will then hear all.'

xoptx 'Is there any evidence of her guilt?'

xoptx 'What does Elizabeth think of that?'

'It was in Plainpalais, during the storm.' I quickly recount the facts of my encounter, while omitting any mention of having created the fiend or even having met ithim before last night.
'I don't understand,' says my father. 'Why would you go out at night, row across the lake in a thunderstorm, and go searching for the spot where William's body was found - which, I must tell you, is not marked in any way?'
'Nonetheless, I did so. I was unable to sleep. At the time I thought it a whim. Now I wonder if Fate intended me to come face to face with the monster.'
'The unfortunate you call a monster, though disfigured perhaps, can only have been a harmless vagabond. If he were the murderer, after all, he would surely have taken the opportunity to murder you.'
This thought has also occurred to me. I am sure the monster's reasons for savingsparing my life do not stem from any benevolence in itshis nature.
'If that "vagabond" was not the murderer, Father, then who?'
'Our servant Justine Moritz. She was arrested shortly after I wrote to you.'

xoptx 'Is there any evidence of her guilt?'

xoptx 'What does Elizabeth think of that?'

'It is better you went away.' She rests her hand on my arm. It's so light, like a bird perching there. 'You've become a man. We're not like brother and sister anymore.'
'Ah, my sweet children reunited!' It's my father. Unhappiness is deeply impressed on his countenance, but he visibly musters himself to welcome me cheerfully.
'I should have come before,' I say as he reaches up to embrace me.
'Indeed, this is a dark time for our family.' Father glances at Elizabeth. 'Have you told him?'
'Told me what?'
Elizabeth bites her lip. 'Our servant, dear little Justine Moritz, has been accused of the murder.'

xoptx 'But she is entirely innocent!'

xoptx 'How very sad, when we trusted her.'

'So it was. Remember that night by her bedside, when she made us clasp our hands?' Elizabeth has lapsed into an odd, clipped tone, which I attribute to grief.
'It is vivid in my memory,' I assure her. 'I remember her very words: "My children, my firmest hopes of future happiness were placed on the prospect of your union. This expectation will now be the consolation of your father. Elizabeth, my love, you must supply my place to my younger child. Alas! I regret that I am taken from you; and, happy and beloved as I have been, is it not hard to quit you all? But these are not thoughts befitting me. I will endeavour to resign myself cheerfully to death and will indulge a hope of meeting you in another world." '
She nods. 'Thank heavens my dear aunt didn't live to see me fail in my trust. I was supposed to raise William like my own son and protect him. And now he has been murdered by - if the magistrates are correct, which I doubt - one whom we almost regarded as one of the household.'

xoptx 'What?'

xoptx 'Don't blame yourself.'

'Our servant - ' she begins, only to be interrupted by a voice from the doorway.
Her face is very pale in the grey dawn light where we stand by the window. 'If I truly believed everything people are now saying, I would account myself doubly to blame. First for letting William out of my sight that day, and second for nurturing in this very household one who - '
She is interrupted by a voice from the doorway.
'More than enough to hang her. On the morning we found my poor boy's body, Justine was taken ill. While she was confined to her bed, one of the other servants was sorting through the laundry. In the clothes Justine wore that night, she found the brooch that Elizabeth gave to William to carry. The one with a picture of your mother inside.'
'I remember it. Not much to tempt an honest girl to murder.'
'It is valuable enough. The case is silver. Oh, I grant you it wouldn't fetch much, but criminals sometimes have a jackdaw mentality. They steal because they covet, not to gain wealth.'
'Did you ask Justine? She may have a perfectly good explanation.'
'In fact I never got the chance. The servant who found the brooch took it straight to the magistrates, and they had Justine arrested.'
'Those magistrates are your own dining companions, Father.'
He spreads his hands. 'I don't want to interfere in the course of the law, son. In any case, on being charged with the fact, the poor girl confirmed the suspicion in a great measure by her extreme confusion of manner.'

'What is Elizabeth's opinion of Justine's guilt?'

'I think she is entirely innocent.'
Hearing the swish of fabric against the door frame, I turn and see her standing there. She wears a white gown tied with a bright red sash. The gown is indistinct as smoke in the half-light, but her rich auburn hair catches the glow of the fire and seems to shine.
'Hello, Elizabeth.'
As she comes nearer, I see how she has changed. Six years ago she was a pretty, good-humoured girl whom every one loved and caressed. Now she is a woman in stature and expression.
As we embrace, she says in my ear, 'Oh Victor, say you do not believe Justine guilty of this terrible crime.'
'It seems very hard to credit.'
'As we all thought at first,' says my father. 'However, as the facts came to light they painted a bleaker picture. Justine has been very confused in her behaviour, too, which hardly bespeaks innocence.'

xoptx 'It's nonsense; she cannot be guilty.'

xoptx 'How sad that he was killed by one he trusted.'

The trial begins at eleven o'clock. My father and Elizabeth are of course obliged to attend as witnesses. In the carriage, inching through the crowd of onlookers surrounding the court, it occurs to me that, of all these thousands, I am the only one - with the exception of that murderous fiend to whom I gave life - who knows the plain and awful truth.
My father and I sit in silence. Elizabeth, however, is jittery with excitement and cannot stop talking. 'Although all seem ready to condemn JustineTake heart. As sure as we may be that God watches over us and always protects the life of the virtuous, I am confident that her innocence will become clear to all.'
'We must trust that the court will determine the truth,' is all my father will say

xoptx How do you feel about this trial?

xoptx It must be hard for Elizabeth.

xoptx You know Justine is innocent. Say so!

It is a living torture. Today's proceedings will decide whether the result of my curiosity and lawless devices will be proved to cause the death of two of my fellow beings: one a smiling lad full of innocence and joy, the other far more dreadfully murdered. I say more dreadfully because the instrument of murder in Justine's case is the law itself, which was created to protect the innocent.

The trial is about to begin.

Elizabeth professes affection for Justine but I think she never really knew her. To my cousin she has always been 'dear little Justine' the faithful servant. But Justine, as I know well, is a real person with her own moods, whims, accomplishments, pleasures and goals. A fully rounded human being, whose human flaws mean that she is deserving of the protection of the law no less - indeed, infinitely more - than if she were the perfect submissive doll that Elizabeth imagines her to be.

xoptx And you know the real Justine?

xoptx The trial is about to begin.

Do you think I have not considered that? A thousand times rather would I confess myself guilty of the crime ascribed to Justine, but I was absent when it was committed. Such a declaration would be seen as the ravings of a madman only. It would not save her.

The trial is about to begin.

You saw her letter to me. I hardly think I need spell out to you the kind of relations that may exist between the eighteen-year-old son of a well-to-do Genovese family and a hearty, uncomplicated young serving girl.
There is not the gulf between classes here in Geneva that existed in France before the Revolution. Yes, I know her as a dear friend.
Moreover, Justine possesses admirable qualities that promised to make her life happy. She should have had a husband and a family, whose lives she would have shaped with passion and wisdom. Now all that may be obliterated in an ignominious grave, and I am the cause. Although there is no possibility of convincing the court of the true sequence of events, perhaps I could concoct a version of the facts that would suffice to acquit her.

xoptx Then speak up now before it's too late.

xoptx They're bringing in the defendant now.

Justine appears calm. She is dressed in black, and her countenance, always engaging, is rendered exquisitely beautiful by the solemnity of her feelings.
'She looks so pale and nervous,' whispers Elizabeth. 'I would not blame her for trembling with so many accusing eyes upon her.'
As far as I can see, Justine carries herself with dignity and confidence, no doubt assured, despite the opprobrium laden upon her, that her guiltlessness will protect her from any calamity. Moreover, mindful that her confused statements at the time of arrest counted against her, I think she must have worked up her mind to this appearance of courage.
She looks around the court and quickly discovers where we are seated. Catching my eye, she gives a strange little grimace. I have seen it on the face of patients during an amputation, holding up steadfastly - because our dignity is always more precious to us than any discomfort - but for those nerve spasms that betray the violent turmoil beneath. Ovid tells us that even Atlas was seen to wince.
Justine, who is of strong character, quickly recovers. She returns my gaze with a smile of sorrowful affection that seems to attest her utter guiltlessness.
'Poor little Justine,' murmurs Elizabeth. 'Give her a little wave to show you've seen her, Victor. I don't think she recognizes you after all these years.'

Let us hear the charges.

The trial begins. After the advocate against her has stated the charge, several witnesses are called. Before long, if I didn't know her for a fact to be innocent, I admit I would start to have doubts.
The facts are damning. She was out the whole of the night on which the murder was committed. Towards morning she was seen by a market-woman not far from the spot where William's body was afterwards found. The woman asked Justine what she was doing there, but she looked very strangely and only returned a confused and unintelligible answer. She returned to the house about eight o'clock in the morning, and when the cook asked where she had spent the night, she replied that she had been looking for William and demanded earnestly if there had been any news. When shown his body, she fell into violent hysterics and kept to her bed for several days.
The advocate now brings out the brooch that the servant found in Justine's pocket. He calls on Elizabeth, who testifies in a faltering voice that it was the same that, an hour before the child was missed, she had placed round his neck. At this, a murmur of horror and indignation fills the court.

xoptx Justine must have a good explanation.

xoptx There's no point subjecting yourself to this.

My father looks up in surprise. 'Victor, where are you going?'
'I cannot bear this.'
'Of course, I should have thought. You have not yet had time to grieve for William, and now this trial reopens the wounds.'
Elizabeth too gives me a look of understanding. 'Do not subject yourself to it, dearest. Your father and I are here to show that our family is not ready to abandon our faith in little Justine.'
I rush from the court in agony, unable to sustain the horror of my situation. Justine casts me a stricken look. But the tortures of the accused do not equal mine. Unhappy victim though she is, Justine is sustained by innocence, while I feel the fangs of remorse tearing my bosom, that will never forgo their hold because my guilt is real.I am sickened by self-loathing. I should have the strength to remain and give her what comfort I can by my presence, but I cannot. Guilt overwhelms me.

xoptx You can ask Elizabeth tonight how it went.

xoptx The verdict will be delivered tomorrow.

'I spoke as a character witness,' she tells me over dinner. 'I told them that Justine seems to me to be the most amiable and benevolent of human creatures. And the affection and care she showed to your mother in her final days - that is hardly the manner of one who could in cold blood strangle an innocent boy.'
'There are not evil men and good men, like two species on the Earth,' says my father, shaking his head. 'It is the curse of Man since the Fall that wickedness and virtue live in the same breast, constantly at war. This was the theme of the Schadenfests of my youth, where masked men might come and smash your shop window, and the next day those same men would be first on the scene to help you fix it.'
'You are describing the pathological mind, Father.'
'I don't believe it is a flaw in the mind or the soul. Circumstances can drive any man to commit any deed.'

She may yet be found not guilty.

It is time for Justine to speak. Her countenance has altered during the morning. Under the veneer of calm I have seen flashes of surprise, horror and misery. Sometimes she struggles to hold back tears. However, when the judges call on her to plead her defence, she rises to her feet staunchly and, collecting herself, speaks in a strong voice, though one shaking with emotion.
'God knows how entirely I am innocent. But I do not pretend that my protestations should acquit me. I rest my innocence on a plain and simple explanation of the facts which have been adduced against me, and I hope the character I have always borne will incline my judges to a favourable interpretation where any circumstance appears doubtful or suspicious.'

xoptx Let us hear her version of events.

xoptx There's no need for you to stay for the whole trial.

'I will give full account of my whereabouts on the night the murder was committed. By the permission of my mistress, Elizabeth Lavenza, I passed the evening at the house of my aunt, who lives in the village of Chêne about an hour's walk from the town. It was about nine o'clock when I got back to Geneva. In the street I met a man who asked me if I had seen anything of the child who was lost. I was alarmed by this account, and passed several hours in the Plainpalais park looking for him. When I got back, the city gates were shut. I knew some people who live in a cottage nearby, but when I got there the lights were out and I didn't want to wake them, so I spent several hours in their barn.
'Unable to rest or sleep, I set out as soon as it was light to resume the search for William Frankenstein. Witnesses have said they saw me near the spot where his body was found. I do not dispute it, I merely say that it was without my knowledge. That I was bewildered when questioned by that market-woman was not surprising, since I had passed a sleepless night, and the fate of poor William was yet uncertain.'

xoptx There are inconsistencies.

xoptx Her testimony seems honest.

Yes. Did the man in the street say to her, 'William Frankenstein has gone missing?' Surely not. He would have merely told her that a search party was out for a missing child. Of course, perhaps he recognized her. The encounter in the street might, after all, have taken place outside our very door. But in that case why be vague? Why not say, 'As I was nearing home, a man recognized me and told me that the household was out looking for Master William'?
Also, she admits being near the scene of the crime. Nobody saw her before the market-woman did, however, despite my father and the servants being out with torches all night.
I am the only one who knows for a certainty that she is not the killer, and even I find elements of her testimony hard to accept. I am afraid there's little chance of an acquittal unless I can produce some firm evidence. But what?

The advocate is asking her about the brooch.

She ought to be more organized in her thoughts. The magistrates will recognize her to be an uneducated girl, without the skill to marshal logic or rhetoric to her aid, but that may not necessarily count in her favour. They may say to themselves, 'She is confused because she is just a blameless serving girl,' or they might say, 'This is exactly the sort of crime a stupid lower-class girl like that might commit.'

Why not? Justine is without blame and, like the rest of us, she clings to her faith that the law exists to apportion justice. But look at the faces of everyone around us. Their minds are already made up. See that old man scowling at her? He is furious that a serving girl like her even has the temerity to defend herself. And that woman there, with pursed lips. She's gripping the seat in front so hard that her knuckles are white. She could pass by a whore in the street and not unveil a look of such disfavour as she has for my poor Justine.

How does she explain the stolen jewellery?

'Concerning the stolen brooch I can give no account, but I did not steal it. I know how heavily and fatally this one circumstance weighs against me, but I have no power of explaining it and, when I have expressed my utter ignorance, I am only left to conjecture concerning the probabilities by which it might have been placed in my pocket. But here also I am checked. I believe that I have no enemy on Earth, and none surely would have been so wicked as to destroy me wantonly. Did the murderer place it there? I know of no opportunity afforded him for so doing. In any case, why should he have stolen the brooch, to part with it again so soon?'

xoptx A good point.

xoptx Hardly in her favour.

Not at all. Her argument actually supports those who accuse her. For why indeed, as she says, would somebody steal the brooch merely to implicate her?
Knowing who it was that really committed this dreadful crime, I must assume that it is set upon a project to punish me. The notebook in the dressing gown that it took from my lab would have informed it of my home town. Somehow it found my brother and choked the life out of him.
But why frame Justine? There was a sketch I did of her that I used as a bookmark - could it have recognized her from that? It makes no sense, though. Even if it hates me for creating it, how can ithe is set upon a project to punish me. The notebook in the dressing gown that he took from my lab would have informed him of my home town. Somehow he found my brother and choked the life out of him.
But why frame Justine? There was a sketch I did of her that I used as a bookmark - could he have recognized her from that? It makes no sense, though. Even if he hates me for creating him, how can he be so callous as to dispose of blameless lives in this way?

She's finishing her testimony.

No indeed. Her argument actually supports those who accuse her, as what reason could the murderer have for stealing the brooch merely to implicate her? Even I, knowing who it was that really committed this dreadful crime, am at a loss to explain why ithe should wish to punish Justine.

xoptx Was there anything in your notebook?

xoptx She's finishing her testimony.

She's finishing her testimony.

There was a sketch I made of her one afternoon when we took a picnic out in the boat. I remember I used it as a bookmark. Could it have recognized her from that? It makes no sense, though. Even if it hates me for creating it, how can ithe have recognized her from that? It makes no sense, though. Even if he hates me for creating him, how can he be so callous as to dispose of blameless lives in this way?

They're about to call witnesses.

'I commit my cause to the justice of my judges,' concludes Justine, not daring to look at those stern countenances, 'yet I see no room for hope. I beg permission to have a few witnesses examined concerning my character. If their testimony shall not overweigh my supposed guilt, I must be condemned, although I would pledge my salvation on my innocence.'
Several witnesses are called. All have known Justine for many years. Some step up to speak well of her. But fear, and hatred of the crime of which they suppose her guilty, renders those few timorous. Others have not shown up at court at all, being unwilling to come forward.
'How her friends desert her in this moment of greatest need!' said Elizabeth, clutching my hand in agitation. 'Will even her last resource, that excellent disposition and irreproachable conduct we could attest to on a thousand occasions, fail her now?'

xoptx Tell Elizabeth to speak up.

xoptx Offer to appear as a character witness.

Elizabeth sends a note to the judges desiring permission to address the court.
'I am,' she begins, 'the cousin of the unhappy child who was murdered. Or rather I could say I am his sister, for I was educated by and have lived with his parents long before his birth. It may therefore be judged indecent of me to come before the court today. But when I see a fellow creature about to perish through the cowardice of her pretended friends, I wish to be allowed to speak, that I may say what I know of her character.
'I am well acquainted with the accused. I have lived under the same roof as her for some seven years. During all that period she appeared to me the most amiable and benevolent of human creatures. She nursed Madame Frankenstein, my aunt, in her last illness with the greatest affection and care, and afterwards attended her own mother during a tedious illness, in a manner that earned the admiration of all who knew her. After which she again lived in my uncle's house, where she was beloved by all the family.'

xoptx Elizabeth's testimony might help.

xoptx This is hopeless. Don't stay to hear more.

Of course. Excellent Elizabeth - this is Geneva, they love to hear a meek, well-spoken girl like her. None of your musket-wielding French harpies for this crowd. But the approval is all for Elizabeth herself. It is her generous interference they applaud, not the defendant.
You see - ? Justine stands and the applause turns to jeers. Now we can add blackest ingratitude to her crimes. Public indignation is adamantine, my friend. It screeches so loud as to deafen Reason. I saw it all in Paris. The crowd, having made up its mind, thinks and acts as a single entity. Not one of these people clamouring for the poor girl's punishment is any longer amenable to facts. They have confused the enormity of the deed, and their consequent thirst for vengeance, with the needs of justice.

Let's go.

It's horrible to witness - like seeing someone slip inexorably towards a precipice, with no power to prevent the calamity. If only I could do something. I don't for a minute doubt that the monster murdered my brother. But could it also in itshe also in his hellish sport have cast this innocent girl to death and ignominy?
Poor Justine. Elizabeth's panegyric achieves the effect only of extolling her own virtues, for public ire is already so aroused that they cannot view that weeping girl in the dock as anything but a viper who betrayed the esteem in which her mistress held her. You can see in the countenance of the judges that their minds are made up.
And yet the tortures of the accused do not equal mine. Unhappy victim though she is, Justine is sustained by innocence, while I feel the fangs of remorse tearing my bosom, that will never forgo their hold because my guilt is real.I am sickened by self-loathing. The crime is mine, or stems from my primum movens. Yet Fate has determined that others shall suffer for it, while I remain the centre of the storm.

Let's see what verdict the judges return.

I tell the court how Justine came to our household. 'Madame Moritz, her mother, was a widow with four children, of whom Justine was the third. Justine - that is, the defendant - had always been the favourite of her father but, after his death, mother and daughter were at loggerheads. My late mother observed this and, when Justine was twelve years of age, prevailed on Madame Moritz to allow her to live at our house.
'My mother conceived a great attachment for her, by which she was induced to give her an education superior to that which she had at first intended. This benefit was fully repaid by Justine's gratitude. I do not mean that she made any professions - I never heard one pass her lips - but you could see by her eyes that she adored her mistress. Although Justine's disposition is gay and in many respects inconsiderate, yet she paid the greatest attention to every gesture of my mother. She thought her the model of all excellence and endeavoured to imitate her phraseology and manners, so that even now she often reminds me of her.'
The prosecution advocate has been trying to interrupt me. 'I beg the court's indulgence, but I am curious as to your use of the term “inconsiderate”.'

xoptx Elaborate.

xoptx Gloss over it.

I tell the court how Justine first came to live in our household at the age of twelve, was a playmate to my cousin and me, grew up with us, how she nursed my mother through her final illness, and the comfort she gave to Elizabeth and the maternal affection she bestowed on William after my mother's death.
'But, Monsieur Frankenstein,' says the prosecuting advocate, 'you have spent the last six years in Paris. How can you testify as to the defendant's adult character?'
'Because I know goodness when I see it, sir. Justine Moritz had a thousand opportunities to steal from us. I would happily leave a thousand thalers on a table where she was dusting, confident that not a single coin would be touched. Unless it had grime upon it.'
The advocate scowls at the nervous titters of laughter. 'Theft is not really the crime that most concerns us today, though, is it?'
'It is the major part of your circumstantial evidence against Miss Moritz. When that is removed, what motive could she have had for killing one she loved?'
'The semblance of love is not hard to affect – ' he tilts his finger in her direction – 'in one of this hardened and dissembling nature. That will be all, thank you, Monsieur.'

xoptx Elizabeth should testify also.

xoptx You've done all you can.

'By that I mean only that she is disinclined to think deeply about matters. She lives, as many do, in the moment. In my opinion she is not the kind of person to plan a crime in meticulous detail, as - '
'Ah, indeed!' says the advocate, smacking his hand down in triumph. 'And we have seen that this crime, appalling though it was, lacks any evidence of careful planning. That will be all, Monsieur Frankenstein.'
With a shrug, I leave the witness stand.

xoptx Why did you say that?

xoptx Elizabeth should testify also.

xoptx You've done all you can.

'I mean only that she is not given to excessive deliberation. That is, she is considerate of the feelings of others, but it is not in her nature to consider her future plans from every angle. That is no doubt why she became a maid of service rather than, say, a lawyer.'
There is a gale of laughter and the advocate resumes his seat with an indignant billowing of robes.
I continue: 'When my dear mother died every one was too much occupied in their own grief to notice poor Justine, who had attended her during her illness with the most anxious affection. We only learned later that over the ensuing months, one by one, her brothers and sister died. The cause was never explained.
'The mother's conscience became troubled. She may have thought the deaths of her favourites was a judgement from heaven to chastise her partiality. She was a Roman Catholic, and I believe her confessor must have confirmed that notion. Accordingly, a few months after my mother's death, Justine was called home by her repentant mother.
'Poor girl, she wept when she quitted our house. I am told she was much altered since the death of my mother. Grief had given softness and a winning mildness to her manners, which had before been remarkable for vivacity.'
The advocate is on his feet again. 'You say that her nature, previously insolent, had now become crafty and dissembling?'


xoptx 'That is your interpretation.'

xoptx 'I was not here.'

'I didn't say so, sir. I merely present the facts as I see them, so that the court may gain a better understanding of the defendant's character. To go on, her residence at her mother's house was not of a nature to restore her gaiety. The woman was very vacillating in her repentance. She sometimes begged Justine to forgive her unkindness, but much oftener accused her of having caused the deaths of her brothers and sister.
'Perpetual fretting at length threw Madame Moritz into a decline, which at first increased her irritability, but she is now at peace forever. She died on the first approach of cold weather, at the beginning of this last winter. Justine has just returned to us and, I assure you, we have had no reason to complain at her conduct.'
The advocate nods, excusing me from the stand. 'Thank you, Monsieur Frankenstein. Your testimony has been very illuminating.'

xoptx Why did you say all that?

xoptx Elizabeth should testify also.

xoptx You've done all you can.

Consider. The monster slew my brother. I have good reason to suppose it is bent on a program of revenge against me. Should I trust the law to hunt it down? They don't know its nature or capabilities. It could elude them forever. In any case, after what it has done, simple hanging will not suffice as punishment. I am itshe is bent on a program of revenge against me. Should I trust the law to hunt him down? They don't know his nature or capabilities. He could elude them forever. In any case, after what he has done, simple hanging will not suffice as punishment. I am his creator and victim, so it falls to me to enact justice.
Ah, it troubles you that Justine thereby becomes the innocent dupe in all this? Set aside your qualms, my friend. If my experiences in Paris taught me anything, it is that the lower classes would slit all our throats simply out of envy. She didn't murder William for that brooch, but she could have done.

A ruthless analysis.

'Then, sir, I must ask what relevance your testimony has on these proceedings. Is it not the case that you have been absent from Geneva these last six years? Can you truly ask us to believe you are sufficiently intimate with a servant that you can vouch for her character after so long?'

xoptx 'Nonetheless I have a right to continue.'

xoptx 'I suppose not.'

You think I'm untroubled by all this? If I could secure Justine's acquittal, I would. It's already gone too far for that. The judges don't believe her story and, even if they did, a not guilty verdict would cause a riot. I am making the best of a bad job, that's all - and if it stains my conscience further, well, I am so steeped in blood that, should I wade no more, et cetera.'

There's a good role model.

The advocate makes an irritable gesture and resumes his seat.
'To continue, I understand that the defendant's residence at her mother's house was not of a nature to restore her gaiety. The woman was very vacillating in her repentance. She sometimes begged Justine to forgive her unkindness, but much oftener accused her of having caused the deaths of her brothers and sister.
'Perpetual fretting at length threw Madame Moritz into a decline, which at first increased her irritability, but she is now at peace forever. She died on the first approach of cold weather, at the beginning of this last winter. Justine has just returned to us and, I assure you, we have had no reason to complain at her conduct.'
The advocate nods, excusing me from the stand. 'Thank you, Monsieur Frankenstein. Your testimony has been very illuminating.'

xoptx Why did you say all that?

xoptx Elizabeth should testify also.

xoptx You've done all you can.

The advocate nods, excusing me from the stand. 'Thank you, Monsieur Frankenstein. Your testimony has been very illuminating.'

xoptx Why did you say all that?

xoptx Elizabeth should testify also.

xoptx You've done all you can.

'She was warmly attached to the child who is now dead, and acted towards him like a most affectionate parent. I do not hesitate to say, notwithstanding all the evidence produced against her, that I believe her to be entirely innocent. She had no motive for such an action. As to the bauble on which the chief proof rests - we had often played with that and I had even allowed her to see herself in the mirror wearing it. If she had earnestly asked me for it, I should have willingly given it to her, so much do I esteem and value her friendship.'

xoptx Hear that murmur of approval?

xoptx This will do nothing to acquit Justine!

My father looks up in surprise. 'Victor, where are you going?'
'I cannot bear this.'
'Of course, I should have thought. You have not yet had time to grieve for William, and now this trial reopens the wounds.'
As I leave the courtroom, Justine casts me a stricken look. But the tortures of the accused do not equal mine. Unhappy victim though she is, Justine is sustained by innocence, while I feel the fangs of remorse tearing my bosom, that will never forgo their hold because my guilt is real.I am sickened by self-loathing. I should have the strength to remain and give her what comfort I can by my presence, but I cannot. Guilt overwhelms me.

xoptx Elizabeth will tell you later how it went.

xoptx The verdict will be delivered tomorrow.

It's in the hands of the judges now. As I leave the courtroom, I catch a last glimpse of Justine's face as she's led away. While I was away at college, whenever I pictured her face it was with an expression of sauciness, or hot temper, or blank and blameless joy - but never the awful look of blank desolation that she wore in the dock today.
And yet the tortures of the accused do not equal mine. Unhappy victim though she is, Justine is sustained by innocence, while I feel the fangs of remorse tearing my bosom, that will never forgo their hold because my guilt is real.I am sickened by self-loathing. The crime is mine, or stems from my primum movens. Yet Fate has determined that others shall suffer for it, while I remain the centre of the storm.

xoptx You can discuss the case at home this evening.

xoptx Wait until the verdict is delivered tomorrow.

'I cannot believe the judges can fail to find her innocent,' says Elizabeth over dinner. 'Justine seems to me to be the most amiable and benevolent of human creatures. Is that not obvious to all? And the affection and care she showed to your mother in her final days - that is hardly the manner of one who could in cold blood strangle a lovely little boy.'
'There are not evil men and good men, like two species on the Earth,' says my father, shaking his head. 'It is the curse of Man since the Fall that wickedness and virtue live in the same breast, constantly at war. This was the theme of the Schadenfests of my youth, where masked men might come and smash your shop window, and the next day those same men would be first on the scene to help you fix it.'
'You are describing the pathological mind, Father.'
'I don't believe it is a flaw in the mind or the soul. Circumstances can drive any man to commit any deed.'

She may yet be found not guilty.

I pass a night of undiluted wretchedness. In the morning I come straight to the courthouse without stopping to have breakfast, so as to be here when the verdict is announced. My lips and throat are so parched that I can hardly speak.
'Ah, it's you Monsieur Frankenstein,' says the officer. I dare not ask the fatal question, but he can guess why I'm here. 'The ballots have been thrown; they were all black.'
So. Justine is condemned to die.

xoptx How does that make you feel?

xoptx You could still save her.

I cannot pretend to describe what I am feeling. I have before experienced sensations of horror, and I have tried to find the words to frankly convey them to you, but I don't have words to describe this heart-sickening despair.
'If it makes it easier, sir,' says the official, seeing me distraught, 'the accused has confessed her guilt.'
'Confessed?'
'Last night. A confession isn't really necessary in a case as glaring as this, but I'm glad of it. It's better for her soul, and none of our judges likes to condemn a criminal upon circumstantial evidence, be it ever so decisive.'

Elizabeth must be told.

Do not, I pray you, torment me with greater burden of guilt than I already feel. How should I save her? No one will believe my story of a monster, and its unnatural powers of endurance and strength, which I know better than anyone, put ithis unnatural powers of endurance and strength, which I know better than anyone, put him far beyond pursuit. I am trapped in a nightmare from which there is no waking.
'If it makes it easier, sir,' says the official, seeing me distraught, 'the accused has confessed her guilt.'
'Confessed?'
'Last night. A confession isn't really necessary in a case as glaring as this, but I'm glad of it. It's better for her soul, and none of our judges likes to condemn a criminal upon circumstantial evidence, be it ever so decisive.'

Elizabeth must be told.

The moment I return home, Elizabeth jumps up from the breakfast table to hear the verdict. She must read it in my face, because her eyes dim with tears and she presses her hand to her mouth as if distrusting what she might say.
'Elizabeth dear, you cannot be surprised. You know that the judges would rather that ten innocent should suffer than that one guilty person should escape.'
'How is that justice?'
'But she has confessed.'
This news is a dire blow to poor Elizabeth, who has all along maintained Justine's innocence. Her vehemence wilts from her and I have to support her as she sinks into a chair.
'Oh, Victor, how shall I ever again believe in human goodness? Justine, whom I loved and esteemed as my sister... How could she put on those smiles of mildness and innocence only to betray? I would have sworn she was incapable of any severity or ill-humour, and yet she has committed the most terrible of all crimes.'
'How often have I heard it said of the most hardened criminals,' says my father over his newspaper, 'that butter wouldn't melt in their mouths?'
A servant enters. 'There's a message for you, sir,' she says, handing me a note.

Read it.

It's from Justine's defence counsel. 'She asks to see you, Elizabeth.'
To my surprise, she agrees to go. This is the last straw for my father, who has been out of countenance all morning. 'How can you even consider it, Elizabeth, after what Victor has just told us? She confessed!' he says in a hoarse voice.
