He should know better than to try to change Elizabeth's mind. 'I will go, although she is guilty. I would regret every day of my life if I left her to go to her death without the chance to speak her words of repentance. And you, Victor, shall accompany me - I cannot go alone.'
'Not I! I agree with Father. I will not give one crumb of comfort to William's killer.'
'Think how it would look, Elizabeth,' pleads my father.
'I do, Uncle.' Later, as she's leaving, she adds to me: 'I think how it would look to the angels above, not the burghers of Geneva.'

Speak to her when she returns.

The idea of this visit is torture to me, yet I cannot refuse.

Go with Elizabeth to the prison.

Justine is sitting on some straw. Her hands are manacled and her head rests on her knees. She rises on seeing us enter the gloomy cell and, when we are left alone with her, she throws herself at Elizabeth's feet. Both women are weeping bitterly.
'Oh, Justine!' says Elizabeth. 'Why did you rob me of my last consolation? I relied on your innocence at least. Although I was very wretched after yesterday's trial, I was not so miserable as I am now.'
'Then do you also believe that I am so wicked?' Her voice is suffocated with sobs.
'Rise, my poor girl,' said Elizabeth, 'why kneel, if you are innocent? I believed you guiltless, in spite of every evidence, until I heard that you had yourself declared your guilt.'
'I did confess, but I confessed a lie. I did it to obtain absolution, but now that falsehood lies heavier at my heart than all my other sins. God forgive me! Ever since I was condemned, my confessor has besieged me, until I almost began to think that I was the monster he said I was. He threatened excommunication and hellfire. Dear lady, I had none to support me. What could I do? In an evil hour I subscribed to a lie.'
Elizabeth catches my eye. I can see she wants to believe.

xoptx Say something to comfort her.

xoptx Say nothing.

'I believe it to be every Christian's right to absolution,' I say, though in fact I know almost nothing of the Roman Catholic faith. 'If the priest forced you to make a false confession, Justine, the sin is his, not yours.'
Mustering a smile through her sad tears, Elizabeth throws me a radiant look of gratitude over Justine's shoulder.

Tell Justine to go on.

Justine pauses, weeping, and then continues. 'I am horror-struck, my friends, to think you might believe me capable of such a crime. Dear William! Dearest blessed child, I soon shall see you again in heaven, where we shall all be happy.'
'Oh, Justine!' cries Elizabeth. 'Forgive me for having for one moment distrusted you. But do not mourn, my dear girl. I will everywhere proclaim your innocence. Yet you must die. You, my playfellow, my companion, my more than sister. I never can survive so horrible a misfortune.'
'Dear, sweet Elizabeth, do not weep. You ought to raise me with thoughts of a better life, and elevate me from the petty cares of this world of injustice and strife. Don't drive me to despair.'
'I will try to comfort you,' says Elizabeth sadly, 'but I fear that is impossible, for there is no hope. Yet heaven bless thee, my dearest Justine, with resignation to your fate. Oh! how I hate the shows and mockeries of this world! When one creature is murdered, another is immediately deprived of life by a slow torturing. Then the executioners, their hands yet reeking with the blood of innocence - '

xoptx Break in on this outburst!

xoptx Let her speak.

I take Elizabeth aside. 'This isn't helping.'
'Oh, but Victor, I must comfort her.'
I don't ask her how a diatribe against capital punishment, delivered in lurid terms, is meant to comfort Justine. Elizabeth would only feign innocent incomprehension. Suddenly I am disgusted with her games - with all such dainty human cruelties.

xoptx It's time you both left.

xoptx Tell Elizabeth you must talk to Justine alone.

Elizabeth returns with a brave smile in place of her earlier tears. She explains that Justine only confessed in order to receive absolution from the priest. I see my father's look on hearing this, but he bites his tongue.
Elizabeth cannot restrain herself from embracing me. 'Thank heaven, Victor. I never could again have known peace if I had been deceived in trusting her. For the short time that I did believe her guilty, after hearing of her confession, I was torn apart with anguish. Now my heart is lightened. The innocent suffers, but she whom I thought amiable and good has not betrayed the trust I reposed in her. I am consoled.'
Amiable cousin! Her thoughts are so mild and gentle. She hates to think ill of anyone, especially one she loves as dearly as her faithful maid. But I - I am a wretch, and none can guess at the misery that I must endure in silence.

Ah, good. She is consoled. Angels may weep, Justine faces death, and I am racked with shame and guilt. But Elizabeth at least can sleep soundly tonight, as her faith in human nature has not had to face the litmus test that would destroy it.

Attend Justine's execution.

'The executioners believe they have done a great deed. They call this retribution. Hateful word! When they say retribution, I know greater and more horrid punishments are going to be inflicted than the gloomiest tyrant has ever invented to satiate his utmost revenge.'
Elizabeth pauses and seems to reflect on the horrific effect her words are having on Justine. 'Yet this is perhaps scant consolation for you, my Justine,' she continues, 'except that you may be grateful to quit so miserable a den. Alas! I would I were in peace with my aunt and my lovely William, escaped from a world which is hateful to me, and the visages of men which I abhor.'
Justine smiles languidly. 'This, dear lady, is despair, and not resignation. Talk of something else, something that will bring peace, and not increase of misery.'
During this conversation I have retired to a corner of the cell, trying to distance myself from Elizabeth's extraordinary outburst which, I can only assume, is intended to chastise both me and Justine. I feel their eyes on my back. Should I speak?

xoptx Nothing you can say will help.

xoptx Tell Elizabeth to leave you alone with Justine.

True, silence better conceals the horrid anguish that currently possesses me. Despair! Who dares talk of that? The poor victim, who tomorrow will pass the dreary boundary between life and death? She cannot feel such deep and bitter agony as mine. I gnash my teeth, grinding them together and uttering a groan from my inmost soul.
Indeed, how can I trust myself to speak when it is my actions that have doomed both my brother and this poor lovely girl who has been made the scapegoat for his murder? And now to hear Elizabeth twist a gentle knife between her ribs with talk of reeking blood, retribution, and her own idle whim for a romantic death. It is insane! A nightmare! I gnash my teeth, grinding them together and uttering a groan from my inmost soul.
Justine gives a start. 'Dear sir, you are very kind to visit me. You, I hope, do not believe that I am guilty.'
I cannot answer; my mouth is bone dry. 'No, Justine,' says Elizabeth. 'He is more convinced of your innocence than I. Even when he heard that you had confessed, he did not credit it.'
'I truly thank you both. In these last moments I feel the sincerest gratitude towards those who think of me with kindness. How sweet is the affection of others to such a wretch as I am! It removes more than half my misfortune. I feel as if I could die in peace, now that my innocence is acknowledged by you, dear lady, and you, Victor.'

It's time you left.

We came to comfort Justine; I think she comforted us. As we say farewell, she indeed seems to have gained the resignation she desired. But I, the true murderer, feel the never-dying worm alive in my bosom, which allows of no hope or consolation.
I'm surprised to find I'm crying. Elizabeth also weeps, but hers is the misery of innocence, which, like a cloud that passes over the fair moon, hides but cannot tarnish its brightness. Anguish and despair have penetrated into the core of my heart. I bear a hell within me, which nothing can extinguish.
As the cell door clangs shut, Elizabeth gives a cry of great passion. 'I wish,' she calls to Justine, 'that I were to die with you. I cannot live in this world of misery.'
Justine reaches through the bars to clasp Elizabeth's hand. 'Farewell, sweet lady, dearest Elizabeth, my beloved and only friend. May heaven in its bounty bless and preserve you. May this be the last misfortune that you will ever suffer. Live, and be happy, and make others so.'

xoptx Walk Elizabeth home.

xoptx Tell her you'll see her tonight.

As we leave the prison, Elizabeth says, 'Thank heaven, Victor. I never could again have known peace if I had been deceived in trusting her. For the short time that I did believe her guilty, after hearing of her confession, I was torn apart with anguish. Now my heart is lightened. The innocent suffers, but she whom I thought amiable and good has not betrayed the trust I reposed in her. I am consoled.'
Amiable cousin! Her thoughts are so mild and gentle. She hates to think ill of anyone, especially one she loves as dearly as her faithful maid. But I - I am a wretch, and none can guess at the misery that I must endure in silence.Ah, good. She is consoled. Angels may weep, Justine faces death, and I am racked with shame and guilt. But Elizabeth at least can sleep soundly tonight, as her faith in human nature has not had to face the litmus test that would destroy it.

Attend Justine's execution.

'I have some errands to run,' I tell Elizabeth, 'so I'll see you at dinner.'
She looks bewildered. 'But, Victor, I thought we might walk a while and talk about Justine.'
'Justine is about to be unjustly executed for a murder she did not commit. Beyond this, I think there's nothing more we can say - unless it's a prayer for her soul. Will you come with me to the execution tomorrow?'
'Oh no. No. I couldn't bear it.'
'Of course. I'll see you this evening, Elizabeth.'
I leave her there and spend the next few hours pacing the streets. Anyone seeing me would think me a man trying either to find or to escape from something. Which is true, I wonder?

Attend Justine's execution.

'You are upsetting yourself,' I tell Elizabeth. 'Why not wait for me outside?'
She is reluctant to leave, but after many tears and embraces and backward glances, she finally suffers herself to be separated from her unfortunate maid. When we are alone, I ask Justine frankly about the matter of the confession.
She kisses me tenderly. 'Oh Victor, don't ask me that. I only want you to know that I am not guilty. Tomorrow I can go to meet the hangman without regrets, if that is so.'
'I know you are not guilty, dearest Justine. But why confess?'
She cannot hold my gaze. 'I still have family,' is all she will say. 'A little niece whom I adore. Since I am to be executed either way - '
'What? Has somebody threatened you?'

xoptx Ask about the brooch.

xoptx Say you'll speak to the magistrates.

'How did you come to have the brooch, Justine? Do you know how it got in your pocket?'
She nods, struggling with a spasm of emotion that deprives her of the power of speech. 'That night. I went to sleep in the barn, and - Oh God, Victor, I didn't believe it could be real! I thought it was a nightmare!'
'What? Tell me!'
I'm shaking her. She gives a sob and suddenly is in floods of tears - ugly, unrestrained tears, very different from her melancholy weeping with Elizabeth. 'It's too - I can't, it's too - '
'Too what?'
'Too shameful!'
I can get nothing else out of her. Bidding an emotional farewell, I escape into the teeming streets where life goes on.

Attend Justine's execution.

'My father appointed half the judges. I'll speak to him...'
She shakes her head, suddenly releasing the flood of unrestrained, ugly tears she wouldn't let Elizabeth see. 'It would do no good. Oh, go, Victor. I want you here but it does no good now. Let me be alone to make my peace with God.'
She is distant, her mind already half in the next world. I stay a little while, watching her pray, then slip away quietly to avoid the pain of farewell.

Attend Justine's execution.

She is hanged the next day. Apart from a priest, the executioner and a few court officials, I am the only one present. Justine, blindfolded, may not even have known I came.
After feelings have been worked up by a quick succession of calamities, the dead calmness of inaction and certainty that follows acts to deprive the soul both of hope and fear. Justine is dead, she rests now, and I am alive. The blood flows freely in my veins, but a weight of despair and remorse presses on my heart which nothing can remove.
In the days that follow I am unable to sleep. I wander the streets like an evil spirit. I have committed deeds of mischief beyond description, I know that, yet my heart overflows with kindness and the love of virtue. I set out on my course with benevolent intentions, thirsting for the moment when I should put my knowledge in service to my fellow beings. Now all is blasted. Instead of that serenity of conscience which would allow me to look back upon the past with self-satisfaction, and from thence to gather promise of new hopes, I am seized by remorse and the crushing sense of guilt, which hurries me on through the darkened streets, hounded by the half-dreams that sleeplessness has rendered feverish, submerged in my own hell of intense tortures such as no language can describe.

END OF PART THREE


PART FOUR
The everlasting universe of things
Flows through the mind, and rolls its rapid waves,
Now dark--now glittering--now reflecting gloom--
Now lending splendour, where from secret springs
The source of human thought its tribute brings.

CHAPTER ONE
The River of Ice

This brooding state of mind starts to take its toll upon my health, which has never entirely recovered from the shock it sustained in Paris. I begin to dread company. All sound of joy or even complacency is torture to me. Solitude is my only consolation.
I'm aware that my father and Elizabeth are concerned at my dark moods and long hours spent apart from them. After six years' absence, I should make the effort to give more of myself. On the other hand, the shades of William and Justine are always at my shoulder, silent and accusing, and yet I can give them nothing, make no reparation for the fiend I unleashed to destroy them.
As always, my father is making arrangements for the family to spend the summer at our house at Bellerive. I am in two minds whether to accompany them.

xoptx It will do you good.

xoptx Stay here in Geneva.

I take your advice, but soon realize my error. I thought I desired to be alone, but while the house murmured to my father and Elizabeth talking after dinner, or the gay thoughtless chatter and laughter of the servants, my exile had a kind of noble remoteness. Now, with the house almost empty, I find the silence pressing in on me until it is hard to breathe. The creak of a door, or the scratching of a leaf upon the windowpane, is almost unbearable. I am plunged back to the terrible estrangement of time and sensation that I experienced during my illness.
Sometimes I weep bitterly, praying for peace to revisit my mind only that I might afford my loved ones consolation and happiness. A foolish, futile prayer! I have been the author of unalterable evils, and must live in daily fear that the monster thatwhom I created will perpetrate some new wickedness.

xoptx Surely its revenge is complete.

xoptx Would you want to meet it now?

xoptx Surely his revenge is complete.

xoptx Would you want to meet him now?

I doubt it. I am troubled by an obscure feeling that it has yet to commit his greatest crime. Terrible to me though William's murder was, the child can have meant nothing to the monster. The act must rather have been calculated to strike at me, and therefore I dread to speculate on how it will next contrive to do me harm.
You cannot conceive my abhorrence of this fiend. See my hand trembling? I don't know if that is fear or rage. Both, perhaps. My heart jumps like one of Galvani's frogs and I feel my jaw tighten, whenever I think of its leering red face emerging from the chemical bath that spawned it. Oh, how I wish I could extinguish that life which I so thoughtlessly bestowed.I doubt it. I am troubled by an obscure feeling that he has yet to commit his greatest crime. Terrible to me though William's murder was, the child can have meant nothing to the monster. The act must rather have been calculated to strike at me, and therefore I dread to speculate on how he will next contrive to do me harm.
You cannot conceive my abhorrence of this fiend. See my hand trembling? I don't know if that is fear or rage. Both, perhaps. My heart jumps like one of Galvani's frogs and I feel my jaw tighten, whenever I think of his leering red face emerging from the chemical bath that spawned him. Oh, how I wish I could extinguish that life which I so thoughtlessly bestowed.

You need to get away for a while.

Oh yes. I would make a pilgrimage to the highest peak of the Andes if I could throw the fiend off it. I both fear and desire to see it again, that I might wreak the utmost extent of abhorrence on itshim off it. I both fear and desire to see him again, that I might wreak the utmost extent of abhorrence on his head and avenge the deaths of William and Justine.
Think what that monster has wrought. Our house is the house of mourning. My father's health is deeply shaken. Elizabeth has become sad and desponding; she thinks that even to smile would be sacrilege toward the dead. She is no longer that happy creature who in earlier youth wandered with me on the banks of the lake and talked with ecstasy of our future prospects. The first of those sorrows that are sent to wean us from the Earth has visited her, and its dimming influence has quenched her dearest smiles.

You need a complete change.

I have taken your advice, and you were right. Back in Geneva I felt constantly trapped. It was impossible to take a boat out on the lake after ten o'clock, when the gates are shut, and in summer that is to waste the most sweetly melancholy of the day's hours.
But here in Bellerive, I'm free. Tonight, for instance. Now that my father and Elizabeth have retired for the night, instead of feeling hemmed in by heavy stone walls, I can go out on Lake Neuchâtel under the moonlight. I almost feel like setting the sails and letting the boat run where it will. Out here in the middle of the water, surrounded by the milky immensity of the night sky, I can shrug off for a short while the sense of oppression that dogs me at all other times. Thoughts, feelings and even sense of self fall away. One is left with just the comforting void.

xoptx Are you talking about suicide?

xoptx Is it safe out here alone at night?

Strange you should say that. I am sometimes tempted, when all around is utterly peaceful like this, and I the only unquiet thing that wanders restless in a scene so beautiful and heavenly...

xoptx Yes, go on.

xoptx What's that in the water?

I was just going to say that I'm tempted to plunge into the silent lake, let the waters close over me and so end my calamities forever. Perhaps I would, but I must think of the heroic and suffering Elizabeth, whom I tenderly love, and whose existence has been bound up in mine since she was first brought into our household to be my betrothed. And then there's my poor father. How can I desert him, when he has already lost one son?
And if duty and love did not carry weight enough, logic also adds her thumb to the scales. For would it not be base desertion to leave my family exposed and unprotected to the malice of the fiend whom I let loose among them?

Better head back.

A large fish, perhaps. I heard it splash. Very large. It must have returned to the depths - I see no sign of anything now.
The night is getting cold. And yet the wind has dropped. I had better row back.

And then?

The days creep by. Neither the tenderness of friendship, nor the beauty of Earth and heaven, can redeem my soul from woe. The very accents of love are ineffectual. I feel myself encompassed by a cloud that no beneficial influence can penetrate. The wounded deer dragging its fainting limbs to some untrodden brake, there to gaze upon the arrow which has pierced it, and to die, is but a type of me.

xoptx You should speak to your father.

xoptx Elizabeth's company will ease this grim mood.

xoptx You need a complete change.

'I have observed the alteration in your mood,' says my father, as we walk out with cigars along the loggia of the house. 'You blame yourself for William's death - '
'Father, I am to blame!'
' - because you were not there to protect him. But, Victor, none of us could protect him. Rather than blame yourself, turn your fury on me. It was almost under my eye that the killer struck.'
'You don't understand.'
'Do you think, my son, that I don't suffer also? No one could love a child more than I loved your brother' - tears come into his eyes - 'but is it not a duty to the survivors that we should refrain from augmenting their unhappiness by immoderate grief? You owe that duty to yourself, for excessive sorrow prevents improvement or enjoyment, or even the discharge of daily usefulness, without which no man is fit for society.'
He does not understand that his advice, although good, is totally inapplicable to my case. I should be the first to hide my grief and console my friends if remorse did not mingle its bitterness, and terror its alarm, with my other sensations. I can only answer my father with a look of despair.

xoptx Speak to Elizabeth.

xoptx It's time you got away for a break.

It's time you got away for a break.

Elizabeth listens as I try to describe my morbid state of agitation.
'When I reflect, my dear cousin,' she says, 'on the miserable death of Justine Moritz, I no longer see the world and its works as they before appeared to me. Before, I looked upon the accounts of vice and injustice that I read in books as tales of imaginary evils. At least they were remote, but now misery has come home, and men appear to me as monsters thirsting for each other's blood.'
'But, Elizabeth, I am not talking of abstract disillusionment.'
'Yes, I see that. Everybody believed that poor girl to be guilty and, if she had murdered William, assuredly she would have been the most depraved of human creatures. For the sake of a few jewels, to have slain the son of her benefactor and friend, a child whom she had nursed from its birth, and appeared to love as if it had been her own!'
'Assuredly. But - '
'But Justine was innocent. I know. I feel she was innocent. You are of the same opinion, and that confirms me. Alas! Victor, when falsehood can look so like the truth, who can assure themselves of certain happiness? I feel as if I were walking on the edge of a precipice, towards which thousands are crowding and endeavouring to plunge me into the abyss. William and Justine were assassinated, and the true murderer has escaped justice. He walks about the world free, perhaps even respected. But even if I were condemned to suffer on the scaffold for the same crimes, I would not change places with such a wretch, for surely his conscience must cause him torment.'
'All the torments of hell,' I agree.

xoptx Ask her advice.

xoptx Speak to your father.

xoptx This is doing no good. You need to get away for a while.

Hear that splash just now? A large fish, perhaps. Very large - although sounds are magnified out here on the water. Anyway, it must have returned to the depths, as I see no sign of anything now.
The night is getting cold. I had better row back.

And then?

Not in deed, but in effect, I am the true murderer. Elizabeth reads the anguish in my countenance, but of course could never guess its cause. Taking my hand, she says, 'My dearest friend, you must calm yourself. These events have affected me, God knows how deeply, but I am not so wretched as you are. There is an expression of despair, and sometimes of revenge, in your countenance that makes me tremble. Dear Victor, banish these dark passions. Remember the friends around you, who centre all their hopes in you. Have we lost the power of rendering you happy? Ah! While we love, while we are true to each other, here in this land of peace and beauty, your native country, we may reap every tranquil blessing. What can disturb our peace?'
These words, from her whom I fondly prize before every other gift of fortune, are almost enough to chase away for a brief interval the fears that lurk in my heart. Even as she is speaking I draw her to me, as if in terror, lest at this very moment the fiend is lurking nearby to rob me of her.It is no use. Unless I were to share with her the horror of my profane deed, I can never make her understand. And that horror is mine alone.

You really need a complete change of scenery.

You have hit the nail on the head. Since I was very young, I've found that sometimes I can cope with the brooding thoughts that overwhelm me, but at other times the whirlwind passions of my soul drive me to seek, by bodily exercise and by change of place, some relief from my intolerable sensations.
So, see here, I have a map of the Alpine valleys. I intend to set out and seek the magnificence, the eternity of such scenes, to forget myself and my ephemeral human sorrows.

An excellent idea.

My father is pleased to hear my plans. 'I see how extreme is your grief, Victor. While you remain in these surroundings, from which you were so long absent, such raw feelings can only fester and grow deeper. The tonic you need is an excursion to those places associated only in your mind with boyhood amusements.'
'For my part,' says Elizabeth, looking up from the sampler she is sewing, 'I should enjoy an excursion up into the mountains.'
I had not thought of them accompanying me. Yet now that I see the smiling relief on their faces at my plan to banish the abject dolour in which I have been sunk, how can I refuse them?

xoptx Say that you need to travel alone.

xoptx Let them come if they want to.

They are not happy about that. Elizabeth seems to fear that I will do myself harm if left alone to brood.
When I explain that I need time and solitude to recover myself, though, my father nods, understanding my feelings. 'You need a new homecoming, one unclouded by the dreadful events of your first. Go, then, and come back to us.'

Over the next few days, my wanderings carry me towards the valley of Chamounix. I visited it frequently during my boyhood. The mountains appear at a distance, gradually advancing as I proceed up the ravine formed by the river Arve until they close off the sun.
Let's see, it's now the middle of August. It's been nearly two months since the trial, that miserable epoch from which I date all my woe. Many years have passed since I was last at this spot. I am a wreck, but nothing has changed in these savage and enduring scenes.

xoptx How have your nerves been since setting out?

xoptx This excursion will be good for you.

'You have so often described to me the beauty of the scenery near Chamounix,' says Elizabeth, in high spirits as soon as I acquiesce to her accompanying me.
During the first day we travel in a carriage. The mountains, appearing hazily in the distance in the morning, swiftly grow nearer. The Arve valley, whose course we follow, closes in upon us by degrees, shutting out the sun. Immense mountains and precipices overhang us on every side, and there is the constant echoing roar of waterfalls and the river raging below.
The next day we continue our journey on mules. As we ascend still higher, the valley assumes a more magnificent and astonishing character. Ruined castles hang on the precipices of pine-swathed mountains, and from time to time cottages peep forth from among the trees.

xoptx Is the trip helping to lift your spirits?

xoptx How are you coping with Elizabeth and your father?

The weight upon my spirit is sensibly lightened as I plunge yet deeper into the ravine of Arve. The immense mountains and precipices that overhang on every side, the sound of the river raging among the rocks, and the dashing of the waterfalls around, all speak of a power mighty as Omnipotence. Faced with that, I cease to fear the monster's vengeance. Why should I bend before any being less almighty than that which created and rules the elements, here displayed in their most terrific guise?

Indeed, now you can start to fight back.

The scenery is of singular beauty, of course, but what renders it sublime are the mighty Alps, whose white and shining pyramids and domes tower above all, as belonging to another Earth, the habitations of another race of beings.
We pass the bridge of Pelissier, where the ravine opens before us, and begin to ascend the mountain that overhangs it. Soon after, we enter the valley of Chamounix. The high and snowy mountains are its immediate boundaries. Colossal glaciers spill their scree upon the road.
'What is that rumbling sound?' says Elizabeth, half afraid and half delighted. 'Is it thunder?'
'An avalanche,' says my father, pointing to a plume of fine white smoke beyond the next ridge.
The weather is uncommonly fine, and above all rises the cathedral-white dome of Mont Blanc - the supreme and magnificent Mont Blanc. If mine were a sorrow to be chased away by any fleeting circumstances, this excursion would certainly have the effect intended by my father.

Press on to your destination.

It's not easy. Overwhelmed by the scenery as much as my turbulent emotions, I need a place to retreat and take stock. It is impossible to do this when Elizabeth is exclaiming every few minutes at some fragile butterfly alighted on a roadside bush, or the charm of peasant clothing, or the wood carvings under the eaves of cottages, or the fresh spray thrown up from waterfalls turning to rubies and sapphires in the sun.
Sometimes I allow my mule to lag behind, so that I may indulge in the misery of reflection. At other times I spur the animal on ahead of my companions, that I might forget them, the world, and most of all myself. At one point, getting a good mile further on the track, I alight and throw myself on the grass, weighed down by horror and despair, only to be disturbed all too soon by Elizabeth's trilling laughter and my father calling in clumsy good-humour, 'Are you going to doze away the whole trip, son?'
'Isn't it beautiful?' says Elizabeth, as I get back on my mule. Then a gust of wind comes up the valley and she has to clutch at her bonnet.
'That's from the south,' I say. 'It'll rain tomorrow.'

By then you'll be at your destination.

Perhaps, perhaps. We'll see. As I ascend higher, the valley assumes a more magnificent and astonishing character. Ruined castles hanging on the precipices of pine-clad mountains, the impetuous Arve, and cottages here and there peeping forth from among the trees formed a scene of singular beauty. It is augmented and rendered sublime by the mighty Alps, whose white and shining pyramids and domes tower above all, as belonging to another Earth, the habitations of another race of beings.
I pass the bridge of Pelissier, where the ravine opens before me, and begin to ascend the mountain that overhangs it. Soon after, I enter the valley of Chamounix. This valley is wonderful and sublime, bounded by sheer walls of rock that rise to the high and snowy mountains. Immense glaciers approach the road. I can hear the rumbling thunder of the falling avalanche and see distantly the smoke of its passage. Mont Blanc, the supreme and magnificent Mont Blanc, raises itself from the surrounding peaks, and its tremendous dome commands the valley.

xoptx Stirring sights indeed. A tonic for your soul.

xoptx Press on to Chamounix.

A tingling long-lost sense of pleasure has often come across me during this journey. At every turn in the road, some sight might remind me of days gone by - days associated with the light-hearted gaiety of boyhood. The very winds whisper in soothing accents, and maternal Nature bids me weep no more.
But at other times this kindly influence ceases to act. At those moments, trekking in the cold twilight of the rock faces with no sight of sun, I have found myself fettered again to grief. Fight it as I will, I cannot help indulging in all the misery of reflection. Then I spur on my mule, striving to forget the world, my fears, and, more than all, myself. And once, overcome by desperation, I alight and throw myself on the grass, weighed down by horror and despair.

Pull yourself together. You're almost there.

Yes, here is the village of Chamounix. It's only as I get down from my mulewe get down from our mules that I realize how exhausted I am. I order an early supper in my room, but am too exhausted to eat it. Instead I stand at the window, watching nightfall reveal the pallid lightning that plays above Mont Blanc and listening to the rushing of the Arve, which pursues its noisy way beneath. The same lulling sounds act as a lullaby. When I place my head upon my pillow, sleep takes me swiftly. I feel it as it comes and bless the giver of oblivion.

xoptx Any dreams?

xoptx A new day tomorrow!

A new day tomorrow!

Curiously, as the purpose of this excursion was to have some time to myself, I dreamt that Father and Elizabeth came with me. All the way up the mountain, I either lagged behind or spurred my mule to overtake them. Elizabeth called out to me as I passed, and I hated myself for ignoring her yet still I did.
Then it became a game of hide and seek, as I concealed myself among the rocks and quaked with silent laughter to see them ride by, unaware of my proximity. 'Victor seems happier now that he's gone, don't you think?' I heard Father say.
I rushed out to protest, but my throat was so constricted that, when I called out to them, I could produce only a muffled groan. After some effort, I made myself heard. Elizabeth twisted in her saddle to look at me, just as a shaft of pure sunlight broke across the mountain peak to illuminate her.
'Why, Victor,' she said, laughing gaily, 'you see what happiness you diffuse when you are newly formed. Do not go back to your vat again.'
And when I raised my hands, I saw that the skin was transparent, revealing all the bloody working of muscles and the gleaming white of bone and sinew.

What do you think that means?

Oneiromancy, you mean? I am not superstitious enough to believe one's fortune can be marked out in dreams. Doubtless the preoccupation of my thoughts by the creature, combined with a somewhat morbid cast laid on by my own culpability in his crimes, created this nasty little shadow play to entertain my sleeping hours. Our minds are a vortex of sensation, memory and speculation, my friend, and small wonder those ingredients make a mystery of our dreams.

What will you do today?

I planned a walkI rise early, refreshed, but when I draw back the curtains it is to find the rain pouring down in torrents and thick mists hiding the mountain summits. I utter a bleak curse. If I remain at the inn all today I will drown in the sea of despond, melancholy closing around me with the dreariness of the day and the lack of exercise.
'A day for the hearthrug,' says the innkeeper, stoking the fire as I enter for breakfast. 'I have one or two books about somewhere, if you'd like them, sir.'
I don't say anything, but he brings me his book anyway. 'The Agonies of Young Werther,' I read on the cover. And inside: 'Volume One.'
'Volume Two is much the same but he dies at the end,' supplies the innkeeper helpfully.
I close the book with a snap that makes him blink in surprise. 'You have decided me. Have a lunch basket made up and my mule brought to the door.'
'On such a foul day, sir? I cannot advise it.'

xoptx He's right.

xoptx A little rain won't hurt you.

I rise early, refreshed, but when I draw back the curtains it is to find the rain pouring down in torrents and thick mists hiding the mountain summits. The sight depresses me, for I had hoped the rain would hold off until after my morning walk.
I cannot spend the day cooped up at the inn with Father and Elizabeth. I bolt a hasty breakfast, telling the innkeeper to have a luncheon basket made up for me, and am on my mule before I hear them stirring.
'Nasty weather, sir,' says the innkeeper from the porch, 'but I expect you won't go far.'
'On the contrary, I intend to cross the glacier,' I tell him, kicking my mule into action before Elizabeth can rush down and reproach me for abandoning her.
I remember the effect that the view of this tremendous and ever-moving glacier produced upon my mind when I first saw it. It then filled me with a sublime ecstasy that gave wings to the soul, and allowed it to soar from the obscure world to light and joy. The sight of the awful and majestic in nature has always had the effect of helping me to forget the passing cares of life. Thank heaven the rain gives me the excuse of going up there alone. The presence of another would destroy the solitary grandeur of the scene.

Of course it would.

Quite. I have ever inured myself to harsh weather. I was set upon a walk today, so walk I shall have.
'Well, I would not venture far if I were you, sir,' says the innkeeper, still brandishing his wretched book.
'I shall only go as far as the summit of Montanvert.'
I remember the effect that the view of this tremendous and ever-moving glacier produced upon my mind when I first saw it. It then filled me with a sublime ecstasy that gave wings to the soul, and allowed it to soar from the obscure world to light and joy. The sight of the awful and majestic in nature has always had the effect of helping me to forget the passing cares of life.
The innkeeper watches glumly from the porch as I wade out into the rain. 'Shall I find you a guide?' he says, expecting the negative.
'I'll go alone. I am well acquainted with the path. And anyway, the presence of another would destroy the solitary grandeur of the scene.'

Of course it would.

Nonsense. If I wanted to coddle myself like a hibernating toad, I should have stayed at home.
I have ever inured myself to harsh weather. I was set upon a walk today, so walk I shall have.
'Well, I would not venture far if I were you, sir,' says the innkeeper, still brandishing his wretched book.
'I shall only go as far as the summit of Montanvert.'
I remember the effect that the view of this tremendous and ever-moving glacier produced upon my mind when I first saw it. It then filled me with a sublime ecstasy that gave wings to the soul, and allowed it to soar from the obscure world to light and joy. The sight of the awful and majestic in nature has always had the effect of helping me to forget the passing cares of life.
The innkeeper watches glumly from the porch as I wade out into the rain. 'Shall I find you a guide?' he says, expecting the negative.
'I'll go alone. I am well acquainted with the path. And anyway, the presence of another would destroy the solitary grandeur of the scene.'

Of course it would.

The ascent is precipitous, but the path is cut into continual short windings, which enable one to surmount the perpendicularity of the mountain. It is a terrifically desolate scene. Everywhere you can see traces of the winter avalanche, where trees lie broken on the ground.
The path, as I ascend higher, is intersected by ravines of snow, down which stones continually roll from above. One of them is particularly dangerous, as the slightest sound - even raising your voice - risks bringing destruction upon the head of the speaker.
The pines here are not tall or luxuriant, but they are sombre and add an air of severity to the scene. Pausing after an hour's ascent, I look down on the valley. Vast mists are rising from the rivers, curling in thick wreaths around the opposite mountains, whose summits are buried in the uniform clouds. It is a melancholy vista, not helped by the sheets of rain lacing down from an iron-grey sky.

xoptx Nonetheless, an impressive view.

xoptx So your mood is no more cheerful?

You ask me that? Alas! Why does man boast of sensibilities superior to those apparent in the animals? It only renders us more needful. If our impulses were confined to hunger, thirst, and desire, we might be nearly free. But we are moved by every wind that blows and chance word spoken. A dream has power to poison sleep, and one wandering thought pollutes the day.

You said yourself that dreams signify nothing.

Let's see if we can get above the cloud cover.

Indeed. It is nearly noon when I arrive at the top of the ascent, where there is a rock overlooking the sea of ice. A mist covers both that and the surrounding mountains, but by the time I've finished lunch a breeze has blown up, dissipating the mist so that it is safe to descend onto the glacier.
The surface is very uneven, rising like the waves of a troubled sea, descending low, and interspersed by rifts that sink deep. The field of ice is about three miles across but it takes me nearly two hours to cross it. The opposite mountain is a bare perpendicular rock. From this side, Montanvert is exactly opposite, and above it rises, in awful majesty, the immense bulk of Mont Blanc.
I remain in a recess of the rock, gazing on this wonderful and stupendous scene. The vast river of ice winds among the mountains, whose aerial summits loom overhead. Their icy and glittering peaks shine in the sunlight over the clouds. My heart was sorrowful before, as you know, but now swells with something like joy must feel, if I can remember joy.
Suffused in the sunlight that plays above the icy breath of the glacier, I cannot resist the urge to raise my voice and shout to the heavens: 'Wandering spirits, if indeed you wander, and do not rest in your graves, allow me this faint happiness, or take me, as your companion, away from the joys of life.'

There is a figure on the glacier.

I see him. A man coming towards me, seemingly at superhuman speed. He leaps over crevices in the ice where I walked with greatest caution. As he comes closer, I am able to measure his stature compared to the boulders of ice. He is a giant. He... it...
Oh, I nearly passed out just then. Only the cold gale along the valley revived me. You know what it is? The thing I created. The monster. It has found me.Oh, I nearly passed out just then. Only the cold gale along the valley revived me. You know who it is? The thing I created. The monster. He has found me.

xoptx It has come to kill you! Flee while you can.

xoptx The fiend! It dares show itself?

xoptx You must be curious to see it after so long?

xoptx He means to kill you! Flee while you can.

xoptx The fiend! He dares show himself?

xoptx You must be curious to see him after so long?

How can I escape it? Oh, I don't only mean the superior strength and agility with which it is endowed, far greater than that which God imparted to Man. But even if somehow I could elude it now, upon the river of ice, the ghostly visage of my guilt, of which it is but the outward clothing, will hang before me until I have died or somehow made amends.How can I escape him? Oh, I don't only mean the superior strength and agility with which he is endowed, far greater than that which God imparted to Man. But even if somehow I could elude him now, upon the river of ice, the ghostly visage of my guilt, of which he is but the outward clothing, will hang before me until I have died or somehow made amends.

Then wait to speak to it.

Then wait to speak to him.

It dares any perfidy! See, itHe dares any perfidy! See, he even waves to me as if in greeting. Monstrous thing! A sight tremendous and abhorred! I am trembling. I feel weak- my heart racing - not just with horror, but rage too.

xoptx It may have no conception of its crimes.

xoptx He may have no conception of his crimes.

xoptx Victor, you tremble from other emotions than those.

Yes, I'm curious. Curious to see its skull broken on these rocks! Curious to find out how swiftly its artificial blood congeals at this altitude! What, did you expect scientific detachment? A civilized conversation with a monster from a vat of noxious fluid? 'My dear fiend, how has life treated you since you butchered my baby brother and allowed my erstwhile lover to perish for the crime?'
No, I have no curiosity. My loathing for itYes, I'm curious. Curious to see his skull broken on these rocks! Curious to find out how swiftly his artificial blood congeals at this altitude! What, did you expect scientific detachment? A civilized conversation with a monster from a vat of noxious fluid? 'My dear fiend, how has life treated you since you butchered my baby brother and allowed my erstwhile lover to perish for the crime?'
No, I have no curiosity. My loathing for him is equal to my loathing for myself. A pair of monsters, we. What will happen here, without witnesses, in the strangest meeting ever to take place under heaven?Hmm, I suppose so. I see it has found clothing. Strange, that almost makes it more monstrous, like seeing a cadaver that has been peeled, grape-like, to instruct the anatomy students, then taken and dressed for a walk in the mountains. I wonder that it found clothes big enough for its frame - though a cloak and trousers, only. Its stamina makes it all but indifferent to the cold, I suppose.
And - oh, this is fascinating! Emotion. See, complex emotion! It's hard to read a face like that, but mark the eyes. I read anguish, disdain... malignity, obviously, but a kind of wonder too. Even trepidation ..? It may be as shaken by its feelings at this moment as I.Hmm, I suppose so. I see he has found clothing. Strange, that almost makes him more monstrous, like seeing a cadaver that has been peeled, grape-like, to instruct the anatomy students, then taken and dressed for a walk in the mountains. I wonder that he found clothes big enough for his frame - though a cloak and trousers, only. His stamina makes him all but indifferent to the cold, I suppose.
And - oh, this is fascinating! Emotion. See, complex emotion! It's hard to read a face like that, but mark the eyes. I read anguish, disdain... malignity, obviously, but a kind of wonder too. Even trepidation ..? He may be as shaken by his feelings at this moment as I.

Soon you'll know. It is here.

Soon you'll know. He is here.

Perhaps I could believe that if it had murdered at random, but it chose my poor little brother as its victim. That speaks of a fully developed sense of good and evil - and a sneering disregard for the former. Its countenance is of an unearthly ugliness that renders it almost too horrible to look upon. Yet how much more horrible is the barbed black soul within.
Perhaps I could believe that if he had murdered at random, but he chose my poor little brother as his victim. That speaks of a fully developed sense of good and evil - and a sneering disregard for the former. His countenance is of an unearthly ugliness that renders him almost too horrible to look upon. Yet how much more horrible is the barbed black soul within.
True. Here approaching is the strange fruit of my years of labour - of my genius. I created him. Whatever wickedness he has wrought, I cannot avoid some of the sentiment with which a father must look upon his son.
We face each other over a few metres of rock and shining grey ice. Its breath steams in the air. As we both stare transfixed, struggling to find words, I am seized by the disturbing fancy that I am beholding myself in a mirror that somehow looks beneath the skin to the exposed nerves of the man within. Does it see itself reflected in me? Perhaps.We face each other over a few metres of rock and shining grey ice. His breath steams in the air. As we both stare transfixed, struggling to find words, I am seized by the disturbing fancy that I am beholding myself in a mirror that somehow looks beneath the skin to the exposed nerves of the man within. Does he see himself reflected in me? Perhaps.

xoptx Wait for him to speak.

xoptx Wait for it to speak.

xoptx Berate this vile monster!

xoptx Reason with him.

xoptx Try reasoning with it.

In contrast to its fearsome visage, its voice is resonant, cultured - even musical. From somewhere it has acquired the accent of a well-bred Frenchman. The effect is to make this meeting on the blasted ice-desert of the glacier all the more dreamlike.
'How I have rehearsed this moment in my mind,' itIn contrast to his fearsome visage, his voice is resonant, cultured - even musical. From somewhere he has acquired the accent of a well-bred Frenchman. The effect is to make this meeting on the blasted ice-desert of the glacier all the more dreamlike.
'How I have rehearsed this moment in my mind,' he begins. 'A thousand times I have considered my words to thee, my creator. Sometimes I plead with thee to rise me from bottomless perdition to regain that blissful seat, and at those times no tears have e'er been shed more piteously. But there are also times when I rage and curse thy name, and dream hotly of pulling thee down, that misery may join us in equal ruin.'

xoptx 'My God, a monster that quotes Milton...'

xoptx 'Evidently you decided on the latter.'

'Devil, do you dare approach me? And do not you fear the fierce vengeance of my arm wreaked on your miserable head? Begone, vile insect! Or rather, stay, that I may trample you to dust! Oh, that I could, with the extinction of your miserable existence, restore those victims whom you have so diabolically murdered!'
ItsHis rictus widens crookedly - perhaps an attempt at a wry smile, though the effect is more like an ape's angry grimace.
'You purpose to kill me. How dare you sport thus with life? Do your duty towards me, and I will do mine towards you and the rest of mankind. If you will comply with my conditions, I will leave them and you at peace. But if you refuse, I will glut the maw of death, until it be satiated with the blood of your remaining friends.'

'You speak of my duty to you?'

'Adom, it'It is you. I had thought, after all this time, you might have found a home for yourself far from here - '
'Thou hadst hoped I was dead.'
In contrast to its fearsome visage, its voice is resonant, cultured - even musical. From somewhere ithis fearsome visage, his voice is resonant, cultured - even musical. From somewhere he has acquired the accent of a well-bred Frenchman. The effect is to make this meeting all the more dreamlike.
'When I encountered you in the storm that night, yes. For then I knew you had returned to visit tragedy upon my house.'
'In this dark world and wide, should I bury the talents you gave me in a hole? No, I came back to present them to my maker. I return the gift you gave me a hundredfold, as a good son should.'
'You return the gift of life with murder?'
'Life? You say you gave me life? What is the life of an outcast? How can I live, that have no friend, no companion, not even a fellow creature? Do your duty towards me, and I will leave you at peace. But if you refuse, I will glut the maw of death, until it be satiated with the blood of your remaining friends.'

'You speak of my duty to you?'

'Why art thou surprised? Is it as though an ape had jumped up before you to recite the Hippocratic Oath? Which, incidentally, includes a stipulation not to harm or bring about abortion, though assuredly abortion would have been the fate of this, thy poor son, if thou hadst been more thorough.'
'You are no more my son than is the vomit I would spew forth after eating food that is spoiled.'
I show him the acid burns still visible on my hands. 'A moment of panic. Will you condemn me for a mistake?'
'I will ask you the same, when I tell you of your brother's death.'
At these words, my heart hardens. 'I merely attempted to destroy the consequence of a botched experiment. Don't compare that to the cold-blooded atrocity you perpetrated on a helpless child.'
'Hippocrates. Milton. My astonishment is simply that you have achieved so much in so short a time.'
'I had the fortune of good teachers, coupled with the best of all incentives: necessity. Paradise Lost fell into my hands by luck and I, seeing all then as with a child's accepting eyes, read it as a true history. And, also as a child might think, it seemed to be my own history. God at war with His creations! I trembled with emotions for which I had no name. I saw that I was like Adam in having no link to any other being in existence. But his state was far different from mine in every other respect. He had come forth into the world a perfect creature, happy and prosperous, guarded by the especial care of his Creator. He was allowed to converse with, and acquire wisdom from, beings of a superior nature. But not I! I was wretched, helpless, and alone. Many times I considered Satan as the fitter emblem of my condition. For often, like him, when I viewed the bliss of others, the bitter gall of envy rose within me.'

xoptx 'Such grandiosity and self-pity in a child-killer!'

xoptx 'I never expected your brain to develop to such a level.'

'I expected this reception. All men hate the wretched. How, then, must I be hated, who am miserable beyond all living things? Yet you, my creator, detest and spurn me, thy creature, to whom thou art bound by ties only dissoluble by the annihilation of one of us.
'Abhorred monster! Fiend that thou art! The tortures of hell are too mild a vengeance for thy crimes. Wretched devil, you reproach me with your creation. Come on, then, that I may extinguish the spark which I so negligently bestowed.'

Attack it.

Attack him.

'That I know to be true, because of other reading matter I found. In the pocket of the gown I took from you was a notebook. In my early days I used some pages to make a fire, but later I learnt to read, and after that I studied the book with diligence. It was your journal of the four months that preceded my creation. You minutely described in these papers every step you took in the progress of your work. This history was mingled with accounts of domestic occurrences.'
'I know the book.'
'Yes. Here it is. I wouldn't throw away my Bible, my one inheritance from my dear creator. In here you put everything about my accursed origin, every detail of that series of disgusting circumstances which produced it. The minutest description of my person is given, in language which paints your own horrors and rendered mine indelible. How do you think I felt to read it? How sickened? "The body is loathsome. I am no Michelangelo. It will suffice for anatomical study." Or here: "If it has no value as an object of academic interest, I could certainly sell it to a travelling fair to exhibit in the freak show …" Cursed creator! Why did you form a monster so hideous that even you turned from me in disgust? God, in pity, made man beautiful and alluring, after his own image, but my form is a filthy perversion of yours, more horrid from its very resemblance. Satan had his companions, fellow-devils, to admire and encourage him. I am solitary and detested.'

xoptx The creature has a legitimate grievance.

xoptx It killed William. Destroy it.

xoptx Tell it to leave you alone.

xoptx He killed William. Destroy him.

xoptx Tell him to leave you alone.

My own heartbeat is like the clamour of an avalanche in my ears, and red mist clouds my vision. I am consumed with rage. I spring on it, impelled by all the feelings which can arm one being against the existence of another.
Ithim, impelled by all the feelings which can arm one being against the existence of another.
He easily eludes me, holding me away with an arm firm as an oak branch, saying, 'Be calm! I entreat you to hear me out before you give vent to your hatred on my devoted head. Have I not suffered enough that you seek to increase my misery? Life, although it may only be an accumulation of anguish, is dear to me, and I will defend it. Remember, thou hast made me more powerful than thyself. My height is superior to thine. My joints more supple. But I will not be tempted to set myself in opposition to thee. I am thy creature, and I will be even mild and docile to my natural lord and king, if thou wilt also perform thy part, which thou owest me.'

'All I owe the world is your destruction.'

'Oh, Frankenstein, be not equitable to every other, and trample upon me alone, to whom thy justice is most due - aye, and not only justice but thy clemency and thy affection too. Remember that I am thy creature. I ought to be thy Adam, but I am rather the fallen angel, whom thou drivest from joy for no misdeed. Everywhere I see bliss, from which I alone am irrevocably excluded. I was benevolent and good. It was misery made me a fiend. Make me happy, and I shall again be virtuous.'

xoptx It can't hurt to hear the creature out.

xoptx Don't permit your brother's killer to dictate terms.

'How can I sway thee?' it asks. 'Will no entreaties cause thee to turn a favourable eye upon thy creature, who implores thy goodness and compassion?'
'Murdering an innocent child is hardly the way to begin your entreaties, monster.'
'Believe me, Frankenstein: I was benevolent. My soul glowed with love and humanity. But look - ' It sweeps its'How can I sway thee?' he asks. 'Will no entreaties cause thee to turn a favourable eye upon thy creature, who implores thy goodness and compassion?'
'Murdering an innocent child is hardly the way to begin your entreaties, monster.'
'Believe me, Frankenstein: I was benevolent. My soul glowed with love and humanity. But look - ' He sweeps his arm to take in the immemorial mountains that watch us, and the miles of ice and air. 'In all this world I am alone. Miserably alone! If you, who created me, view your creature with abhorrence, what hope have I from other men? They spurn and hate me. So these mountains and dreary glaciers are my refuge. The caves of ice, which I alone do not fear, are a dwelling to me, and the only one which man does not grudge. These bleak skies cast the bitter cold upon my head, but they are kinder to me than your fellow beings. When I reveal myself, men invariably try to destroy me. Shall I not then hate them who abominate me? I will keep no terms with my enemies. I am wretched, and all shall share my wretchedness.'

xoptx Ask what it wants from you.

xoptx Tell it to begone.

xoptx Ask what he wants from you.

xoptx Tell him to begone.

'Go! I will not hear you. There can be no community between you and me. We are enemies. Go, or let us try our strength in a fight in which one must die.'
'How can I move thee? Will no entreaties cause thee to turn a favourable eye upon thy creature, who implores thy goodness and compassion? Believe me, Frankenstein, I was benevolent. My soul glowed with love and humanity. But am I not alone, miserably alone? You, my creator, abhor me. What hope can I gather from your fellow creatures, who owe me nothing? They spurn and hate me. The mountains and dreary glaciers are my refuge. I have wandered here many days. The caves of ice, which I alone do not fear, are a dwelling to me, and the only one which man does not grudge. These bleak skies I hail, for they are kinder to me than your fellow beings. If the multitude of mankind knew of my existence, they would do as you do, and arm themselves for my destruction. Shall I not then hate them who abhor me? I will keep no terms with my enemies. I am miserable, and they shall share my wretchedness.'

xoptx Ask what it wants from you.

xoptx Ask what he wants from you.

xoptx There's no reasoning with this monster.

'It is in your power to recompense me. You could deliver mankind from an evil that, if you refuse me, will be so great that not only you and your family, but thousands of others, shall be swallowed up in the whirlwinds of its rage. Let your compassion be moved, and do not disdain me. Listen to my tale. When you have heard that, abandon or commiserate me as you judge I deserve. But hear me. The guilty, whatever their deeds, are allowed to speak in their own defence before they are condemned. You accuse me of murder and you would, with a satisfied conscience, destroy your own creature. Oh, praise the eternal justice of man! Yet I ask you not to spare me. Come with me, Frankenstein. Only listen to my story - and then, if you can and if you will, destroy the work of your hands.'

'Very well. Speak, I'll listen.'

'Why, monster, do you remind me of circumstances which, I shudder to reflect, were all of my doing? Cursed be the day, abhorred devil, in which you first saw light! Cursed - although I curse myself - be the hands that formed you. You have made me desolate beyond expression. You have left me no power to consider whether I am just to you or not. Begone! Relieve me of the sight of your detested form.'
'Thus I relieve thee, my creator,' it says, and places itshe says, and places his hands before my eyes. 'Thus I take from thee a sight which you abhor. Still thou canst listen to me, and grant me thy compassion. By the virtues that I once possessed, I demand this from you. Hear my tale. It is long and strange, and the temperature of this place is not fitting to your fine sensations. Come to my cave up on the mountain. The sun is yet high in the heavens. Before it descends to hide itself behind yon snowy precipices, and illuminate another world, you will have heard my story, and can decide. On you it rests whether I quit forever the neighbourhood of man, and lead a harmless life, or become the scourge of your fellow creatures, and the author of your own speedy ruin.'

Listen to its tale.

Listen to his tale.

It turns back across the ice. What can I do? I follow. As we proceed, I weigh its various arguments and determine at least to listen to its tale. I am partly urged by curiosity, partly by compassion. I have hitherto supposed it to be the murderer of my brother, and I need a confirmation or denial of this. For the first time, also, I feel what the duties of a creator towards his creature should be, and that I ought to render it happy before I complain of its wickedness.
These motives urge me to comply with its demand. We cross the ice, therefore. The air is cold and it is raining again. It leads me to a promontory of rock jutting out from the opposite cliff. It is already splintered by the inexorable force of ice, and in another decade or two will be swept to add to the accumulation of moraine. Up in the side of this buttress is a cave, to which it leads me by way of boulders and ledges in the rock.
It turns at the top of the ascent with an air of exultation. 'Come, creator. I welcome you to my abode!'
The long streamer of its breath hangs beside it. I ignore the hand it holds out to me.
'My heart is heavy,' I say. 'But I said I would hear you out, odious one. Lead on.'
As it stoops to enter, I see it step sharply to the left of a fissure that runs almost to the cave mouth.He turns back across the ice. What can I do? I follow. As we proceed, I weigh his various arguments and determine at least to listen to his tale. I am partly urged by curiosity, partly by compassion. I have hitherto supposed him to be the murderer of my brother, and I need a confirmation or denial of this. For the first time, also, I feel what the duties of a creator towards his creature should be, and that I ought to render him happy before I complain of his wickedness.
These motives urge me to comply with his demand. We cross the ice, therefore. The air is cold and it is raining again. He leads me to a promontory of rock jutting out from the opposite cliff. It is already splintered by the inexorable force of ice, and in another decade or two will be swept to add to the accumulation of moraine. Up in the side of this buttress is a cave, to which he leads me by way of boulders and ledges in the rock.
He turns at the top of the ascent with an air of exultation. 'Come, creator. I welcome you to my abode!'
The long streamer of his breath hangs beside him. I ignore the hand he holds out to me.
'My heart is heavy,' I say. 'But I said I would hear you out. Lead on.'
As he stoops to enter, I see him step sharply to the left of a fissure that runs almost to the cave mouth.

xoptx Do likewise.

xoptx Step to the right instead.

The floor of the cave is split in two, leaving a rift down into the rock and fathomless ice beneath us. In the guttering light of a small fire, it is impossible to make out anything down there. It is like a crack in the world, infinitely black and without end. I am conscious of the powerful bulk of my monstrous creation just a few feet from me. If ithe chose to throw me down, my body would never be found.
AdomThe monster drops a few pine branches onto the fire, which flares up, giving out more noise and light than warmth. I find a cleft in the rock where I am able to perch - not comfortably - while it recounts itshe recounts his experiences. There, surrounded by the glistening ice of the walls, it is as though some primordial forerunner of man had emerged in a thaw to speak of things from the dark predawn of time.

xoptx Hear the creature out.

xoptx Never mind its story - what about William?

xoptx Never mind his story - what about William?

The floor of the cave is split in two, leaving a rift that cuts right down into the rock and ice. It is like a crack in the world, infinitely black and without end.
Next to where I stand is a small fire, barely alight. AdomThe monster is on the other side of the fissure. As I add some twigs from a pile, the flames leap up, painting glistening yellow streaks over the walls and darkening the dreary light outside. We could be in a bubble of rock in the very centre of the Earth.
It gestures for me to sit beside the fire, while it retreats against the far wall of the cave so that it is almost invisible in the shadows. That is to make it easier for me to hear itsHe gestures for me to sit beside the fire, while he retreats against the far wall of the cave so that he is almost invisible in the shadows. That is to make it easier for me to hear his story, of course. Instead of a laboratory specimen made animate, a fiend incarnate in monstrous flesh, I could be listening to the words of any of my fellow men.

xoptx Hear the creature out.

xoptx Never mind its story - what about William?

xoptx Never mind his story - what about William?

The monster's tale is extraordinary and moving. Rejected by mankind, it strove for love but was met only with violence and distrust. In its account it is impossible not to see the heart-rending tragedy of innocence disillusioned, embittered, and finally made corrupt.
Its words have a strange effect upon me. I pity it, and even, at times, wish to console it; but when I look upon it, when I see the filthy mass that moves and talks beside me, my heart is sickened, and my feelings change to horror and hatred.
The monster's tale is extraordinary and moving. Rejected by mankind, he strove for love but was met only with violence and distrust. In his account it is impossible not to see the heart-rending tragedy of innocence disillusioned, embittered, and finally made corrupt.
His words have a strange effect upon me. I pity him, and even, at times, wish to console him; but when I look upon him, when I see the filthy mass that moves and talks beside me, my heart is sickened, and my feelings change to horror and hatred.
I make an effort to stifle these sensations. For, alongside all the things it has experienced, and deeds ithe has experienced, and deeds he has committed, remains the awareness that the germ of these horrors grew out of my own callowness and overweening hubris.Somehow, despite its superior strength, I must find a way to destroy it. Otherwise the long scroll of its future crimes will all have to be signed with the name Frankenstein.Somehow, despite his superior strength, I must find a way to destroy him. Otherwise the long scroll of his future crimes will all have to be signed with the name Frankenstein.

xoptx 'Tell me how my brother died.'

xoptx 'Tell me how my brother died.'

xoptx 'Tell me how my brother died.'

xoptx 'Tell me how my brother died.'

xoptx 'What have you come to me for?'

'The notebook I found'On the night of my birth I stole a gown from you. Much later, in the pocket, I found your notebook. It told me that you lived in Geneva. I was eventually able to find my way there.'
'The notebook. Of course.' Unwittingly, I placed under itshis hand the weapon with which to bring ruin on me. 'And so you sought out my family.'
'It was mere chance that brought William close to my hiding place - except, of course, that I chose that hillside in Plainpalais because I knew of your fondness for walks there.'
'And what happened? I must know everything, though it break my heart a second time.'
The monster hangs its head, though its eyes, having no lids, continue to gleam at me in the yellow firelight. 'You must know that I am filled with remorse that I couldn't save him.'

his head, though his eyes, having no lids, continue to gleam at me in the yellow firelight. 'You must know that I am filled with remorse that I couldn't save him.'

xoptx 'Liar!'

xoptx 'Then it wasn't murder?'

'Then it wasn't murder?'

'I found a hiding place among the fields around the city. I was oppressed by fatigue and hunger, and far too unhappy to enjoy the gentle breezes of evening, or the prospect of the sun setting behind the stupendous mountains of Jura. As I reflected on how best to approach you, a child came running into the recess I had chosen and, as I gazed on him, an idea seized me. This little creature is unprejudiced, I thought. He has lived too short a time to have imbibed a horror of deformity. If I seize him and raise him as my companion, I need not be alone. That was all I wanted.'

xoptx Tell it to go on.

xoptx Tell him to go on.

xoptx Don't listen to any more. It's too painful.

xoptx This lying monster feels no remorse.

'The notebook I found'On the night of my creation I took a gown from you. Much later, in the pocket, I found your notebook. It told me that you lived in Geneva. I made my way there - it took months - and found a hiding place among the fields around the city. I was oppressed by fatigue and hunger, and far too unhappy to enjoy the gentle breezes of evening, or the prospect of the sun setting behind the stupendous mountains of Jura. Then, as I reflected on how best to approach you, a child came running into the recess I had chosen and, as I gazed on him, an idea seized me. This little creature is unprejudiced, I thought. He has lived too short a time to have imbibed a horror of deformity. If I seize him and raise him as my companion, I need not be alone. Frankenstein, I had no intention of harming him.'

xoptx 'You came across William by chance? Absurd.'

xoptx Bid it go on.

xoptx He expects you to believe he came across William by chance?

xoptx Bid him go on.

'I seized the boy as he passed and drew him towards me. As soon as he beheld my form, he placed his hands before his eyes and uttered a shrill scream. I drew his hand forcibly from his face, and said, "Child, I do not intend to hurt you. Listen to me."
'He struggled violently. He called me a monster, an ogre. He thought I wanted to eat him alive. "Let me go," he said, "or I will tell my papa." I told him he would never see his father again. "Hideous monster!" he screamed. "Let me go! My papa is a syndic. He is Monsieur Frankenstein. He'll have you hunted down and killed like a dog. You dare not keep me."
' "Frankenstein! You belong then to him on whom I have sworn eternal revenge. You shall be my first victim."
'The child still struggled. I grasped his throat to silence him, and in a moment he lay dead at my feet.'

xoptx Doesn't this fiend feel the slightest remorse?

xoptx 'And the brooch that was used to frame Justine?'

xoptx Enough of this. What does it want from you?

xoptx Enough of this. What does he want from you?

'Spare me. I cannot bear to hear more.'
'And you a scientist? I thought truth was your one desire.'
'I have to live with the truth. It is your malevolent glee that makes my grief unendurable.'
'It gives me no pleasure to do as I have, Frankenstein. I am like a sleepwalker, watching with one part of my mind while another part takes action.'

xoptx Ask what it wants of you.

xoptx Ask what he wants of you.

xoptx What happened to the brooch that was stolen?

'Not entirely chance. The notebook told me the places you like to frequent. There are even sketches - see? That's how I was able to recognize them. My plan was to wait around your familiar haunts until I should see you.'
'I didn't go back to Geneva for six years! Why should you think I would now?'
It seems genuinely surprised. 'You did not see your family in so long? To have a family, I would cut off these hands you gave me! How could I imagine your own affections are so casual?'
'Don't try to speculate on the emotions of a real human being, fiend. You speak of cutting off your hands - which I found on a dissecting tray, by the way. In killing William, you cut out my very heart.'He seems genuinely surprised. 'You did not see your family in so long? To have a family, I would cut off these hands you gave me! How could I imagine your own affections are so casual?'
'Don't try to speculate on the emotions of a real human being. You speak of cutting off your hands. In killing William, you cut out my very heart.'

xoptx Don't reopen old wounds. Find out what it wants.

xoptx Get it to tell you the rest of the story.

xoptx Those are old wounds. Find out what he wants.

xoptx Get him to tell you the rest of the story.

'I seized the boy as he passed and drew him towards me. As soon as he beheld my form, he placed his hands before his eyes and uttered a shrill scream. I drew his hand forcibly from his face, and said, "Child, I do not intend to hurt you. Listen to me."
'He struggled violently. He called me a monster, an ogre. He thought I wanted to eat him alive. "Let me go," he said, "or I will tell my papa." I told him he would never see his father again. "Hideous monster!" he screamed. "Let me go! My papa is a syndic. He is Monsieur Frankenstein. He'll have you hunted down and killed like a dog. You dare not keep me."
' "Frankenstein! You belong then to he who created me. What curious fate has brought us together."
'He still struggled, and continued to say terrible things that made me despair of ever earning his friendship. I was about to let him go, but I heard others approaching and I panicked. Grasping his throat to silence him, I waited till they had gone by. It was only then that I realized I had killed the child.'

xoptx Ask what it wants from you.

xoptx Ask what he wants from you.

xoptx What happened to the brooch that was stolen?

'Unspeakable fiend, I know what you did. After murdering William, you found Justine sleeping in a barn, worn out by her frantic search for the boy, and you forced yourself upon her. I shudder to think of her terror, woken from exhausted sleep to find a nightmarish charnel-house thing pawing at her. No wonder she was seen wandering half out of her wits the next day. Death must have been a blessing, after her ravishment at your unclean hands.'
'No! That's not true.'
'I only wanted to go,' it mutters with unholy relish, 'where my master had gone.'
'I only wanted to go,' he mutters with unholy relish, 'where my master had gone.'
'And you left the brooch with her, knowing it would implicate her in your foul deed. Oh, my creature, you have learned how to behave like men - but only the basest of men. I pity you, that you have not found in your heart those ennobling sentiments that raise us above the rutting beasts.'

The fiend wants something from you. But what?

'The notebook I found'On the night of my creation I took a gown from you. Much later, in the pocket, I found your notebook. It told me that you lived in Geneva. I made my way there - it took months - and found a hiding place among the hills around the city. I was oppressed by fatigue and hunger, and too downcast to enjoy the sunset and the gentle breezes of evening. Then, as I reflected on how best to approach you, I saw a child running by with all the sportiveness of infancy. He was looking back, laughing happily, and seemed to be casting about for a place to hide, when he ran into the arms of one who meant him harm.'
'You.'
'Not me. This one.'
ItHe holds out my notebook. It is a sketch I made of Justine when we were both sixteen.
'She wanted an item of jewellery he had in his pocket. I heard him threaten to tell. She caught him by the neck and then, hearing people approach, she twisted around in alarm. The boy fell limp. I think it was an accident, Frankenstein.'

xoptx You know why it is telling that lie.

xoptx You know why he is telling that lie.

xoptx Ask why it sought you out.

xoptx Ask why he sought you out.

The monster's expression is unreadable, but I cannot mistake itsThe monster's expression is unreadable, but I cannot mistake his gloating tone. 'Remorse? Hah! My heart swelled with exultation as I gazed on his lifeless form. I too, you see, can create desolation. That act altered your world forever. You are not impregnable, Frankenstein. I have made you feel despair and a thousand other torments.'
'Feel your pulse, monster. I used whatever parts I could come by to make you. That's the beating of a pig's heart. Not human, understand?'
'It's an honest heart, at any rate.'
'Honest?'
'Unlike men, who hide their hatred under the floorboards, and conceal malice in a smile, my own dealings are entirely free of pretence. As you will discover when you ask why I have come.'

xoptx 'And why have you?'

xoptx 'You took a brooch from the body.'

'Oh God, how can a being of so twisted nature walk upon this Earth?'
'Why ask God? Frankenstein created me.'
'I didn't create your nature.'
'Don't be bashful. That's your work too. My nature was decided when you chose to abandon me.'
'I was ill!'
'What convenient sickness. Necrosis of the conscience? An inflammation of self-interest? While you wallowed in your coddled misery, nerves scratched raw by the abrupt encounter between your odious privilege and harsh reality, where was your poor creature? In hell, Frankenstein; in hell. Now I have returned, and since you would not teach me to be good, I have learned another way.'

xoptx 'What do you want from me?'

xoptx 'There was a brooch on the body.'

'I hid myself among the hills around the city. I was oppressed by fatigue and hunger, and too downcast to enjoy the sunset and the gentle breezes of evening. Then, as I reflected on the best way to approach you, I saw a child running by with all the sportiveness of infancy. He was looking back, laughing happily, and seemed to be casting about for a place to hide, when he ran into the arms of one who meant him harm.'
'You.'
'Not me. This one.'
He holds out my notebook. It is a sketch I made of Justine when we were both sixteen.
'She wanted an item of jewellery he had in his pocket. I heard him threaten to tell. She caught him by the neck and then, hearing people approach, she twisted around in alarm. The boy fell limp. I think it was an accident, Frankenstein.'

xoptx You know why he is telling that lie.

xoptx Ask why he sought you out.

Ask why he sought you out.

'Yes, there was a brooch with a picture of a beautiful woman in it. I didn't take it. I went a little way off to fetch water, and when I returned there was a girl stooped over the body. She was tearful, but finding him beyond help, she took the brooch and ran off with it.'
'I know, unspeakable fiend, what you did. You found the poor girl sleeping in a barn, worn out by her frantic search for William, and you forced yourself upon her. I wonder that she did not take her own life. No wonder she went meekly to the gallows. Death must have been a blessing, after her ravishment at your unclean hands.'
'No!' That's not true.'
