But I don't believe it. Among its other accomplishments, it has learned how to lie.

him. Among his other accomplishments, he has learned how to lie.

Find out what it wants you to do.

Find out what he wants you to do.

'That was my mother's likeness, painted in her youth. So how did it come to be in Justine's pocket?'
'At first I was distraught at what had happened, but presently my rage returned. Not for me the delights that such beautiful creatures as that can bestow. She whose resemblance I contemplated would, in regarding me, have changed that serene smile to a look of disgust. More wretched than ever, I wandered in search of a more secluded hiding place and had come to a remote barn when I perceived a woman passing near me. It was late and she was in a state of exhaustion, while blooming in the loveliness of youth and health. Here, I thought, is one of those whose smiles are bestowed on all but me. She shall not escape. Thanks to the lessons of my fellow man, I have learned how to work mischief. I approached her while she slumbered and placed the portrait securely in one of the folds of her dress. It was only then that I recognized her from a sketch in your notebook.''I was distraught at what had happened. Moreover I knew I could not stay at the spot where the child's body lay, as others would soon come looking. Searching for a new hiding place, I came to a remote barn and had just hidden myself inside when a girl entered with a man. They lay down in the hay and coupled there, unaware of the monster that watched from a few feet away. I looked on writhing with anguish at the thought that so vital and healthy a creature would never bestow her caresses on such as I. Feeling unworthy of both the beautiful woman in the brooch and the girl who now lay asleep twined around her lover, I crept closer and dropped the brooch in the folds of her dress. I meant no harm by that.'

Now it will tell you what it wants.

Now he will tell you what he wants.

The monster leans forward and an eager note comes into its voice as we touch on what it is ithis voice as we touch on what it is he desires.
'For some days I haunted the scene of the murder. I thought you might come. Sometimes I wanted only to see you, at other times I resolved to quit the world and its miseries forever. At length I wandered towards these mountains, and have ranged through their immense recesses, consumed by a burning passion that you alone can gratify. We may not part until you have promised to comply with my requisition. I am alone, and miserable. Man will not associate with me, but another as deformed and horrible as myself would not deny herself to me. My companion must be of the same species, and have the same defects. This being you must create.'
So saying, ithe fixes his lambent gaze upon me in expectation of a reply.
'I don't understand. What are you asking?'
'You must create a female for me. This you alone can do, and I demand it of you as a right which you must not refuse.'

xoptx Perhaps worth considering.

xoptx Create a companion for this fiend? Never.

Create a companion for this fiend? Never.

The latter part of its tale has kindled anew in me the anger that died away while it narrated itsThe latter part of his tale has kindled anew in me the anger that died away while he narrated his peaceful life in the French countryside. Now I can no longer suppress the rage that burns within me.
'I do refuse it! And nothing you may do shall ever extort consent from me. Render me the most miserable of men, but you shall never make me base in my own eyes. Shall I create another like yourself, whose joint wickedness might desolate the world? Begone, I have answered you. You may torture me, but I will never consent.'
'You are in the wrong,' replies the creature, 'and, instead of threatening, I am content to reason with you. I am malicious because I am miserable. Am I not shunned and hated by all mankind? You, my creator, would tear me to pieces if you could and destroy my frame, the work of your own hands. Think of that, and then tell me why I should pity man more than he pities me? You would not call it murder if you could precipitate me into that ice rift.'

xoptx 'Indeed not. It would be justice!'

xoptx 'It is too late to reason with me, monster.'

'You see? Where is the respect for my feelings? Live with me in the interchange of kindness and, instead of injury, I would bestow every benefit upon you with tears of gratitude at your acceptance. But that, I see, will never be. So then, mine shall not be the submission of abject slavery. I will revenge my injuries. If I cannot inspire love, I will cause fear. And you, who are my arch-enemy because you created and then spurned me - to you I swear inextinguishable hatred. Have a care. I will work at your destruction, nor finish until I desolate your heart, so that you curse the hour of your birth.'

xoptx 'Your demands are insane. I refuse.'

xoptx 'So now you are threatening Elizabeth?'

'Have a care! Have a care, e'er you say that! Too late for reason? Why then, is it war? Man, I have arsenals that will lay your little life to waste. I am unyielding, untiring, fuelled by the deepest grievance. You created an existence for me that is hell - the intolerable exile from all companionship that leaves the heart no luxury for good and evil, no taste for mercy and compassion and other indulgent virtues. I am beating on the adamantine gates and no single soul will admit me. And I would still be reasonable. Before you spurn that, consider the well-being of your own friends. They are the sacrifices your pride will make if you refuse to grant my one deserving plea.'

xoptx 'I created you, and I can destroy you.'

xoptx 'So you revert to threats.'

True. I made mistakes with this one, but now I know more. The transparent skin, intended to make ithim easier to study, is too grotesque. Well, I can easily culture skin with pigmentation in it. It would be fitting if I use Elizabeth's own skin as a basis, since it is her sampler frames on which I grew the last batch. And, with a better preparation in anatomy, I need not make the frame so gross in size and proportion. She could even be beautiful...

Tell the creature you'll consider it.

A fiendish rage animates it as it sees I am unwavering. Its face wrinkles into contortions too horrible for human eyes to behold, but with an effort it calms itselfA fiendish rage animates him as he sees I am unwavering. His face wrinkles into contortions too horrible for human eyes to behold, but with an effort he calms himself enough to proceed:
'I intended to reason. This passion is detrimental to me, for you do not reflect that you are the cause of its excess. If any being felt emotions of benevolence towards me, I should return them a hundredfold. For that one creature's sake, I would make peace with the whole world! What I ask of you is reasonable and moderate. I demand a creature of another sex, but as hideous as myself. The gratification is small, but it is all that I can receive, and it shall content me. It is true we shall be monsters, cut off from all the world, but on that account we shall be more attached to one another. Our lives will not be happy, but they will be harmless, and free from the misery I now feel. Oh, my creator, make me happy! Let me feel gratitude towards you for one benefit. Let me see that I excite the sympathy of some existing thing. Do not deny me my request.'

You won't be rid of it unless you agree.

You won't be rid of him unless you agree.

'If I have threatened you and your loved ones,' says the monster, 'this is simply the way I have learned from observing my fellow man. All decisions are economic, founded on who has need and who has something to give. Well then, I have nothing to offer you - no wealth, no favours to bestow. You would not want my friendship. So I must show you the one commodity in which I am richly endowed - the ability to bestow misery. I will be generous with that, if you decline to give me what I desire.'

Your family won't be safe unless you agree.

True. But should I truly do as it asks, or merely swear to do so in order to stay itsTrue. But should I truly do as he asks, or merely swear to do so in order to stay his hand from harming my family? I feel no compulsion of honesty, for I am trapped in a cave on a remote mountain with a fiend known to be capable of the most awful crimes. Therefore it is legitimate for me to make that oath merely to escape alive. On the other hand, perhaps the monster's argument has merit. A female companion could prove a restraining influence. William's murder cannot be undone, but insofar as I must bear the guilt for that, I could spare my loved ones and all mankind from the further depredations of this fiend.

xoptx Pretend to agree, then stall for time.

xoptx It has a genuine right to ask you for a mate.

xoptx He has a genuine right to ask you for a mate.

But should I truly do as it asks, or merely swear to do so in order to stay itsBut should I truly do as he asks, or merely swear to do so in order to stay his hand from harming my family? I feel no compulsion of honesty, for I am trapped in a cave on a remote mountain with a fiend known to be capable of the most awful crimes. Therefore it is legitimate for me to make that oath merely to escape alive. On the other hand, it could be an interesting experiment. If I succeed, I will have spared mankind the vengeance of the disappointed fiend - and Frankenstein will go down in history as a modern Prometheus, creator of a new race of man.

xoptx Yes, you must do it.

xoptx Decide later. For now, just stall for time.

That is true. Despite my prejudiced emotions, I did feel moved when it related its story. I shudder when I think of the possible consequences of my consent, but after all there is some justice in its argument. Its account, and the feelings it now expresses, prove it to be a creature of refined sensations. As its maker, don't I owe it all the portion of happiness that's in my power to bestow?
It must perceive my change of feeling, for itThat is true. Despite my prejudiced emotions, I did feel moved when he related his story. I shudder when I think of the possible consequences of my consent, but after all there is some justice in his argument. His account, and the feelings he now expresses, prove him to be a creature of refined sensations. As his maker, don't I owe him all the portion of happiness that's in my power to bestow?
He must perceive my change of feeling, for he goes on:
'I swear to thee, if thou dost consent to do this, neither you nor any other human being shall ever see us again. I will go to the vast wilds of South America. My food is not that of man. I do not destroy the lamb and the kid to glut my appetite; acorns and berries afford me sufficient nourishment. My companion will be of the same nature as myself, and will be content with the same fare. We shall make our bed of dried leaves. The sun will shine on us as on man, and will ripen our food. The picture I present to you is peaceful and human, and you must feel that you could deny it only in the wantonness of power and cruelty. Pitiless as you have been towards me, I now see compassion in your eyes. Let me seize the favourable moment, and persuade you to promise what I so ardently desire.'

xoptx It would never keep its word.

xoptx A forceful plea. You should grant its request.

xoptx He would never keep his word.

xoptx A forceful plea. You should grant his request.

'You propose,' I reply, 'to fly from the habitations of man, to dwell in those wilds where the beasts of the field will be your only companions. How can you, who long for the love and sympathy of man, persevere in this exile? You will return, and again seek their kindness, and you will meet with their detestation. Your evil passions will be renewed, and you will then have a companion to aid you in the task of destruction. No, this must not be. Cease to argue the point. I cannot consent.'
The skinless face betrays few emotions, but I feel the exultation in itshis voice turning to anger. 'How inconstant are your feelings! Just a moment ago you were moved. You can see the justice of my case, so why do you again harden yourself against me? I swear to you, by the Earth which I inhabit and by you that made me: bestow on me a companion, and I will quit the neighbourhood of man and dwell in the most savage of places. My evil passions will have fled, for I shall meet with sympathy. My life will be spent in peace and, in my dying moments, I shall not curse my maker.'

xoptx It would say anything to get your agreement.

xoptx Even an abject criminal deserves a companion.

xoptx If you refuse, you'll doom your loved ones.

xoptx He would say anything to get your agreement.

xoptx Whatever his crimes, he deserves a companion.

'You swear,' I say, 'to be harmless, but have you not already shown a degree of malice that should reasonably make me distrust you? May not even this be a feint that will increase your triumph by affording a wider scope for your revenge?'
'How is this? I thought I had moved your compassion, and yet you still refuse to bestow on me the only benefit that can soften my heart, and render me harmless. If I have no ties and no affections, hatred and vice must be my portion. The love of another would destroy the cause of my crimes, and I shall become a thing of whose existence everyone will be ignorant. My vices are the children of a forced solitude that I abhor, and my virtues will necessarily arise when I live in communion with an equal. I shall feel the affections of a sensitive being, and become linked to the chain of existence and events, from which I have so far been excluded.'
Its appearance evokes nothing but fear, disgust and hatred. Standing with it in this cave of ice, I am aware of being locked in the lion's cage. But I must try my best to stifle these emotions. This is the reaction it has received from all men. I cannot sympathise with it, but I have no right to withhold from it the small portion of happiness which is yet in my power to bestow.
His appearance evokes nothing but fear, disgust and hatred. Standing with him in this cave of ice, I am aware of being locked in the lion's cage. But I must try my best to stifle these emotions. This is the reaction he has received from all men. I cannot sympathise with him, but I have no right to withhold from him the small portion of happiness which is yet in my power to bestow.
For some time there is silence between us. I reflect on everything it has said. Is it the distorting prism of memory that now suggests the promise of virtues which it displayed on first coming into existence? I recall its questioning wonder, a look of innocent trust - but in a visage so ghastly that I fled in terror. And all that kindly feeling, that could have given rise to a magnificent creature capable of poetry and rhetoric - where did that go? It has been blighted by the loathing and scorn it encountered at every turn. It had no protector. All those months, it was in my nightmares, but never in my thoughts.For some time there is silence between us. I reflect on everything he has said. Is it the distorting prism of memory that now suggests the promise of virtues which he displayed on first coming into existence? I recall his questioning wonder, a look of innocent trust - but in a visage so ghastly that I fled in terror. And all that kindly feeling, that could have given rise to a magnificent creature capable of poetry and rhetoric - where did that go? It has been blighted by the loathing and scorn he encountered at every turn. He had no protector. All those months, he was in my nightmares, but never in my thoughts.

xoptx You'll agree out of guilt, then?

xoptx And what about its threats?

xoptx And what about his threats?

Oh yes, I have not forgotten its power to harm my friends. You may be sure that I factor that into my calculations. A creature that can exist in the ice caves of the glaciers, and hide itself from pursuit among the ridges of inaccessible precipices, is a foe to be reckoned with. After a long pause of reflection, I conclude that the justice due both to it and my fellow men demands of me that I should comply with its request. Turning to it, therefore, I say, Oh yes, I have not forgotten his power to harm my friends. You may be sure that I factor that into my calculations. A creature who can exist in the ice caves of the glaciers, and hide himself from pursuit among the ridges of inaccessible precipices, is a foe to be reckoned with. After a long pause of reflection, I conclude that the justice due both to him and my fellow men demands of me that I should comply with his request. Turning to him, therefore, I say,
'I consent to your demand, on your solemn oath to quit Europe forever, and every other place in the neighbourhood of man, as soon as I shall deliver into your hands a female who will accompany you in your exile.'
'I swear,' he cries, 'by the sun, and by the blue sky of heaven, that if you grant my prayer, while they exist you shall never behold me again. Depart to your home, and commence your labours. I shall watch their progress with unutterable anxiety.'
'How will I contact you?'
'Fear not. When you are ready, I shall appear.'
It is the first time I have heard it laugh. It chills me to the bone.
Abruptly it leaves the cave, moving with such speed that it takes me a moment to realize I'm alone. When I go to the ledge, ithim laugh. It chills me to the bone.
Abruptly he leaves the cave, moving with such speed that it takes me a moment to realize I'm alone. When I go to the ledge, he is already some way off across the glacier, and is quickly lost among the undulations of the sea of ice.

It's time you got back to the inn.

Elizabeth and your father will be wondering where you are.

You're right. The sun is touching the western peaks. The monster's tale has taken all afternoon. I ought to hasten my descent towards the valley, as I will soon be encompassed in darkness, but my heart is heavy and my steps slow. The labour of winding among the little paths of the mountains, and of fixing my feet firmly as I advance, perplexes me, occupied as I am by the emotions that the occurrences of the day have produced.
Night has already fallen when I come to the halfway resting place and seat myself beside the fountain. The stars shine at intervals amid the clouds, the dark pines rise before me, and here and there a broken tree lies on the ground. It is a scene of wonderful solemnity, capable of stirring up strange thoughts.
I weep bitterly and, clasping my hands in agony, I exclaim, 'Oh! stars, and clouds, and winds, ye are all here to mock me. If ye really pity me, crush sensation and memory. Let me become as nought. But, if not, depart. Leave me in darkness.'

xoptx These are miserable thoughts.

xoptx The brooding can wait. You need to get back.

The brooding can wait. You need to get back.

Oh indeed, wild and miserable. But see the eternal twinkling of the stars - the clouds like impossible ledges of rock, like broken bits of the moon pressing down towards me. I cannot tell you how they weigh upon me, and how I start at every blast of wind as if it were the sirocco on its way to wither me.
The sun has already risen by the time I reach the village of Chamounix.
My presence, so haggard and strange, hardly calms the fears of my family, who have been sitting up all night in anxious expectation of my return.
The following day we return to Geneva. The intention of my father in coming on this trip was to divert my mind and to restore me to my lost tranquillity. If I do indeed seem changed, he cannot guess at the true reason - that now I have the best of all balms for grief: a purpose.
But the medicine has been fatal for the patient. Unable to account for the excess of misery I appear to suffer, he now hastens us home, hoping the quiet and monotony of a domestic life will by degrees alleviate my sufferings from whatsoever cause they might spring.
For myself, I am passive in all these arrangements, and even the gentle affection of my beloved Elizabeth is inadequate to draw me from the depth of my despair.
The promise I made to Adom weighs upon my mindThe promise I made to the demon weighs upon my mind. I must renew my studies, and there are refinements I need to research before the work can begin.
, like Dante's iron cowl on the heads of the hellish hypocrites. All pleasures of Earth and sky pass before me like a dream, and that thought is all that seems real. Can you wonder that sometimes a kind of insanity possesses me, or that I see continually about me a multitude of filthy animals inflicting on me incessant torture, that often extorts screams and bitter groans?
By degrees, as the days pass, the maelstrom of feelings woken in me by the meeting on the glacier becomes calmed. I enter again into everyday life, if not with great interest, at least with some degree of tranquillity. But there is only one thing that seems truly real to me now, and that occupies the bulk of my inward thoughts: the construction of the female creature.my dreadful promise to the monster.

I take no rest, but return immediately to Geneva. Even in my own heart I can give no expression to my sensations. They press on me with a mountain's weight and their excess destroys my agony beneath them. Thus I return home and, entering the house, present myself to my family.
My haggard and wild appearance awakes intense alarm, but I answer no question, scarcely can I bear to speak. I feel as if I were placed under a ban - as if I have no right to claim their sympathies, as if I never more may enjoy companionship with them.
Yet even thus I love them to adoration. To save them, I resolve to dedicate myself to my most abhorred task. The prospect of such an occupation makes every other circumstance of existence pass before me like a dream. The only real thing to me now is the construction of the female creature.my dreadful promise to the monster.

END OF CHAPTER ONE


CHAPTER TWO
CHAPTER TWO
And Neither Hopes Nor Fears Arise

Weeks pass away on my return to Geneva, and still I cannot find the courage to recommence my work. I amNaturally I fear the vengeance of the disappointed fiend, yet I am also eager to begin. My reluctance only arises because I am now acutely aware of how crude was my earlier work. I need to review and refine my process. Some anatomists and chemists in Britain have made discoveries that would be of great benefit. I need to travel to Oxford and to Edinburgh. This time I must have better equipment too. The immediate problem is how to secure the funds I need from my father. After my nervous collapse in Paris, he may not welcome a return to my former studies.

You must find a way to convince him.

Weeks pass away on my return to Geneva, and still I cannot find the courage to recommence my work. I amI fear the vengeance of the disappointed fiend, yet I am unable to overcome my repugnance to the task.
The scale of the project too is daunting. I cannot compose a female without again devoting several months to profound study and laborious disquisition. This time I am resolved to take some care and, armed with what I learned in constructing Adomthe first monster, to achieve a less repulsive result.
Great discoveries in anatomy and chemistry are being made in Britain. I would benefit greatly from pursuing my research in Oxford and Edinburgh. And this time I must have better equipment. The immediate problem is how to secure the funds I need from my father. After my nervous collapse in Paris, he may not welcome a return to my former studies - though of late he has been pleased to see a return to my former tranquillity.

You must find a way to convince him.

Weeks pass away on my return to Geneva, and still I cannot find the courage to recommence my work. I amI fear the vengeance of the disappointed fiend, yet I am unable to overcome my aversion, instilled by my nervous collapse in Paris, to the very thought of my old studies. Moreover, I am tortured already by guilt at having given life to one fiend - and that was from ignorance. To do so again, knowing what I know now, and thus unleash a second devil on the race of man, would be unforgiveable.
Yet I know that it is watching me. I can feel it. I must give the appearance of preparing to do as I have promised. Recent scientific discoveries in Britain may give me a pretext for travelling there, and thus lead it away from the friends and family ithe is watching me. I can feel it. I must give the appearance of preparing to do as I have promised. Recent scientific discoveries in Britain may give me a pretext for travelling there, and thus lead him away from the friends and family he would use to coerce me. However, to do that I will need funds from my father, and he may not welcome a return - even if only a feigned one - to the same studies that ruined my health and nerves in Paris.

You must find a way to convince him.

The melancholy mood into which I sank following William's murder is gradually passing, but every now and then it surges back without warning. On those days a devouring blackness overcasts the sun. I can bear no company or even walls around me and, fleeing as it were in horror from the stifling bosom of the family, I take refuge in the most perfect solitude. I pass whole days on the lake alone in a little boat, watching the clouds, and listening to the rippling of the waves, silent and listless. The fresh air and bright sun seldom fails to restore me to some degree of composure and, on my return, I am able to tolerate the salutations of my friends with a readier smile and a more cheerful heart.
On one such occasion, when the clink of teacups over breakfast rasps my nerves and every muttered pleasantry makes me want to lash out at them, I tell my father and Elizabeth that I will be spending the day walking in the hills along the lake.
'Oh, Victor,' says Elizabeth brightly, 'do you remember how you and I and Henri used to play along the shore? We would go for miles, inventing stories in which you and Henri were buccaneers, vying to carry away your little prize. Let me come with you today!'

xoptx Good grief, she's as tenacious as the monster.

xoptx I suppose you'll have to let her come.

'I will be walking too fast for you to keep up.'
'We could take a boat out instead.'
'But I want to go for a walk.'
'You are saying that to avoid my company.'
'Don't be ridiculous, Elizabeth. I had already decided on a walk.'
'Are you meeting someone?'
'I sincerely hope not. My ideal is that the hills will be as unpopulated as the Earth after the end of time, and I can indulge my fantasy of being the last man.'
She gets up and sweeps out of the room. 'For heaven's sake, Victor,' says my father, 'at least wait until you're married before you start having such pointless rows.'
At any rate, I get to spend my day striding furiously across the hills without the tender molestation of the nerves that I would receive from Elizabeth's company.
Upon my return at dusk, my father looks out of his study. 'May I have a word, my boy?'

Now's your chance to mention travelling to Britain.

Elizabeth rearranges the day. Instead of the invigorating hike across the hills that I had planned, we visit William's grave and then cross the lake to have lunch at an inn before returning home to tea with a gaggle of friends whom, she suddenly remembers, were invited several days ago.
'Where do you think we shall live when we are wed?' asks Elizabeth as I row us back. She changes the subject at once on seeing my expression, for it must show plainly in my face that the idea of an imminent union with my cousin fills me with horror and dismay. Of course, in that as in other things, I am bound by a solemn promise.
If Adomthe monster is observing me in secret, I should like to use today as an illustration of how its plan may not lead to the perfect bliss of companionship that ithis plan may not lead to the perfect bliss of companionship that he supposes.
Escaping from the shrill clamour of the tea party, I run into my father outside his study. 'May I have a word, my boy?'

Now's your chance to mention the trip to Britain.

'I am happy to remark, Victor, that you have resumed your former pleasures. You do seem to be more yourself these days. And yet you're still unhappy, and still avoid our society. For some time I have been at a loss to explain this, but today an idea struck me. If I am right, will you tell me? Reserve on such a point would be not only useless, but draw down misery on us all.'

xoptx What on Earth is he about to say?

xoptx Quickly, make the case for travelling abroad.

I dread to think. After making quite an affair of cutting and lighting a cigar, my father continues:
'I confess, my son, that I have always looked forward to your marriage with your cousin as the knot of our domestic happiness and a comfort in my declining years. You were attached to each other from your earliest infancy and your mother often used to remark on how well suited you are to one another. But now I see that I may have been foolish. What I conceived to be the best encouragement to my plan may have entirely destroyed it. You, perhaps, regard Elizabeth as your sister. If so, perhaps you don't have any wish that she might become your wife. Or - well, you have told me very little about your time at university. Perhaps you met there with another whom you may love? In which case, considering yourself as bound in honour to your cousin, and mindful of the promise you made your mother in her dying hour, may explain the deep misery which you seem to feel.'

xoptx Tell him how wrong he is.

xoptx Change the subject.

'My dear father, reassure yourself. I love my cousin tenderly and sincerely. I never knew any woman who excited, as Elizabeth does, my warmest admiration and affection. My future hopes and prospects are entirely bound up in the expectation of our union.'
He sinks into his chair, visibly relieved. 'My dear Victor, to hear you say that gives me more pleasure than I have had for some time. If you truly feel thus, we shall be happy in spite of the gloom cast over us by recent events. But it is this gloom that I wish to dissipate. Tell me, therefore, whether you object to an immediate solemnization of the marriage.'
Solemnization. That's a very Swiss way of putting it. Now you see why I was content to stay away for six years. My father's timing is unfortunate, and I must find some excuse to forestall the wedding. The very idea of an imminent union with my cousin fills me with horror and dismay. In any case, I am bound by a promise to the monster that I dare not be seen to break. If I did, what manifold miseries might not befall me and my devoted family? I must perform my task and let the monster depart with its mate before I can allow myself to consider the delights of marriage.I must perform my task and let the monster depart with his mate before I can allow myself to consider the delights of marriage.

Tell him you can't marry Elizabeth right now.

'In fact, father, the long periods of brooding contemplation you have noticed are not sadness, so much as they are anguish for a thing I have left undone.'
'As I thought, it is high time that - '
'I refer to my studies, which were curtailed by my illness.'
He frowns at this. 'I was given to understand from Henri Clerval's letters that your studies were what provoked the illness.'
'Henri was not present in Paris in the early days of the Revolution. Everyone was on edge. People of our class were every day being carted off for execution. Their crimes: education and wealth.'
I knew this would strike a nerve. 'Victor, I had no idea. I knew about the aristocrats, of course.'
'Eventually, the mob decided who was an aristocrat. If this house were in Paris, they would have seized it and we would be lucky to escape with our lives. Only my status as a foreigner preserved me, and that was precarious. So that was the cause of my breakdown, you see, and what troubles me is that I was never able to complete my thesis. For this reason, I should like to spend the next year or two in Britain.'

xoptx Tell him you'll wed Elizabeth when you get back.

xoptx Just let him think about what you've said.

'Of course, the wedding will be held as soon as I get back. It should take no more than a couple of years.'
'Two years.' My father nods. 'Yes, I suppose that is the best way to arrange things.'
I sit in silence while he adjusts his thinking to my plan. Evidently he hoped for the wedding to be sooner, but now at least there is a firm date to schedule for. In the meantime, either I will have fulfilled the monster's request for a mate or I will have found a way to destroy ithim. Thus will I be free of slavery to my past indiscretions, during which it is impossible for me even to think of marriage.
'I can see how, after all that fraught period spent in Paris, you would like to complete your thesis. And then you will be Doctor Frankenstein. But forgive me, Victor, I had no idea how difficult it was for you while you were away.'
I feel a twinge of guilt for exaggerating the situation during my stay in France, which really only became dangerous during the Terror under Robespierre's infamous Committee. But, this way, my father will feel better about me doing what I must, after all, do whether he likes it or not.
'There still remains the question of the betrothal,' he says. 'Have you thought what you will say to Elizabeth?'

xoptx Say you'll marry her when you return.

xoptx Tell him you can't promise anything.

'Elizabeth and I have lived as brother and sister since I was four years old,' I say, picking my words carefully. 'Therefore it is not easy for me to imagine her as my wife.'
'You promised your mother.'
'I love Elizabeth, of course. And I am mindful of my promise to Mother, even though it was exacted on her deathbed. All I say is that it will take me some time to accommodate my feelings to a new form of relationship. Two years away will help, I'm sure.'
He is disappointed not to have a firmer commitment, but finally agrees.

Now you can make plans for your departure.

'I can see how, after all that time spent in Paris, you would like to complete your thesis. And then you will be Doctor Frankenstein. Elizabeth will miss you, though. No sooner are you back, after an absence of six years, than you want to be off again. Have you thought what you will say to her?'

xoptx Say you'll marry her when you return.

xoptx Tell him you can't promise anything.

'But why ever not, Victor? You love the girl. She is devoted to you. The world is really not so complicated, my boy.'
Not his world, perhaps. But I need a plausible excuse. I cannot very well say, 'Well, father, first I intend to make an artificial woman in my laboratory.'
I could tell part of the truth - that I need to complete my studies at several universities in Britain, and to do that I need the freedom of my present unmarried state.
However, that's rather a bloodless reason to give, though true, when divorced from the incentives supplied by the monster's wrath. My father would be more amenable to a plea from the heart. I could simply say that I wish to see some more of the world before I settle down.Or I could simply say that I wish to see some more of the world before I settle down.

xoptx Sowing your wild oats is a good excuse; he'll buy that.

xoptx Tell him you need to go abroad and study.

'Ah yes, ah yes...' He puffs thoughtfully on his cigar. 'I sometimes think that I married your mother too young. It is better for a man to be worldly when he weds.'
He contents himself with the thought that my union with Elizabeth can take place immediately on my return to Geneva.
'These two years,' he says, 'will pass swiftly, and it will be the last delay that will oppose itself to your happiness. I look forward to the day when we shall all be united, and neither hopes nor fears arise to disturb our domestic calm.'
'And I too, father, await that day. By the time I get back, Elizabeth and I shall both have become wiser - and I hope happier - than we are at present.'
I sigh, but my father kindly forbears to question me further concerning the cause of my dejection. No doubt he simply hopes that new scenes and the amusement of travelling will restore my tranquillity.

You must make plans for your trip.

A letter from Henri tells me that he has left Paris. I knew that he would soon bore of academic life - if, indeed, he attended a single lecture. It has been months since we saw one another, and after a short exchange of notes it is agreed that we will meet in Strasbourg and make the trip to Britain together.

xoptx An excellent idea.

xoptx What about the monster?

I am ready to depart at the end of August. After saying his farewells, my father tactfully recalls a letter he must finish, leaving me alone with Elizabeth beside the waiting carriage.
'I am glad that you're going, cousin,' she says, holding onto the tips of my fingers with a grip that belies her words.
'I hope that you will always let me know your opinion of my decisions.'
'As your wife, naturally I will honour and obey you.'
This exchange between us is crisp, formal. Perhaps that's the best way to say goodbye.'Are you, Elizabeth?'
'It will be good for you. I regret that I'm not coming too.'
'You know why.'
She looks into my eyes. 'I would marry you today.'
'When we were children, we played at "making house", and we thought it meant pouring tea and me reading newspapers while you did needlework. If we wed now, that's how it would be. I want us to meet as strangers.'
'I don't understand.'
'So that we may become reacquainted as man and wife, not - as now - with a love such as is more fitting between brother and sister.'
She nods. Her feelings towards me are different, I know that. Perhaps two years apart will help me to adjust mine.

xoptx You can't leave it like that.

xoptx Best be off without further ado.

It's time you left.

'You say "naturally". To speak frankly, it might be better and more natural if you were not always so ready to comply with my wishes, Elizabeth.'
'What do you mean by that?'
'Nothing.' It is futile to say more. Her conception of a wife's role is entirely ingenuous.

You'd better leave now, then.

I have been troubled by the thought of how it may react when it learns of my departure. My family, unaware of their enemy's existence, would be unprotected from its attacks. But it has sworn to follow me wherever I go, and I can well believe it capable of that. Though it is hard to conceive how one so monstrous could hide itself among men, whenever I go out I have the uncomfortable sensation of being watched. Dreadful as it is to think of it tracking me balefully across Europe, I am soothed inasmuch as that will ensure the safety of my friends.I have been troubled by the thought of how he may react when he learns of my departure. My family, unaware of their enemy's existence, would be unprotected from his attacks. But he has sworn to follow me wherever I go, and I can well believe him capable of that. Though it is hard to conceive how one so monstrous could hide himself among men, whenever I go out I have the uncomfortable sensation of being watched. Dreadful as it is to think of him tracking me balefully across Europe, I am soothed inasmuch as that will ensure the safety of my friends.

That's true.

As I throw myself into the carriage, Elizabeth starts to cry. A shame, as it is a fine sunny day for a trip and I had earnestly hoped for no downpour.
'Return to me happy and tranquil, beloved,' she says between great gulping sobs. 'We all depend on you and, if you are miserable, what must our feelings be?'
The carriage is already in motion, and in place of a reply I can only wave back at her.

xoptx And what are your own feelings as you set out?

xoptx Did you remember your chemical apparatus?

xoptx Soon you'll see Henri again.

I am resolved to fulfil my promise while abroadSomehow in the next two years I must deal with the creature I created and return, if possible, a free man. Sunk in grim reflection, over the days ahead we must pass by many beautiful and majestic scenes, but I notice none of them. Even the thought of a reunion with my good friend gives me little cheer. My sole preoccupation is the work that lies ahead.

It will be good to see Henri again, surely?

It was the first case I packed. I am mindful that Adomthe monster has vowed to observe me at all times in order that ithe may keep me to my promise. For a long time after my breakdown in Paris, I could not bear even to hear the rattle of glassware or the sound of bubbling liquid on a stove, as it reminded me of the instruments in the lab where I began that terrible descent into my inner hell. But now I find myself again stirred by the old excitement, and almost eager to see how I can improve on my earlier workI cannot afford such indulgence. If I am seen to falter, the creature will bring destruction on the heads of all those I hold dear.

xoptx And your feelings about the trip?

xoptx You must be looking forward to seeing Henri.

I wait two days for him in Strasbourg. 'This is what it is to live!' he cries, advancing towards me down the street from the coach house. 'Did you see that sunset?'
'Henri, it's midday.'
'Yesterday's sunset. Any sunset!' He claps me on the shoulder, brimming with health and good spirits. 'Oh dear, Victor, you wear a frown like the four horsemen. We are two single men of good means with a year or more at leisure ahead of us. Is there no hope at all? Shall we throw ourselves under the wheels of this cart right now?'

xoptx Explain that you are still mourning William.

xoptx Tell him the truth.

xoptx Ask what news he has from Paris.

'It's gone on too long, then. Grief's like an ingrown toenail after a while. It's turned pathological, and my prescription to the doctor - '
'I am not a doctor.'
'My prescription is a slow boat down the Rhine, with plenty of stops in the vineyards.'

xoptx Impossible! You must get to Britain without delay.

xoptx Actually, that might do you good.

'If you listened more to me, Henri, you would not wonder that I am miserable, for I am haunted by a curse that shuts up every avenue to enjoyment...'
He doesn't give me the opportunity to say much. 'We've talked of this before. It's all in your mind. Victor, I realize that your brother's murder was a shock from which one cannot - and should not - expect ever to fully recover. And that his death should have been at the hands of a trusted family servant - well, I have no words adequate to salve that wound. I believe these delusions of yours arise because you received that grievous shock too soon after your illness in Paris.'
'You don't understand. I have wronged the monster I created, and so I mustIt was the monster who killed him, not Justine. And now it is threatening to do the same to Elizabeth unless IIt was the monster who killed him, not Justine. And now he is threatening to do the same to Elizabeth unless I make a female for it make a female for him to mate with.'
'No, no, Victor. This will not do.' Henri puts his arm around me. 'Have you suffered a relapse without me to nurse you, old friend? If so, I have the tonic. We shall take a cruise down the Rhine to the coast. There is no great hurry to reach England, after all.'
'But, Henri...'

It's no use. After what you said, he will not be swayed.

'Oh, not much, except that the world has turned upside down yet again. There is even talk of banning the Jacobins. Next we will hear that the gates of Heaven have been nailed shut and plastered with royalist caricatures. "All is transformed!" proclaims the head of Lavoisier.'
'Henri, you are a little drunk, I think.'
'It was a boring journey. But I'm in danger of sobering up now, so if you will support your old friend we could stagger into the tavern there and see if you can catch me up.'
'Let's take it easy. We have a year or two of indolence ahead of us.'
'Oh, was that a joke from the famously dour Victor Frankenstein? Lavoisier's head was right. Well, since I perceive a rare break in the clouds of your demeanour, perhaps this is the moment to tell you I have hired a boat to take us down the Rhine.'

xoptx That's too slow. You need to get a move on.

xoptx Why not? A river cruise will do you good.

To Henri's disappointment, we forgo a leisurely boat ride in favour of fast coaches that convey us rapidly up through the Netherlands, where we are able to take ship to Britain.
On arriving in London, I quickly avail myself of letters of introduction supplied by my old professors. These gain me entry to the circle of some of England's most distinguished natural philosophers.
'Stuffy old lurchers, the lot of 'em,' is Henri's verdict as he dresses for yet another party. 'Why don't you come out with me, Victor? I'm ganging with some fine Corinthian swells and we'll be coming it strong when the New Year's rung in.'
'I must be getting old. You young people talk a foreign lingo.'
He laughs. 'Go on, then, off to your chalk-dust soirée.'

xoptx Perhaps you should go with him.

xoptx Your work is more important.

I don't think so. Company is irksome to me. When alone I can cheat myself into a transitory peace. But to be surrounded by busy uninteresting joyous faces only brings despair to my heart. I feel an insurmountable barrier placed between me and my fellow men, a barrier sealed with the blood of William and Justine.

xoptx Now you're being morbid. Go out with Henri.

xoptx All right, if you feel that way.

Curiously, when I appear from my room dressed for dinner, his mood changes. 'Ah, it's a pity you didn't make up your mind earlier,' he says - a little evasively, or so it seems to me.
'Live for the moment, isn't that what you always say?'
'The thing is, Victor, we're going to this chap's club. It's a dinner dance, you see, and there are tickets - well, the thing of it is, he won't have got a ticket for you, do you see?'
'Quite all right. I have borrowed the proofs of Professor Hufeland's new book, so I can stay in and catch up on my reading.'
We sit in silence while Henri waits for his cab. I read the same sentence over several times, distracted by a fluttering sound. Lifting the curtain, I see a smoky yellow quarter moon rising over the cluttered Piccadilly rooftops. The noise is made by a large moth, softly butting over and over against the pane.
'If it did get out, it would soon die in this cold weather,' says Henri. I didn't hear him beside me. 'Well, I'll see you later, Victor. Have a happy New Year.'

xoptx A happy New Year? That remains to be seen.

xoptx You'd think he might change his plans.

I wouldn't want him to, just on my account. I often refuse to accompany him, alleging another engagement, that I might remain alone, so it was a little unfair of me to spring my presence on him tonight without warning.
When I see Henri's reaction to our travels, I am reminded of how I felt on my first escape from home. Everything is new and wonderful. Each day is a foaming vessel ready to be drunk right down to the dregs. He's forever busy with sightseeing, shopping, tea parties, dinners and balls. And he is always very witty and entertaining when he tries to recount to me every fresh experience, but I am too focused on my work to be distracted. I fear my frequent introspection is the only check to his enjoyments, for he is not disturbed by the bitter cares and reflections that haunt my waking hours.
I have plenty to occupy me tonight, in any case. I have begun to collect the materials necessary for a new creature, which is to me like the torture of single drops of water continually falling on the head. Every thought I devote to it is an extreme anguish, and every word I speak in allusion to it causes my lips to quiver and my heart to palpitate. Not the organic materials yet, for they are perishable, but the armatures and frames on which certain components will be cultured.would credibly be cultured.

What is the next step?

Our route will take us down the Rhine to Rotterdam, and from there to London. On the river we pass many willowy islands and beautiful towns. We stay one night at Manheim, and, on the fifth day after our departure from Strasbourg, arrive at Mainz.
The landscape below this point becomes much more picturesque. The river descends rapidly and winds between hills that are not high, but still steep. We see many ruined castles standing on the edges of precipices, surrounded by inaccessible black woods. This part of the Rhine presents a singularly variegated landscape. In one spot you view rugged hills and ruined castles overlooking tremendous precipices, with the dark Rhine rushing beneath. Then, on the sudden turn of a promontory, flourishing vineyards lie revealed, with green sloping banks, a meandering river, and populous towns.

xoptx Quite a sightseeing trip.

xoptx You must not dawdle, Victor!

It is indeed. We are travelling at the time of the vintage, and the song of the labourers rings out from the vineyards as we glide down the stream. Even I, depressed in mind, and with my spirits continually agitated by gloomy feelings, even I am pleased. I lie at the bottom of the boat and, as I gaze on the cloudless blue sky, I drink in a tranquillity to which I have long been a stranger.

xoptx Is Henri also enjoying the voyage?

xoptx We must hope the monster is not watching all this.

Beyond Cologne, we descend to the plains of Holland, and at Rotterdam take ship to England. It's now December, and the air feels thin and clear when I get my first sight of the white cliffs of Britain.

At last your work can begin.

At last you can resume your studies.

Well, if such are my own impressions, how can I describe Henri's?
'I feel as if I have been transported to Fairyland,' he announces one evening over supper. 'I have visited the lake of Lucerne, where the snowy mountains descend almost perpendicularly to the water. I have seen that lake agitated by a tempest, when the wind tore up whirlwinds of water. I have seen the mountains of La Valais, and the Pays de Vaud. But this country, Victor, pleases me more than all those wonders. The mountains of Switzerland are more majestic and strange, but there is a charm in the banks of this divine river that I never before saw equalled. Look at the castle there that overhangs yon precipice. How did they build that? And another on that island, look, almost concealed amongst the foliage. And now that group of labourers coming from among their vines. And there's a village tucked away in the recess of the mountain. Nearly missed that. Look, Victor! Surely, the spirit that inhabits and guards this place has a soul more in harmony - '
'Yes. Pass that bottle before you have quite drained it.'
'This bottle? This cherished amphora, you mean, which contains the oil that fires the lamp of poetry? Bottled at the Hippocrene - '
'According to the label, it was bottled at Oberwesel.'
'Oh, you are just a scientist. Facts are all you care about. There is no hope for you.'

Eventually this trip must end.

Why do you say that? I hope that it is watching. I hope it is crouched in that grove, or up there on the crag. As long as it is nowhere near my family, all is well. If it chafes at my inactivity - well, let me enjoy that small torment that I am able to inflict on it. That is little enough, given the grisly promise it exacted from me. Even if its demand is just, I cannot wait for the day when I will be free of any obligation to it.Why do you say that? I hope that he is watching. I hope he is crouched in that grove, or up there on the crag. As long as he is nowhere near my family, all is well. If he chafes at my inactivity - well, let me enjoy that small torment that I am able to inflict on him. That is little enough, given the grisly promise he exacted from me. Even if his demand is just, I cannot wait for the day when I will be free of any obligation to him.

xoptx In that case, how is the rest of the trip?

xoptx Even so, you cannot put off your task forever.

If it is enough to dispel even my melancholy, I need hardly tell you how it transports Henri, whose habitual mood is gay and frolicsome.
'I feel as if I have been transported to Fairyland,' he announces one evening over supper. 'I have visited the lake of Lucerne, where the snowy mountains descend almost perpendicularly to the water. I have seen that lake agitated by a tempest, when the wind tore up whirlwinds of water. I have seen the mountains of La Valais, and the Pays de Vaud. But this country, Victor, pleases me more than all those wonders. The mountains of Switzerland are more majestic and strange, but there is a charm in the banks of this divine river that I never before saw equalled. Look at the castle there that overhangs yon precipice. How did they build that? And another on that island, look, almost concealed amongst the foliage. And now that group of labourers coming from among their vines. And there's a village tucked away in the recess of the mountain. Nearly missed that. Look, Victor, look! Surely, the spirit that inhabits and guards this place has a soul more in harmony - '
'Yes. Pass that bottle before you have quite drained it.'
'This bottle? This cherished amphora, you mean, which contains the oil that fires the lamp of poetry? Bottled at the Hippocrene - '
'According to the label, it was bottled at Oberwesel.'
'Oh, you are just a scientist. Facts are all you care about. There is no hope for you.'

How soon do you reach England?

Our next stop is Oxford. Unlike Paris, where the university nests within the bosom of the city, here the magnificent colleges, interwoven by the lovely silver thread of the Isis and with stone walls and spires ablaze in the cold January light, create an impression of an Empyrean realm dedicated to the sacred pursuit of knowledge.
After a few days, however, my elation is tinged with disappointment. Oxford's curriculum elevates the arts but, in the sciences, places more emphasis on the teachings of the past than on the uncovering of nature's many still-guarded secrets. Bacon taught the supreme importance of empirical methods, but these scholars of today content themselves merely with endless discussion of his experiments.
At one lecture, my patience wears so thin that, under the pretext of asking a question, I draw attention to a number of developments in biology and chemistry of which the professor seems unaware. Our dispute creates a stir of excitement among the students, who previously gave every impression of slumber. To give him credit, rather than taking offence, he encourages me to speak and afterwards approaches me with an invitation to dine at his college.

xoptx You should accept.

xoptx It's time to move on.

We have a glass of sherry in the common room and then, to my astonishment, one of the dons opens the window, swings his legs over the sill and disappears from view. Others follow and I experience a momentary dreamlike dislocation of reality as I imagine them all jumping to their deaths in the night. Then my host explains, 'It's the quickest way to reach the hall. Across the roof, you see.'
I follow him. Outside, the flat roof is awash with rain and the dons hurry, hunched over, towards the door of the dining hall. In their slick black robes they look like great hopping crows.
My host and I have just been discussing the effect of ethyl mesylate on living cells. Shouting the last part of what I was saying, I brace myself for a dash through the pelting rain when he catches my arm. I recognize the look. What a time to be struck by a profound thought - but, I appreciate, we must respect the moment, for inspiration is not in our control. Therefore we stand together, soaking up water like two strips of blotting paper, discussing Man's progress at prising out the pearls God left hidden. And all the while the lightning crashes overhead.
'Oh my, we're getting quite wet,' he says after a while. 'And instead of feeding you, here I am picking your brains.'
'I only hope my theories have some practical application in your own work, Professor Tyrell.'

Where is your next stop after Oxford?

We make our way to Edinburgh by way of Chester and Liverpool. While in Liverpool, Henri suffers a misfortune that sets back our itinerary by several days.

xoptx What happened to him?

xoptx Never mind, you can press on now.

According to his own account, he was attacked and robbed. The first I know of it is when I am awoken by someone stumbling about in the parlour that adjoins our two bedrooms. I emerge bleary-eyed to find Henri wilted in a chair, a blood-soaked rag pressed to his bruised and lacerated face.
'Let me do that, I'm a doctor.'
'Not actually a doctor yet,' he mumbles through swollen lips.
'Well, if you were dead I could cut you up competently enough. This water is no good, it needs to be hot. I'll ring for the maid.'
He is quite a mess. Apart from the blood, his jacket is covered in muddy boot-prints. 'I am thinking of a career as a welcome mat,' he says. Then, 'Ow!' as laughter splits his lip again.
'What happened?'
He waves it away. 'I took an injudicious short cut by the docks.'
I cannot imagine which two clubs or dinner parties might have adjoined that part of town, but I have noticed that Henri has a taste for some rough dives. I don't press him on it.

xoptx Do you think that perhaps the monster did this?

xoptx As soon as he is recovered, you can set off.

I do fear the consequences of disappointing Adomthe demon, having now neglected my promise for some time. But what possible motive would he have for assaulting Henri, who is entirely blameless of the sad circumstances of his existence?I often fear that the fiendI often fear that the creature has followed me, and might punish my tardiness by harming Henri. When these thoughts possess me, I stay as close to Henri as his shadow to protect him from the fancied rage of his destroyer.
I feel as if I have committed some great crime, the consciousness of which hangs over me. I am guiltless, in that I never intended my actions to harm anyone, but I have indeed drawn down a horrible curse upon my head, as mortal as that of crime.

xoptx Perhaps the monster is still in Switzerland.

xoptx Then it's time you continued your journey.

Then it's time you continued your journey.

If so, it is free to wreak itsIf so, he is free to wreak his vengeance on my relatives, a thought that torments me at every moment. I wait for letters from home with feverish impatience, and if they are delayed I am overcome by a thousand fears. When they do arrive, and I see the handwriting of Elizabeth or my father, I hardly dare to read them in case of some dreadful news. But no doubt it is only my own guilt that makes me distrust the creature's good faith.

You should press on to Scotland with all speed.

Edinburgh has a reputation for being at the forefront of anatomical work. Over the last few months, my reading has reminded me of my weakness in that field. My grasp of biochemical processes and electricity is strong, but in the simple mechanical construction of the human body I am a dilettante. It is surprising, indeed, that the monster is not still more loathsome in appearance. A child with a lump of clay has as much artistry as I with surgeon's scalpel.

xoptx What does Henri make of Edinburgh?

xoptx Who do you plan to meet while you're here?

Oh, you can't possibly be interested in that. Now, I have letters of introduction to several members of the Royal College of Surgeons here...

xoptx Really, what are his impressions of the town?

xoptx Introductions to whom in particular?

In his own words, then: 'I do not like Edinburgh so well as Oxford, whose antiquity is more pleasing. But the beauty and regularity of the new town of Edinburgh, its romantic castle, and its environs, the most delightful in the world, Arthur's Seat, St Bernard's Well, and the Pentland Hills, compensate for the change and fill me with cheerfulness and admiration.'
There, such are Henri's thoughts on Edinburgh. Now, I must hurry. I am meeting somebody.

Who?

In a coffee house near the university, I encounter the celebrated Dr Robert Campbell. To the dismay of the waiters, we are soon sketching out our theories with the aid of napkins.
'You must work on your surgical techniques,' Campbell tells me. 'A man who is only speculative in anatomy and does not learn to be both practical and accurate is doomed to remain a dabbler.'
'Are there any lectures you can recommend?'
'Lectures? You're not listening to me, Frankenstein. You need to grab a knife - well, this is a spoon, but it will do to illustrate. Put your hand to it. Learn to dissect every part. Each bit of the body is an ingenious machine. You Swiss like your clocks, I'm told. And do you go to lectures to learn about clocks? No, you take them apart.'
'Shall I help you with that cake, sir?' ventures a waiter.
Campbell waves him away and mangles a couple of slices for us using the spoon. 'Correct tools help also,' he says with a smile. 'Get yourself some good surgical instruments from Nicolson in Drummond Street, and come along to my classes at the Company of Surgeons.'

xoptx That's worth considering.

xoptx What he's proposing could take months of study.

It is a great opportunity for me. To be taught surgery and practical anatomy by Robert Campbell is every physiology student's dream. But it would mean more delays. Already nine months have passed since I made my promise on the glacier. Will Adom tolerate a further delay while I refine my skills?the monster tolerate a further delay while I refine my skills?

xoptx Even if it means you can build a better mate?

xoptx Good point. You'd better get on with it.

'I must, with regret, decline your offer,' I tell Campbell. 'I have a certain project that demands my full attention, and it would be a waste to attend your classes without being able to devote myself to them.'
He gives me such a penetrating look that for a shocking moment I feel as if he has peeled away my every defence and can see the secrets of my innermost heart.
He produces a loud belch. 'That's better. Too much sugar and tea sloshing around in the guts can play the very devil with digestion. Now, what were we saying? Oh, you can't see your way to it, then? That's too bad. You're a bright fellow, Frankenstein, but I have a feeling you're one of those that wants to run before you can walk.'
We say our affable farewells and I go back to the lodgings to prepare for the next and final stage of my journey.

About time!

I begin attending Campbell's private lessons. In theory, the cadavers he uses should all be of convicted felons, but I don't think so many criminals are executed in Scotland. When I ask him about it, he says only that 'wherever there is demand, there will be someone to supply it.'
After a few classes, I return to our lodgings to find Henri pacing fretfully beside the fire. 'Victor, I would like a word with you.'

xoptx Another time, tell him.

xoptx Better find out what's troubling him.

Under Campbell's tutelage, I am soon accomplished with the scalpel and have a thorough understanding of the workings of the human body. Unlike the surgical lectures at the university, his private classes are not just demonstrations in a theatre. We students are expected to get our hands thoroughly dirty, and thanks to a steady supply of cadavers I do so.
Each day - no, each minute of his instruction reveals to me the mistakes and oversights I made when constructing the original monster. Now I can work on a more delicate scale.
'I never thought, as a student of the sciences, that I would need to become an artist too,' I say to him one day as we're washing our hands.
'Man, if you had said that to Leonardo or Michelangelo!' he scoffs. 'They'd have thought you stark mad.'

Are you now ready?

It seems he is concerned that I am slipping back into my old ways. 'I can tell that this anatomical work disturbs you, Victor. If not for the dedication with which you pursue it, I would have said it disgusts you.'
'Is this chicken left out for me? If I were truly prostrate with disgust, I'd have no appetite.'
'You know what I mean. In Paris you could not bear the sight of your scientific instruments. The doctors told me you had developed a neurotic phobia.'
'I think that was more of a guillotine phobia than a scalpel phobia. In any case, what if some of the work does disgust me? Francis of Assisi kissed a leper to prove his devotion; I only have to stick my fingers into a corpse's cranium. I'll have those pickled walnuts if you don't want them.'

xoptx Perhaps you should abandon the anatomy lessons.

xoptx Don't take any notice of Henri.

Yes. It only remains for me to find some remote location where I can carry out my work in solitude. I have no doubt that Adomthe creature has been close at hand all this time. Now ithe will follow me into solitude and there, I dare hope, this horror will find a conclusion.

END OF CHAPTER TWO


CHAPTER THREE

CHAPTER THREE
Forebodings of Evil

I have fixed on one of the Orkney islands as the scene of my labours. I explain to Henri that I wish to make a tour of that part of the country on my own. 'You enjoy yourself, and we will rendezvous in Perth or Aberdeen. I may be absent a month or two, but I ask you not to come looking for me. I need peace and solitude for a short time and, when I return, I hope it will be with a lighter heart, more congenial to your own temper.'
For a moment he looks at me in silence, then seems to come to some tremendous inner decision. 'I know why you're saying this, Victor,' he blurts out.'I wish you would reconsider, my friend. I fear that solitude is the very worst thing for you.'

xoptx You had better ask him why.

xoptx Tell him nothing will dissuade you.

Tell him nothing will dissuade you.

He sinks into a chair and pulls at his hair, a gesture that strikes me as either Biblical or theatrical, depending on how charitable one wishes to be.
'I know that I've been weak!' he cries. 'A man cannot help his nature, and mine is ardent. Oh Victor, have you never done anything which caused you both satisfaction and shame?'
'I really have no idea what you're talking about. Shall I get you a brandy?'
'Blow the brandy! I'm trying to explain...' He looks up at me, suddenly very calm. 'I sometimes think there's something broken in me. In the way I'm made.'
'We cannot help how we're made, old friend. I am afflicted with a melancholy spirit, and I know my brooding gives pain to Elizabeth and Father, yet I am powerless to change.'
'Don't leave me alone, Victor. I'm afraid of what I shall do.'

xoptx You'll have to take him with you.

xoptx Explain that you must go without him.

Seeing that I am bent on my plan, Henri ceases to remonstrate.
'You will write often, won't you?' he says. 'I would rather be with you in your solitary rambles than with these Scottish people, whom I do not know. Hasten then, my dear friend, to return, that I may again feel myself somewhat at home, which I cannot do in your absence.'
I clap him on the shoulder. 'I am willing to bet, when I next walk in that door, you'll say, "What? Back already?" '

Do you find a suitable location for your work?

How well that will work! A picture of domestic simplicity: me to my elbows in body parts and noxious fluids all day, Henri taking his bottle of cognac for long rustic walks.
No, whatever demons my friend thinks he faces - and they are no worse than homesickness and excessive carousing, in my opinion - he will have to reach his own accommodation with them in my absence.

Then tell him that.

One of the remotest of the islands. It's perfect for my purposes, being hardly more than a rock whose high sides are continually beaten by the waves. The soil is barren, scarcely affording a living for its half-dozen gaunt inhabitants. Food and even fresh water must be procured from the mainland some five miles away.
There are just three miserable huts on the whole island. The one I have hired comprises two rooms, and would be better described as a stable than a house. The thatch has fallen in, the walls are unplastered, and the door is off its hinges.
'You'll no doubt want it done up a wee bit,' says the man who is renting it to me, using the same tone with which an undertaker offers the bereaved a choice of coffins.
I order only the most essential repairs. When my few sticks of furniture arrive from the mainland, the hovel is almost habitable. I feared my arrival might stir up gossip, but the senses of the cottagers here have been benumbed by want and squalid poverty. So I am left entirely alone on the headland.

xoptx Alone but for the monster, perhaps.

xoptx Now you can begin work.

Oh, I do not doubt that it has followed me, and is waiting to reveal itself to me when I finish, in order that it might receive the companion it has dreamt of.
Oh, I do not doubt that he has followed me, and is waiting to reveal himself to me when I finish, in order that he might receive the companion he has dreamt of.
I devote the morning to labour, but in the evening, when the weather permits, I walk along the beach and listen to the waves as they roar and dash among the rocks at my feet. It is a monotonous yet ever-changing scene, desolate and appalling even at this time of year. I like it.
I had not intended to animate the monster's consort, but things have changed. Now I have the knowledge to put right my earlier mistakes. Instead of a monster, I could craft a living woman whose appearance would not disgust any man who looked at her. She might even be beautiful. A Pandora for my Adom.
So there is the question. I could. But should I?

xoptx Yes.

xoptx No.

You're right. I must not allow hubris and curiosity to lead me astray a second time. Somehow I must confound the monster's desires, whether they are just or not, or else a race of such creatures could threaten mankind's guardianship of this world.
The sun has set and the moon is just rising from the sea. I have been here for weeks now. The form of a female, counterpart to the filthy thingcreature I brought to life three years ago, is now complete and lies inert upon the bench, a gross hulk of flesh bathed in a tank of chemicals. She awaits the spark of electricity that will begin the process of vitalization. But that is a spark that will never come.
I have already created one fiend whose unparalleled barbarity has desolated my heart. Would I now form another being, of whose nature I am equally ignorant? She might become a thousand times more malignant than her mate, delighting in murder and wretchedness. If I were to throw this switch, and send that pulse of inception through her frame, it has sworn to quit the neighbourhood of man and make its home in a far desert. But even if I could trust ithe has sworn to quit the neighbourhood of man and make his home in a far desert. But even if I could trust him, she has made no such promise.
Another thought stays my hand. AdomThe creature loathes its own deformity already. Why should it find anything to admire in this fleshy immensity, this obscene parody of the female form? And what would she make of it? In her disgust, she might turn to the superior beauty of man - and if she did, dooming it to loneliness, deserted even by one of its own species, what terrors might that not provoke it to? loathes his own deformity already. Why should he find anything to admire in this fleshy immensity, this obscene parody of the female form? And what would she make of him? In her disgust, she might turn to the superior beauty of man - and if she did, dooming him to loneliness, deserted even by one of his own species, what terrors might that not provoke him to?

xoptx But you made a vow.

xoptx You're right. Destroy her while you can.

The sun has set and the moon is just rising from the sea. I have been here for weeks now. The form of a female, counterpart to the filthy thingcreature I brought to life three years ago, is now complete and lies inert upon the bench, a gross hulk of flesh bathed in a tank of chemicals. She awaits the spark of electricity that will begin the process of vitalization. But that is a spark that will never come.

xoptx Why not?

xoptx Good - destroy her now.

Although I am to blame for the calamity of Adom'smy creature's life, I cannot allow guilt to multiply that error. Professor Waldman was right to say that a moral error is like any other. The mistake is when, having erred, one begins to think of oneself as irrevocably flawed. Even if the creature and its mate were to leave Europe, as it promised, one of the first results of those sympathies for which itEven if the creature and his mate were to leave Europe, as he promised, one of the first results of those sympathies for which he thirsts would be children. A race of monsters would be propagated upon the Earth - Homo superior, as they might see themselves - who could challenge the very existence of our species. Have I a right, for my own benefit, to inflict this curse upon everlasting generations? When we spoke on the glacier, I was moved by the sophisms of the being I created. But, if I were to weaken now and close the circuit that will vivify this grotesque fleshy mound, I shudder to think that future ages would curse me as their nemesis, whose selfishness did not hesitate to buy my own peace at the price, perhaps, of the existence of the whole human race.

xoptx A real danger. You should destroy her.

xoptx Don't be so timid, Victor. Animate her.

Yes - and I must do it quickly, before conscience or cowardice saps me of my resolve. Here is the lancet. With a few quick thrusts I sever the main arteries and watch the strange rose-coloured blood of the creature seep into the amnic waters that surround her. Whole galaxies of different futures seem to blossom and die in those fluid patterns, eternities crawling by in slow seconds.
A noise rouses me from my reverie - a strange, soft, unearthly keening. Looking up, I see outlined in the moonlight the heavy misshapen head of the monster. It looks from the dead form of its unrisen mate to me, her destroyer. The keening wells up, its howl shatters the night, and it is gone.

He looks from the dead form of his unrisen mate to me, her destroyer. The keening wells up, his howl shatters the night, and he is gone.

It will never forgive you.

He will never forgive you.

What's that? There, at the window - it is the monster! So itWhat's that? There, at the window - it is he! So he did indeed follow me, marking every step of my progress, and now is here to claim the fulfilment of my promise.
A vow that the fiend extracted from me only by dint of the most terrible threats. No, I will not be dissuaded. Here is the lancet. With a few quick thrusts - there! and there! - I sever the main arteries. She is dead, that never knew life. See where her blood streams into the amnic waters in which she is suspended. Whole galaxies of dying futures seem encoded in those fluid patterns, lost eternities that will never come to be crawling by and fading in slow seconds.
A noise rouses me from my reverie - a strange, soft, unearthly keening. Looking up to the window, I see outlined in the moonlight the heavy misshapen head of the monster. It looks from the dead form of its unrisen mate to me, her destroyer. The keening wells up, its howl shatters the night, and it is gone.He looks from the dead form of his unrisen mate to me, her destroyer. The keening wells up, his howl shatters the night, and he is gone.

It will never forgive you.

He will never forgive you.

Undisturbed by any human company for days and weeks on end, I work swiftly. The bones are taking shape. I am using a kind of coral -

xoptx Pardon me. Coral?

xoptx Will you make her look like Adom?

xoptx Will you make her look like the monster?

xoptx And when she is complete, what then?

That's a good point. From what I learned in Dr Campbell's classes, it's now within my ability to make her appearance much less alien than Adomthe original creature's. Not only need she not be monstrous, she could be lovely. A perfect woman for a new race of mankind.
But what, then, would she make of the filthy thing that comes to claim her as its bride?he who comes to claim her as his bride?

xoptx Make her beautiful.

xoptx No, she must be as hideous as her mate.

I thought that would intrigue you. I had the idea back in Paris, after seeing a veteran hurrying up the street on a wooden peg leg, and I realized it is not necessary to use pre-existing body parts for the entire skeleton. My first thought was to have the long bones carved in hardwood, and then bone grafts would only be needed where the ligaments are anchored. But those would not be capable of regeneration, and my desire was to make a living creature, not a mechanical automaton. So then I remembered 'of his bones are coral made'. A good strong substance, coral. I could reverse Alonso's fate and make my creature's bones from it.
The inspiration came too late for me to incorporate it into Adom'sthe original monster's design, but this is one of many improvements. See this femur. Indistinguishable from a human bone, apart from the pinkish hue, but it is twice as strong - and lighter, feel. Now I must put it back into the chemical bath. None of these components are stable until connected together and energized.

Will you make her look like Adom?

Will you make her look like the monster?

And when she is complete, what then?

Employed in this most detestable occupation, and immersed in a solitude where nothing ever relieves my attention from the grisly scene in which I am engaged, I daily grow more restless and nervous.
Three years have passed since I was engaged in the same manner. Then, seeking to create a new and glorious race of man, I brought into existence a fiend whose unparalleled barbarity has desolated my family and filled my heart with bitterest remorse. And now I am about to create another such being, whose form I can fashion with greater skill but whose nature is entirely outside my control.
And that is what gives me pause. My first experiment spawned a monster with no moral qualms. She might be worse.

xoptx Together they might breed an entire race of their kind.

xoptx What if she rejects Adom?

xoptx What if she rejects the fiend?

At first I thought to be rid of it by doing as it asked. I told myself that the loneliness that made it a monster was my fault, for spurning my creation, and so I must atone. And when this foul work is done, and it claims its mate, the two of them will leave and never trouble me again.
Or so I thought. But gradually, like a mist lifting over the course of several days, it occurs to me that by making me do this it is not severing our ties, but making me its creature.At first I thought to be rid of him by doing as he asked. I told myself that the loneliness that made him a monster was my fault, for spurning my creation, and so I must atone. And when this foul work is done, and he claims his mate, the two of them will leave and never trouble me again.
Or so I thought. But gradually, like a mist lifting over the course of several days, it occurs to me that by making me do this he is not severing our ties, but making me his creature.

It's too late for second thoughts.

If the creature and its mate leave Europe, as it promised, one of the first results of those sympathies for which itIf the creature and his mate leave Europe, as he promised, one of the first results of those sympathies for which he thirsts would be children. A race of monsters would be propagated upon the Earth - Homo superior, as they might see themselves - who could challenge the very existence of our species.
