Have I a right, for my own benefit, to inflict this curse upon everlasting generations? When we spoke on the glacier, I was moved by the sophisms of the being I created. But, if I were to weaken now and close the circuit that will vivify this grotesque fleshy mound, I shudder to think that future ages would curse me as their nemesis, whose selfishness did not hesitate to buy my own peace at the price, perhaps, of the existence of the whole human race.
As well she might. AdomAs well she might. The creature loathes its own deformity. Why should she find anything to admire in it? She might turn in her disgust to the superior beauty of man - and if she quits the monster, consigning it to even deeper loneliness than before, how would it respond? Deserted even by the only other of its own species, what terrors might that not provoke it to? loathes his own deformity. Why should she find anything to admire in him? She might turn in her disgust to the superior beauty of man - and if she quits the monster, consigning him to even deeper loneliness than before, how would he respond? Deserted even by the only other of his own species, what terrors might that not provoke him to?
She takes shape under my hands and she is lovely. More perfect than any woman can be who is formed by the grossness of life and worn, no matter how subtly, by the rough treatment of daily existence. My creature, my Pandora, by contrast is as radiant and flawless as an angel that has found embodiment on Earth. Her very flesh, cultured without blemish, proportioned to Classical standards, glows like the full moon. The ritual of continually adjusting the flow of chemicals that nurture her perfection, the act of shaping her limbs and running my fingers across her skin - in doing these things, I feel almost as if I am performing some new kind of sacrament.
These days it is all too easy to get lost in my work. Sometimes I am surprised by the dawn, having lost hours in unconscious contemplation of my creation. Now she is almost complete. I am just draining the tank, transferring her slender form to the trestle on which I will apply the vitalizing charge, when I become aware that the sun is setting. Reaching for the lantern, my gaze strays up to the window and with a start I see Adomthe demon there. An obscene grin is stamped upon its bony red face. there. An obscene grin is stamped upon his bony red face.
It has seen how my hands lovingly caressed her as I lifted her from the tank, that's why it leers so horribly. When I think of itsHe has seen how my hands lovingly caressed her as I lifted her from the tank, that's why he leers so horribly. When I think of his misshapen hands upon this immaculate girl... The image is appalling, even blasphemous. How can I awake her into life only to consign her to such a fate? It would be a blacker crime than any I have so far committed.
As I lean forward on the bench, my fingers brush something slim and cold. A scalpel. If I can summon the courage, I should destroy her now rather than suffer her to be taken by the monster.

xoptx Then do it quickly.

xoptx The monster will be angry.

I turn my back on itI turn my back on him and go on with my work. 'So you did follow me,' I say. 'And now you can see what all of that loitering in forests and hiding in caves has been for. Your mate - your Eve, or your Lilith if you prefer - lies ready to receive the spark of life.'

xoptx Do it.

xoptx There's still time to destroy her.

Everything in its countenance speaks of malice and treachery. It envies all mankind, I see that now. It was abandoned? Romulus was raised by wolves! Cicero was banished by his own people! Like an adolescent, it blames everyone but itself. And itEverything in his countenance speaks of malice and treachery. He envies all mankind, I see that now. He was abandoned? Romulus was raised by wolves! Cicero was banished by his own people! Like an adolescent, he blames everyone but himself. And he has suffered, I don't deny that, but there is a time when a man must settle the bill of his own misconduct, not place it on another's account. I know my own culpability well enough. I don't seek to excuse it now, merely to draw a line and say, 'No more!'

Then destroy her now before he can stop you.

Then destroy her now before it can stop you.

As I proceed in my labour, it becomes ever more horrible and irksome to me. Sometimes I cannot prevail on myself to enter my workshop for several days. At other times I toil through the night in order to hasten completion of the filthy thing. It is a truly vile process. During my first experiment, a kind of enthusiastic frenzy blinded me to the horror of what I was doing. My mind was intently fixed on the final goal, and my eyes were shut to the horror of my proceedings. But this time I went into it in cold blood, and my heart often sickens at the work of my hands. Employed in this most detestable occupation, and immersed in a solitude where nothing ever calls my attention from the actual scene in which I am engaged, I daily grow more restless and nervous.

It will soon be finished.

Every day I become more revolted by this work. Sometimes I cannot prevail on myself to enter my laboratory. On other days I toil through the night to hasten completion of the filthy thing. It is a truly vile process. During my first experiment, a kind of enthusiastic frenzy blinded me to the horror of what I was doing. My mind was intently fixed on the final goal, and my eyes were shut to the horror of my proceedings. But this time I've approached it in cold blood, and my heart often sickens at the work of my hands.

xoptx Do you think Adom is here, watching you?

xoptx Do you think the monster is here on the island?

xoptx Your work must be almost complete.

The female is taking shape under my hands. What a hideous thing, a true golem of clay-like flesh. I have not troubled to give her the translucent skin of her prototype, for no scientist will ever study her inner workings. I hope, indeed, that she will flee from these shores with her mate and never be seen by human eyes. She is a gorgon, a harpy, a distaff miscreation.
The sun has set and the moon is just rising from the sea. I have been here for weeks now, but I am determined to finish her tonight because I could not bear another day. Oh God, the feel of her under my hands.
What's that? There, at the window - it is the monster! So ithe! So he did indeed follow me, marking every step of my progress, and now is here to claim the fulfilment of my promise. This ghastly freak, this lusus naturae, which he gazes upon with such an expression of unholy desire. 'What, you monster? You would embrace this grotesque immensity? Is your need for companionship so desperate?'
Look at its countenance. Have you ever seen anything so expressive of the utmost extent of malice and treachery? Was it madness that made me promise to create another like it? Or simple cowardice? Shall I give it the mate it craves, and see a race of these abnormal things spread like vermin across the globe?Look at his countenance. Have you ever seen anything so expressive of the utmost extent of malice and treachery? Was it madness that made me promise to create another like him? Or simple cowardice? Shall I give him the mate he craves, and see a race of these abnormal things spread like vermin across the globe?

xoptx You must not create another monster. Destroy her.

xoptx Keep working.

xoptx Never! Destroy her now, before it can stop you.

xoptx You must, or it will murder your loved ones.

xoptx Never! Destroy her now, before he can stop you.

xoptx You must, or he will murder your loved ones.

I wish you had not said that! Every moment I fear to meet my persecutor. Sometimes, walking along the shore, I keep my eyes fixed on the ground, fearing to raise them lest they should encounter the one that I so dread to behold. This damnable, desolate spot... I chose it to spare my fellow man from its wrath, yet now I tremble to think that I will be all alone when it comes to claim its companion.who I so dread to behold. This damnable, desolate spot... I chose it to spare my fellow man from his wrath, yet now I tremble to think that I will be all alone when he comes to claim his companion.

It is too late to back out now.

Here is the knife. The thought and the deed must be one. There - and there! Once I start slicing, I cannot stop. Trembling with passion, I tear the female creature's body to pieces.
AdomThe wretch, seeing me irrevocably destroy the mate on whose future existence itshis happiness depended, gives vent to a howl of devilish despair. His yellow eyes harden in a look of deadly animus and, in an instant, he is gone.

From now on, be always on your guard.

Be on your guard against his vengeance.

How powerfully our memories can be stimulated by the senses. As I close the circuit, there comes the familiar snap of electrical force, the acrid tang in the air, the acid flavour on the tongue and the faint odour of singed hair. With this instantaneous barrage of impressions, that come more swiftly than I am able to describe them, the intervening three years are dissolved away and I feel myself transported back to that rain-lashed garret in Paris.
As then, a moment of heart-stopping silence is followed by the twitch of new muscles stirring to life. Her eyes open - the pupils of equal dimensions, adjusting satisfactorily to the dim lamplight. Her pulse is strong, settling down to the forty beats per minute that I recorded in the original experiment three years ago.
AdomThe monster is beside me. We watch, both silent and awestruck, as the second of itshis race rises and takes her first steps. And there - hear the sound of her breath! That life didn't exist minutes ago. I did this. I, Frankenstein, created woman!
'You have done everything I asked.'
ItHe moves towards her. Emotions flow across her face at the approach of her mate. Curiosity. Uncertainty. Interest. Yearning. Recognition. Fear. AdomThe monster tenderly takes her head in his hands, exploring the lines of the jaw, the orbits of the eyes. This moment, the meeting of a new race of man, has not been seen on Earth since Adam and Eve looked into each other's gaze.
Her expression of rapture changes to surprise, then terror, as its fingers close with vice-like strength. I stumble back, appalled, and its massive shoulders hide the rest from me. But I hear the low crack as her skull breaks. Her life, which lasted only a minute, is snuffed out. It steps away with a growl almost of distaste, gore dripping from its great hands.his fingers close with vice-like strength. I stumble back, appalled, and his massive shoulders hide the rest from me. But I hear the low crack as her skull breaks. Her life, which lasted only a minute, is snuffed out. He steps away with a growl almost of distaste, gore dripping from his great hands.

xoptx 'Why?'

xoptx 'But now you are alone!'

It turns to me in the dim light, and itsHe turns to me in the dim light, and his face is an unreadable mask. How could I ever have thought I created a new species of man? This thing before me is utterly alien.
'It was enough to make you do as I wanted. Now you know which of us is the master and which the slave.'
'This is senseless... insane. You told me you suffered from being outcast, and now you ensure you will be forever alone.'
AdomThe monster moves tentatively towards her. My heart freezes as I see her dart a look towards ithim and, even before emotion registers on her lovely face, I know what the reaction will be.
Her eyes go wide. The mouth drops open - she doesn't know how to use the vocal cords yet, and the sound she makes is like the angry hiss a swan makes when threatened.
Adom lunges as she goes to turn away, his massive hand gripping her by the slender wrist. She utters a strangled sound and rakes her nails across his face. The nails grew long while she was floating in the tank, and they draw blood.
'So you also reject me,' says Adom with a kind of groan.The monster lunges as she goes to turn away, his massive hand gripping her by the slender wrist. She utters a strangled sound and rakes her nails across his face. The nails grew long while she was floating in the tank, and they draw blood.
'So you also reject me,' says the monster with a kind of groan. It has his back to me, so I cannot see its face, but I sense a change in the squaring of his shoulders. It tightens its He has his back to me, so I cannot see his face, but I sense a change in the squaring of his shoulders. He tightens his grip on her wrist. The expression in her eyes is wild. An animal fights when it is afraid. She rakes her nails towards his eyes, but now he has both her arms.
'No...' I can barely even hear my own voice, it is so weak. I look away, but even with my hands pressed to my ears I can't shut out the terrible sounds.
The monster appears before me. He has something flopping in his hand which he casts into the corner of the room. I am glad of the dim lamplight.

Good God, it's killed her.

Good God, he's killed her.

She sees Adom. Does she recognize a kinship? Hesitantly she circles She sees the creature. Does she recognize a kinship? Hesitantly she circles it, transfixed by itshim, transfixed by his appearance. Then, seeming to notice me for the first time, she compares us, her eyes flicking rapidly from one to the other.
'Speak to her,' I tell Adom.
'Come,' the creature.
'Come,' it says, extending its hand. 'You are mine.'
Her head jerks back - an expression of surprise. Curious. She did not react when I spoke, only when it did. Then it comes to me. Of course. She considers ithe says, extending his hand. 'You are mine.'
Her head jerks back - an expression of surprise. Curious. She did not react when I spoke, only when he did. Then it comes to me. Of course. She considers him a monster.
Nonetheless she copies his gesture, raising her own huge yellow hand, with its weals of puckered scar tissue, towards the extended fingers whose red pads of muscle, glistening sinews and fretwork of bones are visible beneath the translucent flesh.
And she screams.

It's the sight of her own hand.

The screaming doesn't stop. She claws at her face, and despite her grotesquely twisted features it is easy to read the expression of dawning horror as she realizes she's a monster.
Suddenly she's silent. AdomThe creature and I stand frozen. For my part, I am chilled - not so much by the unearthly sound she made, as by the horrible understanding that drew it from her. It takes me a moment too long to react as I see her hand go to the bench beside her.
The scalpel catches a splinter of dazzling light from the lantern, only to be instantly dimmed by the swift flow of synthesized blood. She falls, pulling the bench down on top of her with a crash of chemical instruments, as the pool of blood spreads rapidly across the floor. The scalpel drops from her twitching fingers, her yellow eyes glaze over, and I don't need to feel for a pulse.

She's dead.

'Am I destined, then, to be rejected by all?' Standing over her body, Adom throws back 'Am I destined, then, to be rejected by all?' Standing over her body, the monster throws back its head and utters a terrible forlorn cry.
'She couldn't live with being a monster.'
It turns a bleak look towards me. 'And if I had the courage, I might seek the same release?'
'Perhaps you should.'
It follows my gaze to the blood-stained scalpel at our feet. It stoops to pick it up. Slowly, with great deliberation, it walks to the door. 'I won't die yet. The world is dreadful, but I'm not ready to leave it.'
'Then where will you go?'
I feel a momentary stab of hope that I might be rid of it. That hope is soon dashed when it turns and I see the look burning in those uncanny eyes. I made those eyes, but it's only at this moment that I finally realize I have conception of how the world looks through them.
'Farewell, creator. I will be with you on your wedding night.' It gives me a short nod and vanishes into the night. Following, I see it putting out in a boat. Ithis head and utters a terrible forlorn cry.
'She couldn't live with being a monster.'
He turns a bleak look towards me. 'And if had the courage, I might seek the same release?'
'Perhaps you should.'
He follows my gaze to the blood-stained scalpel at our feet. He stoops to pick it up. Slowly, with great deliberation, he walks to the door. 'I won't die yet. The world is dreadful, but I'm not ready to leave it.'
'Then where will you go?'
I feel a momentary stab of hope that I might be rid of him. That hope is soon dashed when he turns and I see the look burning in those uncanny eyes. I made those eyes, but it's only at this moment that I finally realize I have conception of how the world looks through them.
'Farewell, creator. I will be with you on your wedding night.' He gives me a short nod and vanishes into the night. Following, I see him putting out in a boat. He hunches with great strength over the oars, and the boat shoots out across the water and is soon just a distant speck under the immensity of the moonlit sky.

It's gone.

He's gone.

'Am I destined, then, to be rejected by all?' Standing over her body, the monster throws back its head and gives vent to a howl to chill the blood.
'She killed herself,' I say, feeling behind my back for another scalpel. 'You could do the same.'
It steps closer. So close I can smell the bitter acidic reek of its alien breath. The scalpel - I can't find it -
'Perhaps I will,' it says, 'when I find myself at quits with the world. Until then, there is a balance sheet of afflictions that I will have reparation for.'
'If your life is hell, monster, you have made it so.'
It raises the scalpel I was searching for. Our eyes lock. Its'Am I destined, then, to be rejected by all?' Standing over her body, the monster throws back his head and gives vent to a howl to chill the blood.
'She killed herself,' I say, feeling behind my back for another scalpel. 'You could do the same.'
He steps closer. So close I can smell the bitter acidic reek of his alien breath. The scalpel - I can't find it -
'Perhaps I will,' he says, 'when I find myself at quits with the world. Until then, there is a balance sheet of afflictions that I will have reparation for.'
'If your life is hell, monster, you have made it so.'
He raises the scalpel I was searching for. Our eyes lock. His teeth gape in an unholy grin. I am beyond terror. 'Kill me, then. No doubt that is your intention.'
The steel flashes once in the lamplight and gives a sharp ringing chime as it bounces off the floor. 'Death like that is swift and kind. Would I put you at rest like your brother? No, to the last I'll walk with thee, creator. We'll be twins in misery, and you may be assured I will be there on your wedding night.'
Turning, it moves swiftly to the door and is swallowed up in the night. Rushing to the window, I see it putting out in a boat. Ithe moves swiftly to the door and is swallowed up in the night. Rushing to the window, I see him putting out in a boat. He hunches with great strength over the oars, and the boat shoots out across the water and is soon just a distant speck under the immensity of the moonlit sky.

It's gone.

He's gone.

'You are to blame, Frankenstein! Your arrogance would not let you create an imperfect woman... one who could be content with such a wretch as I. No, you had to make her beautiful.'
To my astonishment, there are tears on itshis great raw-sinewed cheeks.
'I see a pattern here, monster. You rampage through the world, spreading carnage and suffering in your wake, and it is the fault of any man but yourself.'
The scalpel is in my hand. Could I do it? I know where to strike. It would probably dismember me in its death throes, but that would be an end to it.
It sees. I waited too long. ItsHe would probably dismember me in his death throes, but that would be an end to it.
He sees. I waited too long. His huge hand reaches down and takes the scalpel from me with firm gentleness. A parent taking a dangerous toy from a child. There is a single clear ringing note as it lands in the corner of the room.
'That's not the kind of weapon for us. Our war is waged with misery.'
'Why does it have to be war?'
'Because you animated me, but you neglected to give me a life.'
For a long moment it looks down at me and I think, despite its words, that the killing blow must fall. Instead ithe looks down at me and I think, despite his words, that the killing blow must fall. Instead he turns and moves towards the door.
'Then go. Leave me alone.'
'Alone? Never. We are together to the end, creature and creator. I go now, but I will be with you on your wedding night.'

xoptx Wait! What does it mean by that?

xoptx Let it go.

xoptx Wait! What does he mean by that?

xoptx Let him go.

'Villain! Before you sign my death warrant, be sure that you are yourself safe.'
'If all I wanted was your death, Frankenstein, why would I not kill you now?' It leans closer and I smell the odd, bitter chemical smell of itsHe leans closer and I smell the odd, bitter chemical smell of his breath. 'I could tear you into pieces as easily as I did her.'
'It is you who should be dead. What a vile monstrosity you are - as deformed in your soul as in your body. You don't belong on this Earth.'
'There is one place I belong. With the one who conceived me. Our misery will continue to unite us.'
Anxious to understand the import of its words, I lunge forward and try to seize it, but ithis words, I lunge forward and try to seize him, but he throws me down as casually as a man shrugging off a cloak.
I forgot how fast it can move. In a few moments I see it in his boat, which shoots out across the water and is soon just a distant speck under the immensity of the moonlit sky.
The silence returns, but its words still ring in my ears. I burn with rage, my mind teeming with fantasies in which I pursue the murderer, cut itI forgot how fast he can move. In a few moments I see him in his boat, which shoots out across the water and is soon just a distant speck under the immensity of the moonlit sky.
The silence returns, but his words still ring in my ears. I burn with rage, my mind teeming with fantasies in which I pursue the murderer, cut him apart, and scatter the pieces into the ocean. I pace the gore-spattered floor while my imagination conjures up a thousand regrets to torment and sting me.

xoptx You'll never get another opportunity to slay it.

xoptx You'll never get another chance to slay him.

xoptx It is gone now.

xoptx He is gone now.

I know that! You could have spoken sooner. If I had acted then, I might have prevailed. Despite its strength, I am its creator and I know where to strike to sever arteries. There would have been a chance - perhaps slim, but a chance - of returning ithis strength, I am his creator and I know where to strike to sever arteries. There would have been a chance - perhaps slim, but a chance - of returning him to a lifeless state. Instead you stayed silent and let the moment slip away.

And what will the monster do now?

'Alone as you will be, Frankenstein, when I have stripped away your companions and you see the world as I do.'
'But I did it. I gave you a mate. You needn't have stayed as you are...'
'A monster?' It seizes me, itsHe seizes me, his strength so overwhelming as to make opposition unthinkable. 'I am a monster! Why should I aspire to be as thou? Gladly I oppose against the throne and monarchy of Man. Am I cast out? Then so I choose. I want no companion, no aping union. Man takes the promising chemicals and body parts of love and creates of them a travesty - a choking, vitiating thing - and that I won't abide. Alone I am, a new Adam.'
It releases itsHe releases his grip and I stagger back, falling over the carcass of the female creature.

But why?

'Then why make me create her? To what end?'
'To show that I am the one with the power to create and destroy. This hand - here is thy future, Frankenstein, here in my palm. I can shape events, dispense misery or joy. All your future years are here - ' ItHe closes the hand into a fist.
'What do you mean? If you don't want me to make you a companion, then what?'
'All your knowledge, and you understand nothing. I am determined to be the thing you made me. Our fates are sealed together. I will be with you from this moment on, Frankenstein, as I have been with you since that night in the thunderstorm. Look for your shadow, and there I'll always be.'
It walks to the door, crunching broken glass from the table underfoot. As it surveys the broken form of the female for the last time, itHe walks to the door, crunching broken glass from the table underfoot. As he surveys the broken form of the female for the last time, he says, 'She's not the first virgin sacrificed on the altar of matrimony. Farewell, Frankenstein. I will be with you on your wedding night.'

It's gone.

He's gone.

My bedroom is dark, but I don't feel like lighting a lamp. Outside the window the sea lies motionless, with hardly a breath of wind to disturb the dead silence. The moon shines down from a cloudless indigo sky. I feel like the only living thing in creation.
Hours pass. There is the creak of hinges as the outer door is slowly opened. All at once I'm trembling. I want to call out - there's a cottage not far from mine, and if they hear me they will come to help. But my throat is paralyzed. It's like one of those nightmares when you try in vain to run, to cry out, but you are held helpless by fright.

Then you can only wait to confront your monster.

The floorboards creak under a heavy tread advancing along the passage. The door opens. The monster stoops, its hulking frame filling the doorway. With inexorable, unhurried strides it crosses the floor to within a foot of me, effortlessly swatting aside a heavy chair that stands in its way. I am powerless to move, even when I hear the tone of bleak despair in itshis hulking frame filling the doorway. With inexorable, unhurried strides he crosses the floor to within a foot of me, effortlessly swatting aside a heavy chair that stands in his way. I am powerless to move, even when I hear the tone of bleak despair in his smothered voice:
'Do you dare to break your promise? I have endured years of anguish. I left Switzerland with you. I crept along the banks of the Rhine, among its willow islands, and over the summits of its hills. I have dwelt many months in the heaths and forests of Britain. I have endured fatigue, and cold, and hunger. And now you destroy my hopes?'

xoptx Tell it you never intended to animate her.

xoptx Tell it you knew it couldn't be trusted.

xoptx Tell it you'll never do as it asks.

xoptx Tell him you never intended to animate her.

xoptx Tell him you knew he couldn't be trusted.

xoptx Tell him you'll never do as he asks.

'I would have left,' it'I would have left,' he says. 'You would have been rid of me. Why lead me to this remote spot if you never meant to keep your promise?'
'To buy time, fiend, and entice you away from those I care about. If I'd refused you in the ice cave, you would have cast my body down into that crevasse.'
'You are to be wed. You will have your warm mate to hold. Only I am doomed to be alone.'
'As ever, it is your own emotion that you impute to me.'
'Demon - what do you mean by that?'
'Treachery, ingratitude, loathing, guilt... Where did I acquire those? In the filthy workshop of my creator's mind. All men spurn me. You, who should love me, abjure my very existence and deny me the little happiness it is in your power to give. And then you say that I cannot be trusted.'
ItsHis self-pity, which I had not expected, frees me from the paralysis of fear. In its place I feel a surge of anger, long pent up, at the torment this vile wretch has subjected me to.
'Get out. You're wasting your time. I'll never create another like yourself, equal in deformity and wickedness.'
'Slave, before I was prepared to reason with you, but you have proved yourself unworthy of my condescension. Remember that I have power. You believe yourself miserable? Not yet. I can make you so wretched that you will whimper to see the sun rise, knowing that it rises on another day of misery. You are my creator, but I am your master. Obey!'

xoptx Show that you too can make threats.

xoptx Why prolong this? Just tell it to get out.

xoptx Why prolong this? Just tell him to get out.

'I was weak,' I say. 'I admit it. But no longer. You have no power over me now. All your threats can achieve is to strengthen my resolve not to create you a companion with whom to procreate your wicked breed across the globe. Shall I, in cold blood, set loose upon the Earth a demon, whose delight is in death and cruelty? Get out. If you say another word you will only exasperate my rage, and then beware in case I uncreate what I have created.'
The monster reads the determination in my face, and gnashes itshis teeth in impotent anger. 'Shall each man find a wife for his bosom, and each beast have his mate, and I be alone? I had feelings of affection, and they were repaid with detestation and scorn. Man, you may hate - but you beware. Your hours will pass in dread and misery, and soon the bolt will fall which will destroy your happiness forever. Are you to be happy while I grovel in the intensity of my wretchedness? You can blast my other passions, but revenge remains - revenge, henceforth dearer to me than warmth or food! I may die - perhaps you know a way to destroy me - but first you, my tyrant and tormentor, shall curse the sun that gazes on your misery. Beware, for I am fearless and therefore powerful. I will watch with the wiliness of a snake, that I may sting with its venom. Man, you shall repent of the injuries you inflict.'

There's no point in discussing this any more.

'Devil, cease, and do not poison the air with these sounds of malice. I have declared my resolution to you, and I am no coward to bend beneath words. Leave me. I am inexorable.'
Seeming to consider this, it turns slowly and in silence towards the door, giving me an instant in which I feel I might have the upper hand. But then I see itshe turns slowly and in silence towards the door, giving me an instant in which I feel I might have the upper hand. But then I see his eyes.
'Yes, I will go. But remember - I shall be with you on your wedding night.'

xoptx What does it mean by that?

xoptx Never mind, as long as it's leaving now.

'Villain! Before you sign my death warrant, be sure that you are yourself safe.'
'If all I wanted was your death, why would I not kill you now?' It leans closer and I smell the odd bitter chemical smell of itsHe leans closer and I smell the odd bitter chemical smell of his breath. 'I could tear you into pieces, as you cut up the one who was to be my companion.'
'It is you who should be dead. A thing like you - you don't belong on this Earth.'
'There is one place I belong. With the one who conceived me. Our misery will continue to unite us.'
Anxious to understand the import of its words, I lunge forward and try to seize it, but ithis words, I lunge forward and try to seize him, but he throws me down as casually as a man shrugging off a cloak.
I forgot how fast it can move. In a few moments I see it in its boat, which shoots out across the water and is soon just a distant speck under the immensity of the moonlit sky.
The silence returns, but its words still ring in my ears. I burn with rage, my mind teeming with fantasies in which I pursue the murderer, cut itI forgot how fast he can move. In a few moments I see him in his boat, which shoots out across the water and is soon just a distant speck under the immensity of the moonlit sky.
The silence returns, but his words still ring in my ears. I burn with rage, my mind teeming with fantasies in which I pursue the murderer, cut him apart, and scatter the pieces into the ocean. I stalk up and down my room while my imagination conjures up a thousand regrets to torment and sting me.

xoptx You missed your best chance to slay it.

xoptx At least it is gone now.

xoptx You missed your best hope of killing him.

xoptx At least he is gone now.

I know. Why didn't I follow it? I have the scalpel. Despite its strength, I am its creator and I know where to strike to sever arteries. There would have been a chance - perhaps slim, but a chance - of returning itI know. Why didn't I follow him? I have the scalpel. Despite his strength, I am his creator and I know where to strike to sever arteries. There would have been a chance - perhaps slim, but a chance - of returning him to a lifeless state. Instead I stood petrified, vehement enough in my speech but with limbs as weak as water, dreaming of the act but failing even to try.

And what will it do now?

And what will he do now?

Its course was towards the mainland. I shudder to think who might be the next victim sacrificed to its insatiate revenge. And then there are its parting words. 'I will be with you on your wedding night.' Just as, in its eyes, I deprived it of the chance of a happy life with a mate, is that when it finally intends to bring about my end? His course was towards the mainland. I shudder to think who might be the next victim sacrificed to his insatiate revenge. And then there are his parting words. 'I will be with you on your wedding night.' Just as, in his eyes, I deprived him of the chance of a happy life with a mate, is that when he finally intends to bring about my end?

xoptx That must be it.

xoptx Obviously the fiend intends to harm Elizabeth.

xoptx Obviously he intends to harm Elizabeth.

Iniquitous creature! Prince of foulness! Yes, as it perceives Elizabeth to be the soulmate with whom my happiness would be assured, without doubt it will seek to strike at me by threatening her.
It will not find me unprepared. For all its strength, and the energy provided by its envious hatred of me, which of us is greater? I am Frankenstein! I wrested the secret of life from the locked heart of creation. I will not be the victim of my own experiment. Let the monster beware. Its creator may now become its nemesis.Iniquitous creature! Prince of foulness! Yes, as he perceives Elizabeth to be the soulmate with whom my happiness would be assured, without doubt he will seek to strike at me by threatening her.
He will not find me unprepared. For all his strength, and the energy provided by his envious hatred of me, which of us is greater? I am Frankenstein! I wrested the secret of life from the locked heart of creation. I will not be the victim of my own experiment. Let the monster beware. His creator may now become his nemesis.

I shudder to think who might be the next victim sacrificed to its insatiate revenge. And then there are its parting words. 'I will be with you on your wedding night.' Is that when it finally intends to kill me? I shudder to think who might be the next victim sacrificed to his insatiate revenge. And then there are his parting words. 'I will be with you on your wedding night.' Is that when he finally intends to kill me?

xoptx That must be it.

xoptx Obviously the fiend intends to harm Elizabeth.

xoptx Obviously he intends to harm Elizabeth.

That then is the period fixed for the fulfilment of my destiny. In that hour I will die, and at once satisfy and extinguish its malice. I feel no fear for myself. Ithis malice. I feel no fear for myself. He has made my life so wretched with guilt and grief that to leave it will be a blessing.
But Elizabeth - what will she feel, to find her betrothed so barbarously snatched from her? When I lift the rock of the monster's cruelty, what intricate and fiendish plans are revealed. But there's no sense in these tears. Am I weeping for what might have been? I still have a future. I have the mind that enabled me to do what no man had achieved. The same determination. Only guilt can make me helpless - revenge will give me strength. I am resolved not to fall before my enemy without a bitter struggle.

Let us hope you can prevail.

The night passes away, the sun rises from the ocean, and with the approach of day my feelings become calmer - if it may be called calmness, when the violence of passion sinks into the depths of despair. I leave the house and walk far along the shore. If only I could spend my life on this barren rock. If I return, it will be to my destruction, or to see those whom I most love die at the hands of a demon that I myself created.whom I myself created.

END OF PART FOUR


PART FIVE

May my fears,
My filial fears, be vain! and may the vaunts
And menace of the vengeful enemy
Pass like the gust, that roared and died away
In the distant tree: which heard, and only heard
In this low dell, bowed not the delicate grass.

CHAPTER ONE
The Moment of Darkness

I walk about the island, a restless spectre. At noon, with the sun beating down strongly despite the chill wind off the sea, I am abruptly overcome by fatigue. Stretching out on the grass, I soon fall into a deep sleep, which restores me, and on awakening towards sunset I feel again as if I belong to a race of human beings like myself.
Now I am able to reflect with greater composure on what has passed, but still Adomthe fiend's words ring in my ears like a death-knell. I will be with you on your wedding night. Already it is a phrase that has taken on the character of a pronouncement in one of Perrault's fairy tales, doomy and yet dreamlike, with a dream's ability to be both more and less tangible than life.
A fishing boat arrives with a packet of letters for me. There is one from Elizabeth and one from Henri.

xoptx Read Elizabeth's.

xoptx Read Henri's.

xoptx They will keep. You have things you must do first.

She hopes I will not strain my eyes by reading too late. Also that I am eating well, getting enough sleep, and so on and so forth. She knows me well enough by now that these sentiments are merely a token. But what are all these pages after the signature? Good heavens, she has copied out an entire chapter on the work of Galvani that she found in a book and thinks might interest me. I read it years ago, of course, but it's touching that she is making an effort to support my interests.. Really she has alarmingly little conception of my work.

xoptx And what about Henri's letter?

xoptx It's time to pack if you mean to leave today.

It's time to pack if you mean to leave today.

Dear Henri - he entreats me to join him. 'Nearly a year since we left home...' Et cetera. 'More of the continent to see than this - ' conspicuously crossed out, replaced with that - 'benighted isle.' Ah, he sets a date for us to rendezvous in Perth. That's a week from now. Well, his timing could not be better. As soon as I've taken care of things here, I shall be ready to set out.

You'd better get on, then.

xoptx You'd better get on, then.

xoptx What about Elizabeth's letter?

I have to steel myself for this, but I can't pack my apparatus without going back into the workshop. It takes me until daybreak to summon up the courage. The remains of the female creature lie on the floor. With trembling hands I lift her - so light! - into a basket, which I put in my sloop. For good measure I fill it with stones from the beach. That can go overboard tonight. For the rest of the day I sit out on a bench in front of the cottage in the blazing white sunshine, cleaning and arranging my utensils.

xoptx Will you ever resume your work, do you think?

xoptx Or are you done with it now?

I will never go back to it. Perhaps before I regarded my promise to Adomthe demon with a gloomy despair, as something that must be fulfilled or, at best, somehow vaguely postponed. I suppose my old obsession still burned, that in my heart I still yearned to penetrate those secrets nature keeps hidden from us, and everything else was just an excuse. But now the scales are gone from my eyes. I will never create another creature like that one.fiend like that one.

xoptx Not even to save your life?

xoptx Then it is time to leave here.

Oh, you mean the threat. It weighs on my thoughts, of course. But I do not suppose that any voluntary act of mine can avert it. If my creation means to destroy me, then one or both of us is destined to die. We can put aside all the pretences now. This is - has always been - simply about it and me.him and me.

Are you ready to leave?

It is unlikely, I think. And yet... I overreached myself. Too young and too driven, I sought to do everything myself and steal all of the apples of the Hesperides with one sweep of the hand. I'm wiser now. This experiment of the female was misconceived, but if I were to make an infant, less monstrous than the original creature, it might be possible to instil in it moral precepts from the beginning.
But these are speculations for the future. They must wait until the matter between it and me is settled.him and me is settled.

Then are you ready to set off?

Not quite yet. I must wait for the moon to come up. At about two in the morning I cast off.The moon rises around two in the morning, and I cast off. There are a few other boats on the water but I steer well clear of them. At a point some four miles out, feeling as if I am carrying out a dreadful crime, I heave the basket over the side. At first, despite the weight of stones, it only bobs on the waves and I feel growing panic. Then bubbles start to stream up around it and with a gurgling sound it sinks from view. I breathe again.
As I sail on, the sky begins to cloud over. A strengthening breeze blows from the northeast and the sea becomes choppy. We could be in for quite a storm.

xoptx Sail on.

xoptx Turn back.

The sun is up when I wake. I don't remember dozing off. The wind is strong and steady now, tearing the spray off the whitecaps all around. There's no sight of land in any direction. My first thought is to change course, but I can see that I'm more likely to capsize the boat than to achieve anything else. The only thing for it is to run before the wind until it blows itself out.

xoptx This looks bad.

xoptx You'll be fine.

Nonsense. I'm an excellent sailor - I've been going out on the lake at home since I was eight years old. I am surprised and disappointed that after all this time you so little know me that you think I would timidly flee back to dry land at the first gust of wind. In fact the breeze is quite refreshing. I shall set the tiller and sit back a while, luxuriating in this all too brief period when, under the timeless roof of the stars, I can forget the cares that freight my life.

If you say so.

I am a good sailor but I don't know these waters, so perhaps you're right. Turning about, I return to the island and wait to go back with the next packet boat. The skipper is an old fisherman whose face is so leathery and lined that I would reject it as a component for building one of my creations.
'Bit different from the wee ponds ye'd be used to back home, no doubt,' are the only words he speaks to me on the entire voyage, this statement being accompanied by a laconic nod that seems to imply ownership of the entire Pentland Firth.

Immeasurable grey leagues stretch all around me, the waters roaring and buffeting my little vessel. I brought no compass, and am so little acquainted with the geography of this part of the world that the sun is of little use.
How long did I sleep? Perhaps twelve hours - I was still exhausted from my sleepless night when Adomthe monster came. Given the strength of the wind, and if it hasn't changed direction in all that time, I could be a hundred miles out into the Atlantic. Already I feel the torment of a burning thirst, a prelude to my other sufferings.
I look to the heavens, which are covered by clouds that fly before the wind only to be replaced by others. I look upon the sea and wonder if it is to be my grave. 'Fiend,' I exclaim, 'your task is already fulfilled!' I think of Elizabeth, of my father, and of Henri, and sink back in the boat. Helplessness has robbed me of my strength. My only companions are fear and despair.

You can only trust to providence.

You can only trust to providence.

You're right. I am a skilled sailor, and whatever my other faults I do not want for physical courage. Even with the waters crashing and foaming all around, and the lines taut to bursting point, I can bring her through this.

Arriving in Perth, I head to the lodging house where Henri and I arranged to meet. I hope I haven't kept him waiting. With my grisly task behind me, my thoughts are filled with plans for where we should travel next.
The manager gives me a cheerful greeting, but his manner becomes uncomfortable when I sign my name in his book. He hands me a note that requests me to call on a Mr Kirkdale, at an address in the town.
'And who is Mr Kirkdale?'
'He's a magistrate.'
He pretends to find something of importance in his ledgers. He is being very evasive.

xoptx Demand that he tells you what's going on.

xoptx Go and see this man Kirkdale.

Hours pass. By degrees, as the sun dips towards the horizon, the wind drops to a gentle breeze and the sea becomes free from breakers. But these give place to a heavy swell. Feeling sick, I am hardly able to hold the rudder when suddenly I see a line of high land towards the south. And so I arrive safely on the coast of Scotland.

Hours pass. By degrees, as the sun dips towards the horizon, the wind drops to a gentle breeze and the sea becomes free from breakers. But these give place to a heavy swell. Feeling sick, I am hardly able to hold the rudder when suddenly I see a line of high land towards the south. After the fatigue and dreadful suspense of the last few hours, this sudden certainty of life rushes like a flood of warm joy to my heart, and my view of the approaching coastline is blurred by tears
How mutable are our feelings, and how strange is that clinging love we have of life even in the excess of misery. The sail is torn, but I contrive another with my cloak. The shore has a wild and rocky appearance but is obviously more populated than the barren isle I left. There are vessels near the shore, and find myself suddenly transported back to the neighbourhood of civilised man.
I follow the coast and soon the steeple of a church appears from behind a small promontory. It stands in a neat town of whitewashed houses. Exhausted and thirsty, I sail directly into the harbour, my heart bounding with joy at my unexpected escape.
Several fishermen saunter over as I am fixing the boat and arranging the sails. Hearing them speak English, I ask them in that language where I have been swept to.
'He wants to know the name of this place, is that it?' says one old man.
'It's Carrick,' says a younger man.
'In Scotland?'
He laughs. 'Some navigators you foreigners are. It's Ireland, man.'

xoptx Find a place to stay.

xoptx Better unload your chemical apparatus first.

It's your friend, I'm afraid, Mr Frankenstein.'
'Monsieur Clerval? What about him?'
'He met with an accident.'
I look at him stunned, reading his grave expression clearly enough, and hear myself saying, 'Is he all right?'
'He is at peace. I'm sorry.'

I find myself outside Kirkdale's house, the note with his address in my hand. I feel like a sleepwalker, hardly aware of how I got here. After a few minutes in a gloomy dark-panelled study, I hear the door open and look up into the face of an kindly looking man.
'I am Monsieur Clerval's friend,' I tell him, though he must know that already. 'I'd like to know how he died.'
Kirkdale has a fine old stone house on the high street. I'm shown into a room whose dark panels drink in the bright sunlight outside. My host sees me at once, and as soon as he extends his hand I can tell he has bad news.
'It concerns your friend, Mr Clavell.''Clerval. I thought so.'
'I'm afraid he is dead.'
He tells you that Henri was found on the mud by the river at low tide, just two days ago.
'We were supposed to meet here,' I say. 'We are on a tour of the British Isles.'
'Yes, so he mentioned to the owner of your lodging house.'
'How did he die? Did he drown?'
'Drown?' Kirkdale removes his glasses and breathes on the lenses before polishing them. 'He hadn't fallen into the river, Mr Frankenstein. I'm sorry, I thought you'd been told. He was beaten to death.'
'Where is the body?'
'You don't need to identify him. We have his possessions and the manager's statement for that. I say this because - well, never a pretty sight, a dead body, but it may not be the image you'd like to remember.'

xoptx Insist on seeing him.

xoptx Perhaps the magistrate is right.

I enter the room where Henri's coffin lies. How can I describe my sensations? I am parched with horror. Kirkdale seems to become like smoke, blown away. The room is darkness. There is nothing else in creation except for me and the broken, lifeless form of Henri Clerval, my lifelong friend. I reach out to touch his hand and, feeling the terrible coldness where there will never be warmth again, I give an anguished cry. 'My dearest friend, I did this to you!'
Kirkdale tries to lead me away. Perhaps pull me away. His voice is saying I mustn't blame myself, mustn't blame myself.
'Then who should I blame? God? The devil? He is my third victim. How Death must laugh - I set out to conquer him, but all I have done is consign three people to his care! And there will be more!'
I'm not sure what happens next. I think I try to embrace the corpse. There is shouting. Men burst into the room and drag me away from poor Henri's side. Someone says, 'He's lost his wits!'
I suppose my nerves simply spool out beyond their endurance. The darkness at the edge of my vision grows to swallow me and then I vanish too, mere smoke like Kirkdale and the others, and there is nothing at all left except for the baleful, invisible, but palpable presence of the monster.I enter the room where Henri's coffin lies. How can I describe my sensations on beholding his face? It is as though, far away, there is a tumult of great anguish but I am in the eye of the storm and can hear and feel nothing.
The undertaker has worked his art skilfully, but there is no disguising the tracery of lacerations under a heavy caking of powder. I lean forward. Kirkdale puts his hand on my arm.
'I am a doctor,' I tell him.
'Even so, sir, please.'
I have seen enough to know that my friend was bludgeoned to death. A very horrible and sustained attack that must have gone on long after he lost consciousness.
'We think several men... They may have been disturbed while trying to dispose of the body, as no money was taken.' As we leave the room, he says, 'Do you have any idea who killed your friend, Mr Frankenstein?'
'Oh yes,' I say calmly. 'I did.''None at all.'

xoptx 'What is being done to find the killer?'

xoptx Go back to your lodgings.

Kirkdale shows me to the door. My pulse is racing and the hall seems to press in on me, hot and heavy. I pull away from his handshake and hurry out into the fresh air, desperate to get away. Except that, from the thing that pursues me, I know there is no escape.

'We think he was killed by more than one man,' says Kirkdale.
'What makes you say that?'
'The extent of his injuries.' He spreads his hands, doesn't want to talk about it. 'Are you all right, Mr Frankenstein?'
'The shock... I think I'd better go and lie down.'
'Of course.'
He shows me to the door. My pulse is racing and the hall seems to press in on me, hot and heavy. I pull away from his handshake and hurry out into the fresh air, desperate to get away. Except that, from the thing that pursues me, I know there is no escape.

'Give you a hand?' offers a young man with a smiling, freckled face.
'Thank you.' I gather my clothes and notebooks, and as he jumps down into the boat I nod towards the tarpaulin in the bow. 'There are some boxes there I'll need delivered to my lodgings.'
I'm getting out of the boat when I sense a sudden tension in the crowd. They're staring past me. I turn and see the young man, his freckles now standing out on a very pale face, hastily lowering the tarpaulin.
'What is it?' I say.
He gives me a stricken look, then seems to recover his wits. 'Grab him!' he says. And several onlookers step forward to seize my arms.

xoptx Demand they unhand you.

xoptx Ask what is under the tarpaulin.

'Come on, now, sir,' says one of the men holding me. 'You're going to have to explain yourself to Mr Kirwan.'
'Who is Kirwan? Why should I explain myself? Isn't this a free country?'
'For honest folks it is.'
As you are led away, you hear a woman saying, 'He must be a madman. What else would explain it?'

'You know that well enough, villain,' says one of the men holding me.
'How dare you. I am Victor Frankenstein, a gentleman of Geneva, and I demand to know by what right you detain me.'
'You should get a lynching,' says another man, 'but we're civilized folk. So you can come along and explain to the magistrate how come you've got a dead body in your boat.'
'And then you'll get a lynching!' screeches a toothless old woman, to the primitive amusement of the gathering crowd.

I take lodgings at an inn near the wharf and spend the afternoon planning my itinerary to rendezvous with Henri in Perth. The town of Carrick, though pleasant enough, is rather isolated, so getting by carriage to Belfast is likely to take longer than the sea voyage around the coast of Scotland. I could take my sloop, of course, but the storm has quashed my enthusiasm for sailing.
That reminds me, I had better fetch my equipment. I am on the point of ringing a bell to get some servants from the inn to help me, but there will be plenty of willing workers down at the quayside.

xoptx Of course there will.

xoptx Why go yourself? Send some servants.

It is late afternoon by the time I return to the quay. At first I think I may have left it too late to hire anyone to help me carry my things. A moment later I'm very glad I didn't, as sticking out from the tarpaulin in the bow of the boat is a limp white hand.

xoptx It is the female monster!

xoptx Call for help.

xoptx See who it is.

Half an hour later, the servant I sent comes bursting into the room with several other men. 'That's him!' he cries, pointing at me. Before I can protest or even question what is going on, they have laid hold of me and I am dragged from my chair.
'What is the meaning of this? Innkeeper, fetch the police.'
'They are the polis, sir.' He turns to the roughest-looking of the men. 'What are you about, McCluskey? Manhandling this gentleman?'
'Gentleman, is it? I'd say a bloody murderer is another word. Come on, you - we'll see what Mr Kirwan has to say to you.'

You'll have to go with them.

Ridiculous. I disposed of her. Didn't I? I placed the remains in the basket. You saw me weight it with stones. Did I put it in the ocean? I saw it sink in the moonlight. I can still hear the ghastly sucking sound as it went down. Or did I dream that? I was exhausted. Did I sleep, imagine I had disposed of the body, and then sail on with it lying under the tarpaulin all that time ..?

Better take a look.

Hearing my cries for help, a crowd soon gathers. Steeling myself, I draw back the tarpaulin. The body is lying face down, but even as I reach to turn it over, I know. The dead face that is revealed to me is that of my dearest friend.
'Henri!'
'Do you know this man?' says a rough-looking fellow with an air of authority.
I slump in the bottom of the boat, holding poor Henri's cold white hand. The marks of the monster's fingers are still on his neck.
'I do,' I manage to sob out as tears stream down my cheeks.
'Then you'd better come with us.'
Still weeping uncontrollably, I am dragged in front of the local magistrate, a man called Kirwan. Numbly I give my name and a muddled account of how I come to be here.
'And can you explain how Mr Clerval met his demise, Mr Frankenstein.'
I don't care anymore. With Henri's death, surely Adom has attained the demon has attained itshis acme of spite against me.
'I killed him,' I reply.
And at that the darkness closes in, the room spirals into a blur around me, and I fall gratefully into the arms of oblivion.

Steeling myself, I draw back the tarpaulin. The body is lying face down, but even as I reach to turn it over, I know. The dead face that is revealed to me is Henri's.
I fall back in the bottom of the boat, having to grit my teeth to stifle a howl of anguish that would bring half the town running. Somehow I keep enough presence of mind to throw the tarpaulin back over the body before anybody should see him. The sight of those staring eyes remains imprinted on my vision. It seem as if I will carry it to my dying day.
AdomThe monster must have murdered him, anticipating a calamitous outcome to the recent experiments, and left his body here to draw the blame onto my head. Cursed fiend! Exquisitely cruel demon! To devise a strategy that has, in one stroke, robbed me of my dearest friend and nearly put my head in the noose for it.

xoptx Find a magistrate and explain what happened.

xoptx No one has seen him. Dispose of the body.

Are you mad? There is no explanation I could give that would satisfy the law I didn't kill him.
I steal out into the bay under cover of darkness. Weighting the body with an anchor will ensure it will not be found until decay and the crabs have made the face unrecognizable. Henri's wallet and other effects I take back with me and dispose of carefully, all the while feeling as though I really am the murderer.

Mr Kirwan, the local magistrate, seems a benevolent and mild-mannered old man, but his compassionate smile turns steadily to a frown as I set out my story.
'Well now, it's an extraordinary tale, Mr Frankenstein.'
He doesn't believe me. I am too sick at heart to defend myself. 'In truth, I am his murderer,' I say, trembling uncontrollably. 'I am to blame for my brother's death, too - for which another hanged, an innocent. The world would be better off without me.'
'Now I have two stories!' says Kirwan, his patience wearing thin. 'There is another murderer at large, or you committed the crime yourself?'
I lift my gaze to his. All light has gone out of the world. What does anything matter now? The room, the old man, the crackling fire in the hearth, the darkening sky outside - they blur and spin around me, and I know no more.

Mr Kirwan, the local magistrate, seems a benevolent and mild-mannered old man, but the look he gives me is one of great severity. After I give my name and a brief account of the unexpected course of events that brought me here, he demands to know if I have anything to say for myself.

Somebody said there was a body in your boat.

Ask why you have been arrested.

How can that be? I placed the female's remains in the basket. You saw me weight it with stones. Did I put it in the ocean? I saw it sink in the moonlight - I can still hear the ghastly sucking sound as it went down. Or did I dream that? I was exhausted. Did I sleep, imagine I had disposed of the body, and then sail on with it lying under the tarpaulin all that time ..?

xoptx Just explain that you're an anatomist.

xoptx Ask to see the body.

'That is your defence?' says Kirwan, sitting back in astonishment. 'That you were entirely ignorant of the dead body that was lying in the back of your boat?'
'Er, it was in the bow, your honour,' ventures McCluskey.
one of the men who dragged me here.
A body? How can that be? I placed the female's remains in the basket. You saw me weight it with stones. Did I put it in the ocean? I saw it sink in the moonlight - I can still hear the ghastly sucking sound as it went down. Or did I dream that? I was exhausted. Did I sleep, imagine I had disposed of the body, and then sail on with it lying under the tarpaulin all that time ..?

xoptx Just explain that you're an anatomist.

xoptx Ask to see the body.

'You say you came upon this specimen by legitimate means, then?' says Kirwan. 'It's a difficult story to credit, Mr Frankenstein. As a rule, surgeons don't go sailing around the Irish Sea with the cadavers they're studying.'
'I have already explained that a storm blew me off course. I was on my way back to the Scottish mainland.'
Kirwan turns to my captorone of the men who arrested me. 'What do you say, McCluskey?'
'Begging your pardon, your honour, but it don't seem this particular body was for surgical cutting and whatnot, seeing as it's fully clothed and has a wallet and watch and all the rest.'

Ask to see the body.

I am brought to the room where the corpse lies. How can I describe my sensations? I am parched with horror. The magistrate and witnesses seem to step back out of the physical space around the coffin. I and the lifeless form of Henri Clerval are the only players on the stage.
With a dreamlike dislocation, I observe my own reactions - just as, I am aware, Kirwan is observing them. Seizing the cold hand, I give an anguished cry full of guilt and regret. 'My dearest friend, I did this to you!'
Kirwan steps from the wings. I hardly notice him, so intent am I on the blank clay that used to contain the vital spirit of my friend.
'Are you confessing to this man's murder, Frankenstein?'
'Not him alone. I have a string of victims. He's the third. How Death must laugh - I set out to conquer him, but all I have done is consign three people to his care! And there will be more!'
I'm not sure what happens next. I think I try to embrace the corpse. There is shouting. Hands drag me away. Someone says, 'He's mad!' Another: 'Stop him!'
Whether a blow is struck or my nerves simply spool out beyond their endurance, I cannot say. But the darkness at the edge of my vision grows to swallow me and then there is no one on the stage but for the baleful, invisible, but palpable presence of the monster.

END OF CHAPTER ONE


CHAPTER TWO

CHAPTER TWO
Doomed to Live

So Henri is dead and I am doomed to live. AdomThe monster has learnt from its creator the painstaking application of the surgeon, for ithis creator the painstaking application of the surgeon, for he takes his revenge with deft but life-changing strokes, having first identified the place where a cut will be most effective.
I sit for a short while in my room. At least, I think it is a short while, but when the maid brings the landlord it seems I have been shut away for an entire day. Do I want to eat, I am asked. Do I have friends in the city they should send for. I hear the tone more than the words. As though they were talking to an uneasy child who might explode into a tantrum or tears at any moment.
Kirkdale sends a note about Henri's funeral. I mean to go but am still in bed at the time the service is due to begin.
ItHe has crushed me,' I write to my father. 'Be on your guard, for a doom follows everyone I love.'
Mr Kirkdale comes to see me. They have not found Henri's killer. Of course not. 'They could very well be sailors,' says Kirkdale. 'Long gone by now, in that case.'

xoptx Tell him who the murderer really is.

xoptx What's the point? Let him believe that.

I talk at length, but he isn't really listening. 'Mr Frankenstein,' he says, 'I would advise you to put all this speculation out of your head. There isn't any need to go looking for complex motives and ravening fiends and what-have-you. I'm sorry to say it, but a man who chooses Mr Clerval's way of life is going to mix with certain elements - '
'What do you mean by these insinuations? I tell you, he was killed by a creature thatwho has sworn to make me suffer.'
'Are you familiar with Occam's razor, Mr Frankenstein?'
'I am a scientist. So of course I am.'
'Well, we're scientists in our way too. And I can tell you that there's nothing in Mr Clerval's sad case that impels the law to extend its enquiries.' Kirkdale gets his hat and cane. At the door, he turns. 'If you'll forgive me saying so, I think you should see a physician. You may not be aware how much this affair has taken its toll on you.'

xoptx He's right.

xoptx Ask him about Henri's funeral.

What nonsense. My nerves are on edge because a murderous demon is out there, watching me, steadily picking off my friends and family because it has chosen to blame me for all itshe has chosen to blame me for all his ills.
It's getting dark. I'd light the lamp but then the monster would know I'm in. I daren't even draw the curtains...
Ah, perhaps you are right after all.

After a few days, having belatedly accepted that I am in no state to travel alone, I am pleased to receive a note from my father saying that he is on his way. We will travel home together.
The weeks pass slowly, the summer a turmoil of overwhelming clouds, oppressively close days, and crashing thunderstorms - or so it seems to me, for whom the sun has been forever darkened by Henri's death. Alone in a strange place, I start at every step on the path outside at night, and I both dread and hope for the heavy tread on the stairs that would end my despair.
At last, in the middle of a sweltering afternoon in which the whole city lies drugged like a patient awaiting an operation, a carriage stops in front of my lodgings. Down in the street, a familiar figure raises his hand. I smile for the first time since my interview with the magistrate, and hurry down to greet my father.
'Victor,' he says, trying to hide the concern he must feel at seeing me so drawn and pale, 'it is so good to see you, son. Terrible what happened to Clerval. A tragic accident.'

xoptx Agree.

xoptx Dispute that it was an accident.

'He was laid to rest in the Greyfriars cemetery in the middle of town here. It has recently been extended, so I was quite fortunate in securing a plot for him there.'
'You're very kind. I will of course pay all the bills.'
'Not a Catholic, I suppose, being Swiss?'
'Now that he's dead, it hardly matters, does it?'
'Berkeley would say that he is still an idea in the mind of God,' Kirkdale says, fetching his hat and cane. 'As are we all.'
I think it better to make no reply, so I simply wish him good day.

The magistrate has to investigate my claims, of course, but despite strong protestations on my part that I am responsible for Henri's death, he soon comes to other conclusions.
'Your friend frequently ventured into the, ah, let's say the seedier parts of the city,' he tells me. 'We have plenty of witnesses who can testify to that.'
We are sitting facing each other in two comfortable armchairs by a fire in a small room in his house. If not for the bars on the windows I could be a guest.
'We are not natives of this country,' I remind him. 'Henri wouldn't necessarily have realized he was straying into danger. The streets of Geneva are perfectly safe at night.'
'Come now, Mr Frankenstein, I believe your friend was a man of the world. He lived with you in Paris, did he not? And during the Reign of Terror, as it is now being called. How safe was that?'
'What of it, in any case? The murderer was watching him. When the opportunity came, ithe struck.'
'When you put it in those terms, it hardly seems you can claim to be describing yourself.'

xoptx Tell him about the monster.

xoptx Admit you weren't in the city the time.

For a long time I writhe in a fever, only dimly aware of my surroundings. My lucid moments are few, like a drowning man surfacing and gulping at the air before the water again closes over him. I lie stretched on a bed, wound in perspiration-drenched sheets, surrounded by the quiet bustling of nurses, whispered voices, bitter medicines washed down with water trickled over my parched and unresisting lips. And the endless flicker as day follows night follows day.
Why do I not die? Death snatches away many children, the only hopes of their doting parents. How many brides and youthful lovers have been one day in the bloom of health and hope, and the next a prey for worms and the decay of the tomb? Of what materials am I made, that I can thus resist so many shocks?
But I am doomed to live. And at last my mind awakes to understanding. Morning light blazes in through the window. At first I remember only the sense of a great misfortune having overwhelmed me. Then the memories fall back into place like pieces in a jigsaw, and I give a deep groan.
A maid who is clearing my bedside table looks around with a start. 'Oh sir, you're awake.'

xoptx Speak to her.

xoptx Ask to see Mr Kirkdale.

'The murderer didn't care about Henri. It wanted to strike at me by killing my dearest friend.'
'Why do you keep referring to the murderer like that? It, you say. Not he.'
'Because it is not a human being. I created it to be a man, a new kind of man, but it has forfeited its right to be considered such.'
'You created your friend's killer? You're speaking figuratively, perhaps. Some confusion between our native tongues?
'I am fluent in English, I assure you. I created the monster as a carpenter creates a cabinet.''The murderer didn't care about Henri. He wanted to strike at me by killing my dearest friend.'
'For what motive? Jealousy?'
'Jealousy, yes. He is jealous of the human race, jealous of the shrinking island of happiness which he is eroding from under me.'
'What is his name, this passionate killer?'
'Adom. The first of his kind, you see.'
'He has no name.'
'And where does he live?'
'In my shadow.'
He nods. 'Yes, I had already established that fact. So your insistence on your own guilt does seem all the more perverse.'
'Henri's death is my fault. His killer intended to wound me, and by killing my friend it has achieved its object. The sun seems robbed of warmth, food of flavour. Ithe has achieved his object. The sun seems robbed of warmth, food of flavour. He has desolated me, not only by bereaving me of my dearest friend, but by placing upon me this unsupportable weight of remorse.'
'I do sympathize. But if you think you know the identity of the murderer - and I must tell you, I doubt you do - can you tell me where we would be able to find him?'
'You will never find it. I made ithim. I made him too strong, too swift, too intelligent - qualities I thought befitted a new race of Man, but which have proved equally apt for a Devil.'
Kirkdale looks at me thoughtfully. 'I am concerned that the news of your friend's death has been more of a shock than you realize.'
'You think I'm mad.'
'I must tell you that I took the liberty of examining your papers. There was an address in Geneva, and I have written to your father. My recommendation is that you stay here until he arrives to take you home.'

xoptx 'So I am not under arrest?'

xoptx 'If I am free to leave, I will make my own way home.'

'Mr Frankenstein,' he says with a smile, 'if I were to credit every statement of guilt made to me while the speaker was distraught, I would by now have filled all the prisons of Perthshire.'
'I am guilty,' I insist, 'but not of crimes that any human court can try me for.'
'I have seen a few truly wicked men, sir. One of the defining characteristics is that they do not torture themselves with thoughts of guilt. Now, will you remain my guest until your father comes to get you?'

xoptx Agree to wait.

xoptx Travel home on your own.

Mr Kirkdale disapproves, but he has already admitted I am not a suspect. 'With your nerves in a shaken state, I feel you would do better to await your father's arrival,' he tells me.
I shake his hand. 'I must thank you for your kindness and hospitality, but I have not needed my father to see me home since I was ten years old.'
I try to make my voice sound firm and confident, but I can hear the tremor. AdomThe monster's deed has cut me to the quick, an injury to the soul from which I will never fully recover. But ithe can bleed too.
The carriage is here. Taking my leave of Kirkdale, I set out for the docks where my ship is due to sail.

In the days that follow, I have to fight a constant battle against the black melancholy that threatens to suck me down. The image of my friend is there whenever I close my eyes. His face was covered when I slid him into the water, but in my dreams he is staring blindly back at me, and as he sinks from sight the mouth opens. At that point I always wake, and often it takes several minutes to remind myself that he was definitely dead.
The cup of life is poisoned forever. Melodramatic, you think? That is how I feel. Although the sun shines upon me as upon the happy and gay of heart, I see around me nothing but a dense and frightful darkness, penetrated by no light but the glimmer of two eyes that burn me with their scrutiny. Sometimes they are the expressive eyes of Henri languishing in death, the dark orbs nearly covered by the lids and the long black lashes that fringed them. But mostly they are the lidless, cloudy eyes of the monster as I first saw them in my workshop in Paris.

It is time you went home.

For a long time I writhe in a fever, only dimly aware of my surroundings. My lucid moments are few, like a drowning man surfacing and gulping at the air before the water again closes over him. I lie stretched on a wretched bed, wound in perspiration-drenched sheets, surrounded by an endless succession of closing doors, gaolers, jangling, keys, locks, chipped tin cups of water pressed to my parched lips. And the endless flicker as day follows night follows day.
Why do I not die? Death snatches away many children, the only hopes of their doting parents. How many brides and youthful lovers have been one day in the bloom of health and hope, and the next a prey for worms and the decay of the tomb? Of what materials am I made, that I can thus resist so many shocks?
But I am doomed to live. And at last my mind awakes to understanding. Morning light blazes in through the bars of my cell. At first I remember only the sense of a great misfortune having overwhelmed me, but then the memories fall back into place like pieces in a jigsaw. I groan bitterly.
This sound disturbs a woman who is sleeping in a chair beside me. The lines of her face are hard and her tone callously empty of sympathy as she asks me, 'Feeling better now?'

xoptx Speak to her.

xoptx Ask to see Mr Kirwan.

She is a hired nurse, the wife of one of the turnkeys. I am obscurely aware that she has been attending me throughout my illness, but it is the first time I have felt in the same place and moment as my surroundings.
I start to reply in French, then feel the gears of my mind beginning to turn again. 'How long have I been here?'
'Must be almost two months. I was sure you were for the madhouse.'
'I am better now, I believe. But, remembering the misery and horror of my situation, I'm sorry I ever woke up.'
'Oh, you've been awake, right enough. Just raving the whole time, is all.'
'What did I say?'
She snorts. 'Like I'd know your fancy foreign language. Talking about the poor soul you murdered, I wouldn't wonder.'

xoptx 'His death was on my account, that's true.'

xoptx 'Fetch Mr Kirwan, please.'

'For that matter,' says the old woman, 'it would be better for you if you were dead, as you're only going to be hung when the next sessions come on, and if you pegged out from fever you'd save the expense of a trial and execution. Still, that's none of my business. I've been told to nurse you and get you fit to be strung up. I do my duty with a clear conscience, and it'd be just as well if everybody could say the same.'
I turn with loathing from the woman who can utter so unfeeling a speech to a person just rescued from the very edge of death. It is a terrible thing to wake from oblivion and have to acknowledge again the harsh truth of tragedy. Henri is dead. That catastrophe cannot be undone. I meant it when I told her I would rather have died too.

xoptx Not until you have revenged yourself.

xoptx Who will be next to die?

Yes, and for that reason only do I give thanks that my life has been spared. Last time I sank into this torment of shattered nerves, Henri was there to bring me back to sanity. Now it is mere force of will that sustains me. AdomThe monster thinks to rip away all I hold dear? It has made a fatal miscalculation. Instead of being broken by these adversities, I am tempered and made stronger. I am Frankenstein, it a mere brute created by my genius. And it will learn, in ruining my hopes, that it has created a prodigy too. A man without hope, and thus a man without fear. thinks to rip away all I hold dear? He has made a fatal miscalculation. Instead of being broken by these adversities, I am tempered and made stronger. I am Frankenstein, he a mere brute created by my genius. And he will learn, in ruining my hopes, that he has created a prodigy too. A man without hope, and thus a man without fear.

First you have to get out of here.

All the time I lay in my fever on the point of death, the faces of William and Justine and Henri have streamed before me. I felt as if the fingers of the monster were already grasping my own neck. But it has made a critical misjudgement, for what does my own life matter now that it has taken away my closest friend? It is left with no threat left to daunt me. Hope is burnt away. Now let it do its worst. There is nothing left.he has made a critical misjudgement, for what does my own life matter now that he has taken away my closest friend? He is left with no threat left to daunt me. Hope is burnt away. Now let him do his worst. There is nothing left.

First you have to get out of here.

The woman makes a pantomime of looking around the cell. 'Bless me, but I can't see where your servant has scurried off to, so I expect you'll have to wait till Mr Kirwan has time to look in.'
'Take him my message. I'm sure he'll want to know I've recovered my wits.'
'You can thank him for that. A very kind man, is Mr Kirwan, even to the worst sort of scum we get in here. See those bottles of medicine? He's had a physician in every day treating you. At his own expense, I might add.'
I pick up the bottles to examine the labels - almost knocking them to the floor as I do, because of the tremor in my hands.
'Careful,' mutters the old woman peevishly.
'Laudanum! Why not leave me a bottle of gin? Is your local physician also an innkeeper in his spare time?'
That was a mistake. Throwing me a venomous look, the old woman bustles out. The hollow sound as the bolt shoots home is like the single miserable toll of a funeral bell. Despite my opinion of the doctor's prescription, I am curious as to why Kirwan should be so concerned about my health. For who could be interested in the fate of a murderer, but the hangman who would gain his fee?

You can ask him when he comes to see you.

I am gradually recovering my strength. Today I am sitting up in a chair, though with eyes half open. Gloom and misery weigh heavily on my spirits. Perhaps I should confess to the crime, as Justine did, just to have an end of it.
Such are my thoughts when the door of my room opens. Mr Kirwan joins me. His countenance, as always, is a picture of compassion. He brings a chair close to mine and addresses me in French:
'I fear that this place is very shocking to you, Monsieur Frankenstein. Can I do anything to make you more comfortable?'
'Thank you. It doesn't matter to me one way or the other. I am beyond comforting.'
'I realize that the sympathy of a stranger can be of little relief to one borne down by so strange a misfortune. But you will, I hope, soon quit this melancholy abode. Doubtless evidence can easily be brought to free you from the criminal charge.'
'That is the least of my concerns. I am the most miserable of men. Persecuted as I have been, can death be any worse?'
'That's what Hamlet asked himself, with the mortal coil and so on, wasn't it. No one's ever going to be able to answer that this side of the grave. But what interested me is this talk of persecution. While you were ill, there were quite a few things you said that strike me as puzzling.'

xoptx 'What things?'

xoptx 'As I have told you, his death was my fault.'

xoptx 'Why are you keeping me locked up?'
