'Sometimes you'd be raving about how you'd killed your own brother, and a girl called Justine who you - well, whom you knew, it seems. And even the unfortunate Mr Clerval, whose body was there in that boat.'
'There you are. Condemned out of my own mouth.'
'You're in a dreadful hurry to climb that scaffold, Mr Frankenstein. Because at other times you begged us to help you overcome the fiend - the monster, you called him - who killed all those people.'
'I might as well ask for help in destroying my own shadow.'
'Hmm. But you see, I happen to know that you didn't kill your brother. In fact it was the girl Justine who was convicted of that. It's funny the sort of things people will say. I've been a magistrate long enough to know the truth is never quite on the surface.'

'You will have to dig very deep to find the truth in this case.'

'Do you know, more good men say that kind of thing than bad ones.'
I shake my head. 'You cannot have any conception, Mr Kirwan, of the horrors I have unleashed on the world.'
He nods, but I see he doesn't understand. 'I don't bandy words like evil about too readily, but I had this young fellow up in front of me and he was rotten to the core. If he got into a fight, he didn't just look to knock the stuffing out of you, he'd try to maim. And in love he was worse. He had his girl's eye out, and that's why they finally got him.'

xoptx He's rambling.

xoptx Listen to his parable, if such it is.

'I'm coming to that. Nothing indeed could be more unfortunate than the strange events that have befallen you. You are thrown, by some surprising accident, on this shore, renowned for its hospitality, seized immediately, and charged with murder. The first sight that was presented to your eyes was the body of your friend, murdered and placed in your boat, I am now convinced, by some fiend.'
Some of my astonishment at this must show in my face, for Kirwan goes on to say, 'Oh, I know it all seemed to point at your guilt at the start there. But since then, I've been able to find out a bit more about the unfortunate victim.'
'Henri...' Again there are tears. I put my head in my hands. 'My oldest and dearest friend. I still can't believe...'
'Yes. Poor man. Let's not go into the details. All I wanted to say was that certain circumstances, shall we say, of Henri Clerval's life have come to light that shed a bit of a different complexion on things.'

xoptx 'What do you mean?'

xoptx 'So will you now release me?'

'This is all very well,' I tell Mr Kirwan, 'but if you don't mind I am more concerned with my own circumstances than with the exploits of your town's violent drunkards.'
'I gave him ten years' hard labour,' Kirwan goes on. 'Did he so much as blink? Not he. There's more remorse in a rattlesnake. But his father came to see me that night. I could smell the drink off him. A big man, used to hauling great nets filled with fish, but he was weeping like a child. And he was no soft touch, let me tell you. He'd broken a few tavern tables in his time.'
'Like father, like son,' I say, hoping to wind his homely little tale to a close.
'Mr Frankenstein, you're a clever man but you're awful blind with it. Didn't I say the son was cold as a thaw wind? The father had a conscience, though, so all the sins of the young man went onto his bill. That's how he saw it. "I'm to blame, Mr Kirwan," he told me.'
'What did you say?'
'I told him nonsense. A man is responsible for his own deeds, good and bad. Stealing a loaf to eat, I don't mean that. I'm talking about wickedness. If you murder somebody, you can't lay that at someone else's door. Now, I don't know all the facts of your case, but I can see that Mr Clerval got himself murdered, and I'm fairly sure you know why. But I don't believe you're to blame, unless we also want to blame the Almighty for Lucifer's crimes.'

xoptx That strikes close to the mark.

xoptx Then why has he got you in gaol?

Kirwan obviously sees the surprise on my face. 'You talked a great deal while you were ill,' he says. 'Well, of course, you were delirious. But I speak enough French to be able to pick out the parts that rang true.'
'Such as?'
'The law is looking elsewhere for his murderers.'
'I know very well who murdered him. A fiend who is intent on destroying my happiness to slake itshis revenge.'
'We can talk about that when you're better. What I actually came to tell you was that some letters were found among your things.'
Alarm leaps within me - but there were no scientific notebooks. Those went down into the sea with the remains of the female.
'The long and the short of it,' continues Kirwan, 'is that I wrote to your father at his address in Geneva.'
'What?' I jump up at this and Kirwan draws back slightly. After my long illness, my stare must present him with a frightful sight. I can imagine my drawn, pallid face, disarranged hair and sunken red eyes.
'I see you're still not well. You're trembling. I should leave it a bit before bringing you any news to agitate you like this.'
'Oh God, what has happened? Who has ithe murdered now?'
'Put away all this talk of murder,' says Kirwan gently. 'Your family is perfectly well. And there is somebody here to see you.'

xoptx Elizabeth?

xoptx Your father?

xoptx Surely not Adom?

xoptx Surely not the monster?

'It's not quite so straightforward. A charge was brought, so your case will need to be heard by the county court in a few weeks' time. But I'm confident you'll then be a free man.'
'They may not share your belief in my innocence. I'm not sure I do.'
He smiles, not sure whether I'm joking or not. 'The presiding justice is my brother-in-law. Not that we've discussed the case or anything.'
'Of course not.'
'Anyway, I realize this gaol is not the best place to recuperate from your illness, but I've got a bit of good news that will make the rest of your time here pass more easily. You have a visitor.'
He looks towards the door as he says this and, absurd as it is, the thought instantly darts into my mind that the monster is there. The image of it wrapped in a disguising cloak and hat is as vivid as life, and the chair goes flying as I retreat trembling into the corner of the cell.
'Oh my God. Keep it away! Keep ithim wrapped in a disguising cloak and hat is as vivid as life, and the chair goes flying as I retreat trembling into the corner of the cell.
'Oh my God. Keep him away! Keep him away!'
Kirwan goes to the door. My hands are pressed to my eyes. I cannot bear to see the grinning face of my tormentor.
'Mr Frankenstein,' he says with a sigh, 'please do calm yourself. How do you think your poor father will feel, seeing you like this?'

'My father?'

It is my father. Nothing could give me greater pleasure than to see his strong, wise countenance. I stretch out my hand. 'You are safe? And Elizabeth?'
'Victor, my boy, of course we are safe. We're going to get this cleared up, don't worry. Kirwan understands. There are a few formalities, but you'll be out of here in no time.' He looks around at the barred windows, the wretched appearance of the room - and, no doubt, the wretched appearance of its occupant. 'A terrible business, what happened to Clerval...'
In my weak state, the name of my unfortunate friend is an agitation too great to be endured. I slump on the bed, weeping. 'Poor Henri. He would be alive if not for me.'
'An unfortunate accident,' says my father, uncomfortable with the display of emotion.

xoptx An accident? How can he call it that?

xoptx Just go along with what he's saying.

'Elizabeth?' Kirwan smiles. 'Is she your sweetheart, so?'
'My cousin. And my fiancée.'
The smile curdles in place. 'Ah well, it's quite a wide world out there, isn't it, and there's more than one great guide of human life. But anyway, I'm going to leave you alone because I daresay you'll have quite a lot to talk over with your father.'
He looks towards the door and, absurd as it is, the thought instantly darts into my mind that the monster is standing just outside. The image of ithim wrapped in a disguising cloak and hat is as vivid as life, and the chair goes flying as I retreat trembling into the corner of the cell.
'Oh my God. Keep it away! Keep ithim away! Keep him away!'
Kirwan goes to the door. My hands are pressed to my eyes, hard enough to squeeze spots and whorls of light out of the void. I cannot bear to look at the grinning face of my tormentor.
'Mr Frankenstein,' he says with a sigh, 'please do calm yourself. How do you think your poor father will feel, seeing you like this?'

'My father?'

'He was strangled, Father! The marks on his neck... like William.'
'Calm down, Victor. This place is hardly conducive to recovery, but I've spoken to Mr Kirwan and we appreciate the severity of the shock you have suffered.'
'I thought we were talking about Henri. The shock, in his case, was fatal.'
'There's no call for sarcasm. I've made some enquiries about Clerval. The general feeling is that he skirted a little close to the wind and paid the price, and if Kirwan is willing to leave it there then I think we should too.'
'Oh yes, Father, Kirwan too insinuated something sordid about Henri's activities. But I can tell you that he was the dearest and best man in the world and, as his death was my fault, I'm damned if I'll let his good name be taken from him too.'
My father puts his arm around me, comforting and controlling too. 'No one will say a word against him, Victor,' he says in my ear. 'And you shouldn't blame yourself either. What's past is past, and soon we'll be going home.''I cannot guess how the demon has contrived this - to rob Henri of his good name as well as his life.'
'I suppose it was a demon that rode him,' says my father thoughtfully. 'At any rate, nobody blames you for what happened. Clerval brought it on himself.'

All you can do now is wait for the hearing.

'Alas, Father, a destiny of the most horrible kind hangs over me, and I must live to fulfil it, or surely I should have died of my fever.'
He manages a smile, though I see it costs him. 'You are well on the path to recovery, my son, thanks to the kindness of Mr Kirwan. Now just try to bear up until the hearing, then you can walk away a free man.'

The season of the assizes approaches. I have already been three months in prison and, although in continual danger of a relapse, I am obliged to travel nearly a hundred miles to the county town where the court is held.
I am spared the disgrace of appearing publicly as a criminal, as the case is not brought before a criminal court. The speed with which they deal with my case, coming after the feverish introspection of long weeks pacing my cell, leaves me bewildered. I am hardly called on to speak. Mr Kirwan has charged himself with every care of collecting witnesses and arranging my defence. The jury rejects the bill on the basis of a sealed affidavit from my father and very soon after I am set free.
As I quit the prison, I hear one of the men say, 'He may be innocent of the murder, but there's no doubt he has a bad conscience.' My father also hears this and, seeing my agitation, tries to distract me on our way to the coach stand with talk of home.

xoptx Tell him about your bad conscience.

xoptx Ask after Elizabeth.

What that warder said, Father...'
'Tittle-tattle!' he says with a snort. Obviously he's been brooding on it too. 'At least we can leave all that behind us.'
'But he's right, I do have a bad conscience. William, Justine and now Henri all died through my infernal machinations.' He tries to quieten me, but I hear my voice rising. 'And whose death is to finish the tragedy? Oh Father, let's not remain another instant in this wretched country. Take me where I may forget myself, my existence, and all the world.'
'Help you with your bags, sirs?' says the driver as we board the coach for Dublin. 'We'll soon have you out of this wretched country, now, don't you worry.'

You're going home.

'You'll see her soon. And home. Those are tonics as effective as anything you learnt to prescribe in Paris, I wouldn't wonder.'
'I do long to take a boat out on the lake and see the blue water and the snow-capped mountains all around.'
My father is just paying for our coach tickets, but he is so taken aback by my tone that he pauses with the notes still in his hand and turns to look at me. 'You say that with such melancholy, Victor.'
'Because I allowed myself a momentary dream of happiness. The truth is, the prison I have just left is as likely a place for me to find peace now as the loveliest scenery of home. If I were to find a place or person that gave me comfort, I can be sure that it will tear them from me.'
'Of whathe will tear them from me.'
'Of whom do you speak? I don't understand.'
'If I had the courage, Father, I should put an end to myself. Existence has become loathsome to me. Happiness is no longer possible. Why, then, not quit this sterile promontory for good?'
The coachman is overseeing the loading of our bags. Looking over his shoulder, he says, 'That's exactly my own feelings about these parts, sir. Don't worry, just a few minutes now and I'll have you safely on the road to Dublin.'

You're going home.

I feel as if I am relieved from a heavy weight when the ship sets out with a fair wind from Ireland. The coast drops away over the rim of the world and I know I am at least quit of the place that has been to me the scene of so much misery.
We share a cabin. I lie awake in the darkness listening to my father snore. It must be gone midnight. The waves smack and slap against the hull, the timbers creak slumbrously, but my mind as ever teems with a thousand notions.

xoptx Why not go up on deck?

xoptx Rest, and sleep will come.

I stand at the rail looking at the stars and listening to the dashing of the waves. The darkness shuts out any sight of land, for which I am glad. The past appears to me in the light of a frightful dream. Yet the vessel, the sea all around, the straining sails, and the wind that blows me from that detested shore - all speak too forcibly of the inescapable truth. It is no dream. Henri, my friend and dearest companion, is not waiting for me in the cabin below. He has fallen victim to me and the monster of my creation.
Such brooding thoughts prolong a journey that, measured by the clock, takes only a few days, and my pulse beats with a feverish, etiolated joy when we sight the harbour at Le Havre and I reflect that I shall soon see Geneva.
My mind, instead of giving me the dreamless sleep I crave, begins to replay my whole life. The days of youthful joy with my family in Geneva. The first glimpse of Elizabeth standing wide-eyed beside the carriage that brought her. The awful, rupturing blow that was the death of my mother. And my feelings of trepidation and excitement on setting out for university.
I am forced to relive the unreasoning fervour that propelled me towards the moment of creation. I see again the watery eye and imbecile smile as my hideous enemy awoke to life, and I call to mind the panic of that first night, pacing the courtyard in the rain.
I cannot hold a train of thought. My mind is a matrix of memories only. A thousand feelings press upon me, and I feel cold tears soaking my face. I reach for the jug of water beside me, and my fingers brush the little brown bottle of laudanum that I have carried with me since that first breakdown in Paris.

xoptx Go up on deck for some fresh air.

xoptx A dose of laudanum will help quiet your nerves.

I do find a small quantity of laudanum helps me to get to sleep. Oppressed by morbid visions as I am, perhaps a double dose...
But on this occasion sleep does not afford me respite from thought and misery. My dreams race into the future, presenting a succession of terrible outcomes. Half awake, as the predawn sky pales outside the porthole, I am possessed by a kind of nightmare in which I feel the fiend's grasp on my neck. Tossing to and fro on my bunk, I struggle to break free. Groans and cries ring in my ears.
'Victor! Victor!' My father shakes me to full consciousness. I see him glance at the laudanum bottle, but he says nothing.
Thank you for your advice, but I think I will not add drug addiction to my other problems. Instead I lie on my bunk. Sleep will not come. After many hours in which my mind seems to run in smaller and smaller wheels, a trapped mouse in a maze of undefined fears, I see the sky lightening outside and decide it is time to get up.
After a short voyage we put in at Le Havre.
Where am I?' I stop, seeing her look of incomprehension. Of course, I had forgotten I'm in Britain. I repeat the question in English.
'It's Mr Kirkdale's house, sir,' she says, hurrying with her tray to the door.
'How long have I been unwell?'
Already out of the door, she calls back, 'I don't know, sir. I've only been here six weeks myself.'
Six weeks! What enormities might Adomthe monster have wrought in that time? My father. Elizabeth. They are in danger.

xoptx Get dressed at once.

xoptx Stop and think.

I swing my legs out of bed, but the effort sends a sharp pain throbbing through my skull. I have to crawl on all fours to the wardrobe. My clothes are hanging there. Soaked in a cold sweat, I lean back against the wall, and that's how Kirkdale finds me.
'My dear fellow,' he says, helping me back to the bed. 'You must take things easy at first. You have been very ill.'
Why did Henri have to die? Because of the female creature, obviously. There's a biblical logic at work, an eye for an eye and a life for a life. But Adomthe fiend said I will be with you on your wedding night, so ithe cannot mean to murder Elizabeth. Is my father in danger? Perhaps. But as long as I've been lying helpless here in Perth, I don't think Adom would undertake a journey to Geneva.the creature would undertake a journey to Geneva.It gains no satisfaction from itsHe gains no satisfaction from his crimes unless I am fit to understand and to suffer.
I look towards the window. It is late afternoon. It must have rained earlier, because there are bright drops on the glass and I can see a faint streak of colour against the sky where the sun struggles to form a rainbow.
It makes me shudder to think it is out there in the streets, probably looking up at this very window. But, as long as it is here, at least my family are safe.he is out there in the streets, probably looking up at this very window. But, as long as he is here, at least my family are safe.

Speak to Mr Kirkdale.

Kirkdale draws up a chair beside my bed. 'You have been delirious for some considerable time, Mr Frankenstein. You had us quite worried.'
'It's very kind of you to have looked after me.'
'Think nothing of it. Simple Christian charity.' He pauses, choosing his next words carefully. 'You recall what happened? The shock you received?'
'My friend's murder.'
He nods. 'I'm afraid you've missed the funeral.'
'I wish you could tell me you've apprehended the one responsible.'
'It's very difficult in the case of a stranger,' he says, thinking that I meant to criticize the local constabulary. 'Hard to find witnesses and so forth. And when you couple that with Mr Clerval's predilections...'

xoptx What does he mean by that?

xoptx Tell him about the monster.

Kirkdale listens to me with the expression of a man indulging an over-imaginative child. 'I must say that a creature such as you describe cannot hope to blend in among the populace. That being the case, you can take it from me that he will be arrested soon, whether he is a monster built of living parts or a murderer of the more mundane variety.'
'You will never catch ithim,' I say bleakly.
'He had taken to frequenting some insalubrious parts of the city at night. A wealthy, well-dressed foreigner like that, poking his head into taverns along the wharf - in my opinion, it was only a matter of time before he came to harm.'
'I know very well who murdered him. A fiend who is intent on destroying my happiness to slake itshis revenge.'
He obviously attributes this remark to my illness. 'Eventually an informant will come forward. There is no honour among thieves, Mr Frankenstein, and less still among murderers.'

xoptx Explain to him why he will never catch this killer.

xoptx Ask if he has news of your family.

'Elizabeth?' Kirkdale smiles. 'She'd be your sweetheart, I take it.'
'My cousin. And my fiancée.'
'Well, the world is wide and, to use Hume's words, there's more than one great guide of human life.' He moves over to the door. 'I'm certain you have a lot to talk over with your father, so I'll take my leave for now.'
He looks towards the door and, absurd as it is, the thought instantly darts into my mind that the monster is standing just outside. The image of it wrapped in a disguising cloak and hat is as vivid as life, and I press myself back against the wall.
'Oh my God. Keep it away! Keep ithim wrapped in a disguising cloak and hat is as vivid as life, and I press myself back against the wall.
'Oh my God. Keep him away! Keep him away!'
Kirkdale goes to the door. I cover my eyes in horror. I cannot bear to gaze upon the grinning face of my tormentor.
'Mr Frankenstein,' Kirkdale says, 'I do ask you to make an effort to master your nerves. How do you think your poor father will feel, seeing you like this?'

'My father?'

'Indeed, that's precisely what I want to talk to you about,' he says. 'As you have been so unwell, and unable to attend to your affairs, I took the liberty of examining your papers and any letters that arrived here for you.'

xoptx Could he have found your notebooks?

xoptx But what about your family?

There is a knock at the door. 'Ah, there is somebody here to see you.'

xoptx Could it be Elizabeth?

xoptx Your father?

xoptx Surely not Adom?

xoptx Surely not the monster?

That concerned me too for a moment - but there are no surviving notebooks with details of my process. Those went down into the sea with the remains of the female creature.
'For God's sake, I must know if there is some fresh tragedy. Has ithe harmed my father or my cousin? Do you have news?'
He draws back slightly. After my long illness, my stare must present him with a frightful sight. I can imagine my drawn, pallid face, disarranged hair and sunken red eyes.
'There's no cause for alarm, Mr Frankenstein. Quite the reverse. Your family is perfectly well.'
It is my father. Nothing could give me greater pleasure than to see his strong, wise countenance. I clasp his hand in both of mine. His health is not usually good, but he feels robust as a rock compared to my fever-wasted strength.
'Father! You are safe? And Elizabeth?'
'Yes, yes, my boy, we are safe. Of course we are. I'm going to get you home, don't worry. We have berths on a ship to Le Havre sailing tomorrow.' He pauses. A muscle twitches in his jaw. 'A terrible business, what happened to Clerval...'
In my weak state, the name of my unfortunate friend is an agitation too great to be endured. I slump on the bed, weeping. 'Poor Henri. He would be alive if not for me.'
'It was an unfortunate accident,' says my father.

xoptx An accident? How can he call it that?

xoptx Just go along with what he's saying.

'He was strangled, Father! The marks on his neck, black and huge and deep... Like William.'
'Victor, I must tell you that I've made some enquiries about Clerval. The general feeling is that he skirted a little close to the wind and paid the price. Mr Kirkdale has reached the same conclusion.'
'God, what are you saying? How has the demon contrived this? To rob Henri of his good name as well as his life! ItHe is a fiend indeed.'
'I suppose it was a demon that rode him,' says my father thoughtfully. 'At any rate, nobody blames you for what happened. Clerval brought it on himself.'

Now at least you can go home.

'Alas, Father, a destiny of the most horrible kind hangs over me, and I must live to fulfil it, or surely I should have died of my fever.'
He manages a smile, though I see it costs him. 'You are well on the path to recovery, my son, thanks to the kindness of Mr Kirkdale. Whatever troubles have weighed you down, they are all behind you.'

Now at least you can go home.

I feel as if I am relieved from a heavy weight when the ship sets out with a fair wind from Scotland. The coast slides swiftly past and I know I am at least quit of the place that has been to me the scene of so much misery.
We share a cabin. I lie awake in the darkness listening to my father snore. It must be gone midnight. The waves smack and slap against the hull, the timbers creak slumbrously, but my mind as ever teems with a thousand notions.

xoptx Why not go up on deck?

xoptx Rest, and sleep will come.

The weeks pass slowly. The summer sky is piled with clouds like overhanging crags. During the day, the weather is frequently oppressively close. Thunderstorms blacken the afternoons and turn the sky into an immensity of rattling noise - or so it seems to me, for whom the sun has gone out since Henri's death. Alone in a strange place, I start at every footstep on the path outside at night. When I wake in the night, breathing hard, it is with both dread and hope that I fumble for the candle and look around the unfamiliar room, in case I should find the visitor at my bedside whose coming would end my despair.
At last, in the middle of a sweltering afternoon in which the whole city lies listless and fevered, a carriage stops in front of Mr Kirkdale's house. Down in the street, a familiar figure raises his hand. I smile for the first time since my arrival here, and hurry down to greet my father.

xoptx Talk to him about Henri's death.

xoptx Ask after Elizabeth.

xoptx Get home as quickly as possible.

'A terrible business, what happened to Clerval...' says my father.
'Poor Henri. He would be alive if not for me.'
'I don't see how you are to blame, Victor. It was an unfortunate accident.'

xoptx How can he call it an accident?

xoptx It's good that he thinks that.

'I thought you would have received her letters,' says my father. 'She has written to you almost every day.

xoptx Discuss Henri's death.

xoptx Set sail for home.

'He was strangled, Father, exactly like William. And by the same foul hand.'
'Victor, I must tell you that I've made some enquiries about Clerval. He was one of those men who led another life. Mr Kirkdale has reached the same conclusion.'
'Then you have both got it entirely wrong. Henri's friendship with me was the cause of his death.'
'I hope,' says my father gravely, 'that you don't know what you are saying. You were like two brothers, it always seemed to me, and what on earth is wrong with that. A simple, wholesome friendship.'
He refuses to be drawn further on the matter. As usual for my father, he has said either too little or too much.

Now at least you can go home.

'It is a tragedy that I wasn't here,' I say. 'To think of him, set upon and murdered. I weep to think of the terror and confusion he must have felt in those last moments.'
My father takes my hand. It's a gesture that belongs to my childhood. After six years in Paris I am a different person, and after a moment of embarrassment he lets go.
'I merely thank God you were not with him,' he says.

Now at least you can go home.

I set sail for Le Havre, not wishing to revisit any of the places where I enjoyed a few moments of tranquillity with my beloved Henri. It would be dreadful to see again any of the people we visited together, who would be bound to reopen the wound left by his death, even if they should remain silent about his absence.
On arrival I show the port official my papers under signs with the words Havre de Marat freshly painted out. The Revolution has cooled, the counter-revolution met with grapeshot outside the Tuileries, and all the while the French bureaucracy has quietly kept the store open.
There is a parcel of letters waiting for me at the coaching inn, including several from Elizabeth. The latest, dated only ten days earlier, is the longest.

xoptx Read it.

xoptx You'll see her soon enough.

'My dearest friend,' she begins,
'It gave me the greatest pleasure to receive a letter from my uncle to say you are on your way home. You are no longer at a formidable distance, and I may hope to see you in less than a fortnight. My poor cousin, how much you must have suffered! I expect to see you looking even more ill than when you quitted Geneva. These last months have been passed most miserably, tortured as I have been by anxious suspense. Yet I hope to see peace in your countenance, and to find that your heart is not totally void of comfort and tranquillity.
'Yet I fear that the same feelings now exist that made you so miserable a year ago, even perhaps augmented by time. I would not disturb you at this period when so many misfortunes weigh upon you, but a conversation that I had with my uncle prior to his departure renders some explanation necessary before we meet.
'Explanation! you may possibly say. What can Elizabeth have to explain? If you really say this, my questions are answered, and I have no more to do than to sign myself your affectionate cousin. But you are distant from me, and it is possible that you may dread and yet be pleased with what I am going to say. As I think this is probably the case, I dare not any longer postpone writing what, during your absence, I have often wished to express to you, but have never had the courage to begin.'

xoptx Read on.

xoptx Leave it. You'll see her in a few weeks.

'You well know, Victor, that our union has been the favourite plan of your parents ever since our infancy. We were told this when young, and taught to look forward to it as an event that would certainly take place. We were affectionate playmates during childhood, and dear and valued friends to one another as we grew older. But as brother and sister often entertain a lively affection towards each other without desiring a more intimate union, may not such also be our case? Tell me, dearest Victor. Answer me honestly, for our mutual happiness is at stake. Do you love another?
'You have travelled; you have spent several years of your life in Paris. I confess to you, my friend, that when I saw you last year so unhappy, so desirous of solitude, flying from the company of your family, I could not help supposing that you might regret our connection. Perhaps you believe yourself bound in honour to fulfil the wishes of your parents. But this is false reasoning. I confess to you, my cousin, that I love you, and that in my airy dreams of futurity you have been my constant friend and companion. But it is your happiness I desire as well as – ' She's crossed that out and put more than ' – my own. Thus I declare to you that our marriage would render me eternally miserable unless it were of your own free choice. Even now I weep to think that, borne down as you are by the cruellest misfortunes, you might stifle all hope of that love and happiness which would alone restore you to yourself, simply because you believe that to wed me is your duty. And so that I, who have so strong an affection for you, may increase your miseries tenfold by being an obstacle to your true wishes.
'Ah! Victor, be assured that your cousin and playmate sincerely loves you, and it would make me miserable to trap you in matrimony against the wishes of your heart. Be happy, my friend; and if you obey me in this one request, remain satisfied that nothing on earth will have the power to interrupt my tranquillity.
'Do not let this letter disturb you. Do not answer tomorrow, or the next day, or even until you come, if it will give you pain. My uncle will send me news of your health, and ifIf I see but one smile on your lips when we meet, occasioned by this or any other exertion of mine, I shall need no other happiness.
'Elizabeth Lavenza.'

xoptx The poor girl.

xoptx It's brave of her to write that letter.

xoptx How do you feel about what she's written?

I find it difficult to spare much sympathy for a young woman who, in her bored idleness, entertains herself only with morose speculation about my feelings. Oh, I care for Elizabeth, do not mistake me, but my own sufferings are real. I am tormented by a fiend of my own creation, that has slain my dearest friend only to prove itswho has slain my dearest friend only to prove his power over me. Elizabeth's concerns, as she vicariously suffers what she supposes to be the conflicts of my heart - how can I take these seriously?Indeed. I am chastened to think that, while I wallow in the indulgent miseries consequent upon my pride and folly, Elizabeth too is weighed down by fears and worries. And hers must be infinitely harder to bear, for she is unable to determine whether they are fancies or real, nor can she take arms against them. Her helplessness must be suffocating to her, while I can add to my catalogue of guilt the fact that I have not written her a letter, or even thought of her, in weeks.

xoptx You must write back immediately.

xoptx But do you want to marry her?

Yes, every sentence cost her dearly, that much is clear, and in the forced lightness of tone I detect the labour of hours of thought, and dozens of crumpled sheets and broken quills.

xoptx It seems she loves you very much.

xoptx She does not realize the Damoclean threat that hangs over any marriage plans.

Sweet and beloved Elizabeth! I read and re-read what she's written, and some softened feelings creep into my heart and dare to whisper paradisiacal dreams of love and joy. But the apple is already eaten, and the angel's arm bared to drive me from all hope. Yet I would die to make her happy. If a normal life. But the apple is already eaten, and the angel's arm bared to drive me from all hope. To make her happy will bring about my doom. If Adom executes the monster executes itshis threat, death is inevitable - and my wedding could hasten my fate. But if I were to postpone it, and my torturer suspected that I did so to defer the end, it would surely find other and perhaps more dreadful means of revenge. It has vowed to be with me on my wedding night, yet that threat clearly doesn't bind it to peace in the meantime for, as if to show me that it was not yet satiated with blood, it must have he would surely find other and perhaps more dreadful means of revenge. He has vowed to be with me on my wedding night, yet that threat clearly doesn't bind him to peace in the meantime for, as if to show me that he was not yet satiated with blood, he must have already murdered Clerval before issuing murdered Clerval immediately after issuing itshis threats.
I am therefore resolved that if my immediate union with my cousin will make her and my father happy, my adversary's designs against my life must not retard it a single hour.

xoptx You must write back at once.

xoptx You can tell her that when you see her.

My reply is calm and affectionate. 'I fear, my beloved girl,' I write, 'little happiness remains for us on earth. Yet all that I may one day enjoy is centred on you. Chase away your idle fears. To you alone do I consecrate my life and my hopes of happinessendeavours for contentment. I have one secret, Elizabeth - a dreadful one. When you hear it, far from being surprised at my melancholy, you will only wonder that I can survive what I have endured. I will confide this tale of misery and terror to you the day after our wedding - for, my sweet cousin, there must be perfect confidence between us. But until then, I ask that you not mention or allude to it. This I most earnestly entreat, and I know you will comply.'
I give the letter to the postmaster. My father looks at his watch for the third or fourth time. 'Did you write her a whole book?' he says, nonetheless pleased. 'Come along, Victor, our coach will be leaving.'Outside, the coach driver is ringing his bell.

Continue your journey.

When I read this, I can only think of the fiend's words: 'I will be with you on your wedding night.' On that night will it try to tear me from the glimpse of happiness which would console my sufferings. On that night it has determined to consummate its crimes. Well, so be it. A deadly struggle will then assuredly take place. If it is victorious, I will be at peace, and itshe try to tear me from the glimpse of happiness which would console my sufferings. On that night he has determined to consummate his crimes. Well, so be it. A deadly struggle will then assuredly take place. If he is victorious, I will be at peace, and his power over me be at an end. If he is vanquished, I shall be a free man.

xoptx And even that a Pyrrhic victory.

xoptx At any rate, you had better write back.

I was about to add: what freedom? The same freedom as the peasant enjoys when his family have been massacred before his eyes, his cottage burnt, his lands laid waste, and he is turned adrift, homeless, penniless and alone, but free. Such would be my liberty, and that only if I can destroy Adom, except that in the monster, except that in my Elizabeth I possess a treasure who might help to hold at bay those horrors of remorse and guilt which will now pursue me until death.starting a family I might at least find solace and the shell of a life. Poor Elizabeth, she will be the virgin sacrifice on the altar of my misery.

xoptx You had better write back.

xoptx You'll see her in a few weeks.

That would be heartless, when she has screwed up her courage to set all these concerns down on paper. No, I shall write back to her this minute. No doubt you are right. What would I put in a letter, anyway, that isn't better said in person?
My father's sole desire and purpose is to see me restored to peace of mind. His tenderness and attentions are unremitting. My grief is obstinate, but he is obdurate, and throughout our journey he maintains an often one-sided conversation.
After one period of protracted silence, as we go juddering and rattling up a rutted track that connects two highways, my father says, 'There is no need to feel ashamed of your appearance before that court, Victor. Perhaps you feel some stain yet attaches to your character, but the judges were at pains to exonerate you fully.'
I am not much in a mood to talk, having had enough of his attempts to draw me out of myself, and I fear that if I answer him it will be ill-temperedly.

xoptx Pretend to be asleep.

xoptx Tell him honestly what you think.

'You think I am silent out of hurt pride? Father, how little you know me. I have no pride left at all. Justine, poor unhappy Justine... She suffered the same charge, except that she died for it. She was more innocent than I. And I am the cause. I murdered her. William, Justine, and Henri - they all died by my hands.
I don't think that would be helpful. I force myself to say, 'I had been concerned about that, Father, but I see now I was wrong. Thank you for pointing it out. Now, please excuse me but I am very tired and I'm going to try and get a nap before our next stop.'

'A very unfortunate affair,' says my father. 'Still, we can look forward to happier times, eh? Look, Victor, there is a sign to Dijon. Not far now. Elizabeth will be waiting eagerly by the window every morning, if I know her.'
He seems content to ride on in silence, so I settle my head back and try to get some sleep.

He's right, you'll be home in a few days.

'What do you mean, Victor?' he cries desperately. 'You keep making assertions of this sort. Are you mad? I entreat you, son, never to say anything of this kind ever again.'
Usually when I make an outburst like this, he attributes it to delirium and the madness of grief. Occasionally he has pressed me to say more - even then, I think, more to draw the poison of what he considers insanity than out of any suspicion that I might speak the truth. Until now I have retreated into silence, convinced that, if I were to tell him about the wretch I created, he would certainly think I have lost my wits.
'Father,' I say bitterly, 'I would give the whole world to reveal to you the fatal secret, but I would spare you that horror.'

xoptx You're wise to keep silent.

xoptx Perhaps it's time you told him.

I must strive to be calm. It is enough that I am conscious of my own transgressions. It helps no one if I rant about them incoherently. And so I sit and bite my tongue, literally, until it bleeds, and so curb the imperious voice of wretchedness, which yearns to declare itself to the whole world.
After a while my father says, 'I must say, Victor, you seem more contented than I have seen you since the trip to Chamounix. Look, there is a sign to Dijon. I didn't realize we'd come so far. Elizabeth will be waiting eagerly by the window every morning, if I know her.'
He seems content to ride on in silence, so I settle my head back and try to get some sleep.

You'll be home in a few days.

'I am not mad. The sun can bear witness. The sky knows I am telling the truth. I am the assassin of those most innocent victims; they died by my machinations. I would rather have shed my own blood, drop by drop, a thousand times if I could have saved their lives. But I could not, Father. Indeed, I could not sacrifice the whole human race.'
After a long pause, my father responds cheerfully enough, changing the subject to point out the road signs and how near we are to home. But I see the pain and worry in his eyes, convinced as he must be now that I have become deranged, and I feel a fresh twist of self-loathing that I have burdened him with that too.

Better continue the journey in silence.

'Poor Clerval,' he says. 'Will his killer ever be brought to justice, I wonder. But perhaps I should have more faith. It is easy to distrust the law in a foreign country.'
'And what of the law in our own country, Father? Was that justice, to hang an innocent girl for William's murder?'
'I know you felt Justine to be innocent. It's natural. She was part of our household for years. Indeed, there were times in your youth that I thought you preferred her company to your cousin's.'
'It isn't mere sentiment, Father, that makes me protest her innocence. She died by my machinations, as surely as if I had built the gallows on which she was hanged. You may believe I would have put my own head in the noose if it could have saved Henri's life, but I could not. Nor could I doom the human race, though one deed of mine would spare this family future misery.'
'What do you mean?'
I stare out at the woods rushing past the carriage, noting distantly that it will not be long before the leaves turn. 'A man may commit a dreadful deed believing he does good. Macbeth thought he would make a better king than Duncan, perhaps. But then another must be sacrificed to cover up the crime. And another - no self-delusion now can hide the truth.'
He turns to me, tight lines of worry across his face. 'Victor, what are you saying?'
'I cannot fix what I've done. But one monster is better than an entire species. I will see an end of it, Father. And because I refuse to compound my crimes, Henri is dead.'

Enough. He already thinks you're mad.

I rest my forehead against the cool glass of the carriage window and watch the countryside speed by. This is the road I took eight years ago when I set off for Paris. Odysseus full of dreams of adventure, before the horrors of war blasted any taste for glory from him.
Oh, that I could wind back time, each league taking me back a month, so that I should arrive in Geneva eighteen years old and innocent.

xoptx There's your next project.

xoptx And would you still create the monster?

xoptx What will you do when you arrive home?

I rest my forehead against the cool glass of the carriage window and watch the countryside speed by. This is the road I took eight years ago when I set off for Paris. I was Odysseus on the cliffs at Ithaca, full of dreams of adventure, before the horrors of war blasted any taste for glory from him.
When I get back, I'll have to go and see Henri's family. It will be the hardest thing about my homecoming.
Perhaps I can leave it a while. Or send Elizabeth - she'll have a better idea what to say to them.
There is the signpost for Beaune. Less than thirty leagues to go now.

You'll soon be home.

What? Having plundered the secrets of Life, now I should conquer Time? Hah! And then bring back the dead, if I still had an idle moment before bedtime? Ha ha, that is a fine jest!
The coachman slides back the partition. 'Are you all right, sir?'
It's absurd - inside, my heart is black as pitch, and here I am rolling merrily on the seat like a drunk coming back from the theatre. With a supreme effort I pull myself together.
'I am fine. How far to our next stop, citizen?'
'We'll be at Beaune in a couple of hours, sir.'
'A nice wine with supper tonight, then. Thank you, citizen.'
Before closing the panel, he says, 'No need for "citizen" these days, sir.

xoptx You'll soon be home.

xoptx What will you say about Henri's disappearance?

Are you mad? I would never even set out for Paris! All that revolutionary fervour, it stirred me to a Promethean hubris. If I had taken a place instead at Ingolstadt, say, then I might have gone about my studies unremarkably and been content with curing smallpox or creating a serum to increase human intelligence. It was that fanatic madness dressed in the clothing of reason that made me build a monster.I'd do it differently, of course. Rather than starting with a human being, I would first give life to a much simpler organism. I don't mean a cat or dog - a type of worm or jellyfish would do. Then, having proved the principle, it would be easier for me to raise funds and recruit a team of specialists, anatomists and so on, that would permit the creation of a being without the monstrously distorted proportions that have led to Adom being shunned and so running amok.
 the monster being shunned and so running amok.
On the other hand, what purpose do these idle speculations serve? As the Duke says in Othello, to mourn a mischief that is past and gone is the next way to draw new mischief on.It's pointless to think of such things until I face my first monster again. That night will decide which of us lives.

What will you say about Henri's disappearance?

Oh God, I'll have to go and see his Henri's family. How will I face them? In case I had not reason enough to despise my own wretchedness, I'm inflicting years of distress on them. They will vainly wait for him to come home, the sands of their hope slowly trickling away. Although I've heard it said that a mother can sense when her child dies. It's better if that's so.
A signpost flashes by: a familiar crossroads. Geneva is less than a hundred miles away now.

You'll soon be home.

Geneva is unchanged. It never changes. My cousin is there at the door, as I knew she would be. She welcomes me with warm affection, yet I see a drawn look in her eyes, close to tears, as she takes in my emaciated limbs and feverish cheeks. She is wondering if I am back home to stay this time.

xoptx How do you think she's looking?

xoptx Give her a smile.

She's thinner than I remember. And as we talk I can't help noticing that she has lost much of that heavenly vivacity that always artless vivacity that always charmed me. But her gentlenessredeemed the plainness of her conversation. But her docility and soft and bovine looks of compassion make her a more fit companion for one as haunted and burdened and miserabledisconsolate as I have become.

xoptx How do you feel about being home?

xoptx What about preparations for the wedding?

Thank you, I had not forgotten. I greet her with a smile and a kiss, with looks and endearments giving the impression that I have thought of none but her since we last parted.
'Oh, dearest cousin,' she says, pressing her cheek to mine, and I taste a salty wetness on my lips. 'You remembered my letter.'
'I have read it a hundred times,' I assure her, noting over her shoulder the pleasure this remark gives my father.How could I forget?' I reply, casting about for some way to change the subject.

xoptx How do you feel about being home?

xoptx What about preparations for the wedding?

It's not easy. The cosy familiarity of my surroundings, which I had expected to soothe away the nightmares, in fact throws the events of the last few months into still more terrible relief. The worst moments are the most commonplace - when I sit at the breakfast table and hear the inexorable soft ticking of the clock, or notice that old crack across the tile inside the back door, or watch Elizabeth at her tirelessthe unending Penelopean work of her embroidery under the apple tree in the garden.
I feel as though I am burning with rage, but that the fire is pouring into an immense cold sea, so that in the end my thoughts churn darkly but I only sit silent and despondent, unaware of place and time.

xoptx You must try to control your moods.

xoptx Take a walk across the hills.

You may as well counsel me to try and control the weather.

Leaving the house one morning, I find Elizabeth already dressed for a long hike. 'Exercise will do you good, Victor,' she says, taking my arm.
I must say she is right. Not that striding across the hills dispels the multitude of miseries that beset me, but this is the same landscape that inspired me to miraculous endeavours before. The helplessness is gone. Danger remains, but I recall who I am. Frankenstein! I created life! I have my rage and I will be revenged on the mean wretch that thinks to degrade me like itselfhimself.
I am muttering under my breath. I see Elizabeth's sidelong look. 'Not madness, my dear,' I assure her. 'Inspiration.'

And what about preparations for the wedding?

My father also wishes to discuss that. One day after lunch he asks me into his study to talk about the future. 'I have bought you a house near Cologny,' he tells me, affecting to make light of it but secretly hoping for expressions of delight.
'Father, this is marvellous!' I say, forcing a tone of gaiety into my voice.'
'Thank you, that will be very convenient.'
'I've seen how you still enjoy long walks in the country. And'You always enjoyed the countryside, and at the same time you'll be near enough that we can see each other every day.'
'Perhaps not every day until after the honeymoon, eh, Father?'
'I could not ask for a more perfect arrangement, Father.'
'You are looking forward to the wedding, then?'
One of his probing questions. He pretends to fill his pipe while actually keeping a close eye on my reaction. How do I answer that honestly, knowing that a frightful fiend doth close behind me tread, and has chosen that day for our fateful assignation?

xoptx Best to say nothing.

xoptx You can tell a white lie, surely?

'Have you, then, some other attachment?'
'None on earth.
'I love Elizabeth, and look forward to our union with delight. Let us fix the day, and on that day I will consecrate myself, in life or death, to the happiness of my cousin.'
'My dear Victor, don't speak like that. Heavy misfortunes have befallen us, but let us cling closer to what remains. Remembering those we have lost, we must transfer our love to those who are alive. Our family circle will be small, but it will be bound by the close ties of affection and mutual misfortune. And when time has allayed your grief, new and dear objects of care will be born to replace those of whom we have been so cruelly deprived.'
By objects of care he means babies. It was a different world before the Flood - or when he was young, whichever came later. But all I say is, 'Yes, Father. So, as to the date ..?'
'Yes, Father. So, as to the date ..?'
'Everything is ready. Let us say ten days from now.'

xoptx You could arm yourself against the monster.

xoptx The day is fixed. Your fate is inevitable.

Omnipotent as the fiend has been in its deeds of blood, it is small wonder that I regard ithis deeds of blood, it is small wonder that I regard him as almost invincible. And those words, 'I shall be with you on your wedding night,' sound to me as inevitable as the tolling of a funeral bell.
But death is no evil to me if the loss of Elizabeth should be balanced against it. Therefore I put on an air of contentment and even of cheerfulness. Ten days - there is the seal on my fate. Nothing can avert it now.

Fatalism seems to be the only response left to you.

You're right. Strong and cunning though it is, I know from its own account that ithe is, I know from his own account that he can be hurt. I contrive a harness in which I can conceal a dagger and a pistol under my jacket. As I have lost weight since Henri's death, there is ample room to disguise their presence with padding. Coming across a silver hip flask Elizabeth gave me for my birthday, I fill it with a chemical of my own devising that will react corrosively with the monster's skin. The quantity is small, but it would be enough to cripple a limb or even blind ithim if I am quick enough. I just have to remember not to drink from it.

Now you're ready to face it.

Now you're ready to face him.

As the period fixed for our marriage draws near, whether from cowardice or a sense of helpless inevitability, I feel my heart sink within me. When I'm with my father, I affect a bluff manner that entirely deceives him. He is beaming with joy as he helps with arrangements for the ceremony.
Elizabeth, though, sees a little deeper. I feel her eyes on me, sensing the troubled thoughts that I am concealing behind a mask of happiness. She seems to look forward to our union with placid contentment, but it must be mingled with a little fear just as a horse picks up nervousness from its rider., which past misfortunes have surely impressed on her, that what seems to be our certain and tangible joy might dissipate into an airy dream, and leave no trace but deep and everlasting regret.

END OF CHAPTER TWO


CHAPTER THREE
CHAPTER THREE
The Cup of Life is Poisoned

Preparations are made. Congratulatory visits are received. Everyone is smiling, talking, joyful. I shut up in my own heart the anxiety that preys there in order to throw myself into my father's plans, even though they might only serve as the decorations of my tragedy. It is tomorrow. As I wind the clock, I reflect on how, in twenty-four hours, my fate will be decided.

xoptx How do you feel?

xoptx What about Elizabeth?

Curiously, the nearer the day has come, the more I see Adomthe monster's threat as a delusion, somehow unreal and not worth losing a minute's sleep over. And at the same time, the happiness I might hope for in my marriage seems more certain. Everyone speaks of it daily, in such unremarkable and glad terms, that it seems no accident can possibly prevent it. And, of course, I have armed myself well. Pistol, knife and acid, all with their comforting weight at hip or breast, combine to create a great tonic for the nerves.

xoptx Let's hope you are right.

xoptx Hopefully you won't have to use them.

Let's hope you are right.

Hopefully you won't have to use them.

xoptx Does Elizabeth share your mood?

She seems happy. I've striven to remain tranquil, absorbed in arrangements for the move to our new house, and that has helped to calm her mind. But this evening at dinner she was melancholy, as though a presentiment of evil pervades her. She may be brooding on the dreadful secret that I promised to reveal after our wedding.

xoptx And your father?

And your father?

xoptx What of your own mood?

My father? He's overjoyed, of course. If, in the bustle of preparation, he has noticed Elizabeth's distracted state, I am sure he will just have attributed it to a bride's diffidence.

Unable to sleep, I finally give up the attempt and rise as predawn turns the sky pale. Throughout the morning I am content to be moved about according to the plans that have been made for me. There is an unreal sense about the house, festooned as it is with streamers of coloured paper and with fluttering womenfolk in every room. I catch my father's eye as we tie each other's cravats. How happy he looks.
After the ceremony, which takes place at midday, a large party assembles at my father's. I hear myself telling face after unfamiliar face that Elizabeth and I will be going to Evian this afternoon. 'We'll spend the night there,' she says, coming over to take my arm. 'And then go back to our house at Cologny tomorrow.'
'It's a fine day,' replies a man who I think may be an uncle. 'Will you go by boat?'
'Oh yes,' says Elizabeth, her face lighting up as she seizes the opportunity she's been waiting for, 'my husband loves sailing.'
She takes great pride in saying it, and I feel guilty that my deliberations about what is to come keep me at a distance, a sleepwalker in the midst of the revelry.

When we get out on the water the sun is bright. It's perhaps the last day like this we'll get this year. The banks of the lake are patterned with the changing hues of autumn, fading into the distant line of blue mountains with their caps of brilliant white.
Elizabeth is watching me from under the shade of a canopy. I stand at the rail, scanning the water for sight of the fiend thatthe enemy who could be lurking there.

xoptx She's waiting for you to say something.

xoptx Stay alert. There'll be time to talk later.

I take her hand. 'You are quiet, Elizabeth.'
'Because you are, my love.'
This has been her mood throughout the day. There will be joy in her eyes for a moment, continually darkening behind a look of pensive reverie.
'Last night at dinner you seemed sorrowful. 'Lately I have sensed a melancholy behind your smiles. But if you knew what I have suffered - and what I may yet have to endure - you would strive to let me taste the freedom from despair that this one afternoon affords me.'
'Be happy, my dear Victor. There is nothing to distress you. Even if a lively joy is not painted in my face, my heart is contented. True, for days I have felt a growing sense of dread. Something whispers to me not to depend too much on the prospect that is opened before us. But I will not listen to such a sinister voice.'

xoptx This is the moment to tell her everything.

xoptx Try to enjoy what might be your last hours of happiness.

Not yet. If the monster keeps itsNot yet. If my adversary keeps his promise, she will know everything soon enough. So why cloud her hopes of our future life together?
'You have waited patiently for this day,' I tell her. 'And now it is here. When we have a cherished dream, nurtured over months or years, often the moment of attaining it can take the wind out of one's sails.'
At this, as I planned, we both look to the white canvas overhead, which strains against a billowing wind.
Elizabeth laughs. 'You are right, beloved. It's an undiscovered land we are sailing to - '
'Evian. There be dragons.'
She laughs again, her mood visibly lightening. 'Matrimony! And we have no map or chart to help us navigate its climes, which no doubt explains why I have been unwontedly fretful. But it will be a land of many summers, Victor.'
I squeeze her hand. 'I would not turn back from today's voyage, at any rate - whatever regrets I have earned in the rest of my life.'
I can think of no reply. After a minute or so of silence, Elizabeth goes on:
I remain silent, watchful. If I have learned anything about the fiend by now, it is that it is infinitely cunning and resourceful. I have an image of it clinging to the hull, itsmy adversary by now, it is that he is infinitely cunning and resourceful. I have an image of him clinging to the hull, his monstrous arm erupting from the water like a ghastly red eel to seize my bride. Instead of dismissing such a macabre inspiration, as I would once have done, I welcome it. It shows that I am starting to reason as the monster does.
Elizabeth comes up behind me and lays her small hands on my arms. She must feel how tense I am. I say nothing. After a moment, I feel the butterfly weight of her cheek rest on my shoulder.
'Look how fast we're going, Victor. We'll get to the inn and they won't have our rooms ready. I mean our room. Oh, see those clouds! That big one, it's rolling back from us. A moment ago you couldn't see the peak of Mont Blanc and now the cloud seems balanced on it. I think it's the weather that makes this scenery so interesting, because it is never the same. People think of mountains as unchanging, but I could watch them for hours and be entertained as much as at the theatre. Oh my, look, darling! Look at all the fish! The water's so clear you can see every pebble at the bottom. Did we bring any food to give them? What a shame. But it's a divine day, the most perfect day of the year. Or ever. How blissfully serene all of nature appears at this moment!'
The sun sinks lower in the heavens. We pass the River Drance, where the Alps come closer to the lake, and approach the amphitheatre of mountains that forms its eastern boundary. Amid the woods, the spire of Evian shines in the evening sun, a splinter of gold on the velvet dusk of the mountains.

xoptx Blissful.

xoptx Ominous.

The wind dies at sunset to a light breeze. The soft air just ruffles the water and causes a pleasant stir among the treetops as we approach the shore. A delightful scent of flowers and hay hangs in the air.
The sun sinks behind us as I touch the shore. The glare of red is the only vivid colour in a twilight that feels suddenly cold. And, with the going of the day, I feel the reawakening of those cares and fears that have lain coiled asleep within me these past few hours.
We walk for a short time on the shore, enjoying the transitory light while our bags are carried into the inn. As night falls we have dinner with the curtains open, providing us with an awe-inspiring backdrop of water, woods, and mountains, obscured in darkness, yet still displaying their black outlines.
We are alone in the dining room, though there are candles lit on every table. I look across to a floor-length mirror where a newly married couple dine in silence. Are they happy, I wonder.
As the waiter withdraws having poured our wine, Elizabeth slides her hand across the tablecloth, her fingertips just resting on mine. 'This meal is delicious, Victor, but I'm so excited I can hardly taste it.'
Knowing what is to come, I have hardly touched the food on my own plate.

xoptx Are you excited?

xoptx Or resigned?

The wind has started to pick up. Clouds sweep across the face of the moon, darkening the evening, which had seemed so tranquil before. The lake becomes choppy. Suddenly a heavy burst of rain lashes the windowpanes, causing us both to jump.
The night is still, without even a breath of wind to stir the surface of the lake. The moon hangs among the unending stars. There is no sound. The rest of the human race could have vanished, and we alone here to eat this final supper.
I have been calm up till now, I have surprised myself with my calm, but as night blurs the edges of things, a thousand fears rouse in my mind. I slip a hand inside my coat. The pistol is still there. Whatever lies ahead, I will sell my life dearly. Either I or the monster will die tonight.
Elizabeth casts me a timid look as we go through to the parlour. When she speaks, it is with downcast face, and so quietly that I almost don't hear her. 'What agitates you, dear Victor? What is it you fear?'
The answer bursts from me. 'Oh! Peace, peace, my love. This night only, and all will be safe. But this night is dreadful, very dreadful.'
A servant brings us brandies and coffee. Mine sit beside me untouched. When the clock strikes, I realize we have been sitting here for an hour. Elizabeth is watching me from the other side of the hearth.
'It is late, dearest,' she says softly.
Following her gaze, I catch a glimpse of the servant who is waiting in the hall for us to go to bed.

xoptx Tell her you'll be up shortly.

xoptx Go up with her.

Good idea. She's in the way, and the servant too.I haven't been thinking. Elizabeth must not be here when Adom the monster makes its makes his move. What if she were to see me slaughtered before her eyes? Dreadful enough to lose her husband, if that is to be the outcome of our combat, but at least I can spare her the full horror of being a witness.
'You had better go up,' I tell her.
'Aren't you coming?'
'There is something I have to do. I will join you presently.'
I dismiss the servant too. Now I am alone by the fire listening to the wind rattle the windows.in the hushed watches of the night.

xoptx Take a look around outside.

xoptx Wait here for the monster.

We ascend to our room. I am tense, eyes restlessly probing the shadows for the furtive movement of the one I fear. Though how could it conceal its hulking presence among the low, narrow passages and groaning floorboards of this ancient building? Perhaps ithe conceal his hulking presence among the low, narrow passages and groaning floorboards of this ancient building? Perhaps he will not come after all.
Elizabeth takes my hand. She wears a radiant smile. 'You are trembling, my love,' she says, 'come here into the bedroom where it's warm.'
I stand by the small fireplace, hearing the small whispering movements of fabric as she gets ready for bed in the adjoining roomthe importunate blustering of wind and rain against the tiny leaded panes. The door to the dressing room opens. Elizabeth stands there with the lamp behind her. Through the thin cotton of her nightdress I see that she is very slender, barely sturdier than a child. A woman's day clothes conceal that. The female forms I have seen naked, whether dead or living, have usually been lower-class women, and they are always fleshier. And her skin - it's so taut. I know better than anybody what a rig of capillaries and bone and meat and nerve fibres must fit under that skin. It seems miraculous that it could all be packed so perfectly in so delicate a frame.
I am advancing towards the bed when I feel a gust of cold air from under the door. The fire flattens and jumps in the grate. I know what it means. A door or window has been opened downstairs.

xoptx Go to investigate.

xoptx Stay with your wife.

And lead the monster right to her? What are you saying? Whatever happens tonight will be bad enough. I can at least spare my wife the horror of witnessing it.
'Victor!' Elizabeth sits up on the bed. 'Where are you going?'
What can I tell her? 'Patience, my love. I shall be back in a moment.'
I edge nervously down the stairs. TheA knife is in my hand. What use it will be against the monster, I cannot say. I remember well enough the great weight of its limbs as I lifted them in its amnion. If it were to seize my skull, ithis limbs as I lifted them in his amnion. If he were to seize my skull, he could press it flat between those enormous palms.
The front door of the inn is open, allowing rain to blow in sheets across the floor of the hall. Outside, the garden lies in velvet stillness. Is it an invitation? AdomThe fiend wants me to go out to meet it?him?

xoptx You are armed. Do so.

xoptx No. The monster is already inside.

Elizabeth stretches out on the bed, her hair spread across the pillows, feet together and arms laid out beside her as if she were on a cross. A smile plays on her lips, meek and yet promising a smouldering heat. With half-closed eyes she watches me stoke the fire.
'Are you coming to bed?' It's really just a breath of words, barely audible.
Fearful of relaxing my guard, I cross to lock the door. Onto the bedside table I unload my arsenal of defences - the knife, gun and flask of acid. Elizabeth gives a thick, slightly nervous laugh. 'Was that in case I triedElizabeth giggles. 'Is that in case I try to get away?'
I'm beside the bed. She doesn't move a muscle, just looks up. I'm reaching for her when -
An explosion of glass. I sense a blur of movement, something huge leaping into the room, but before I can raise my eyes I feel a crunching blow. My teeth grind together. A metallic taste in my mouth. A blossom of spreading red darkness behind my eyes. And over the roar of my own heartbeat I seem to hear, from the top of a deep well, the faint frenzied cry of my bride.

Alas, the monster kept his promise.

Alas, the monster kept its promise.

I walk right around the building, examining every bush, bower and cranny in the grounds in case my adversary is lying in wait. There is no sign of him. And then, as I return across the lawn, I hear a piercing scream from an upper window. It is Elizabeth!
I race back, coming upon Adomthe monster by the wall beneath our window. ItsHis ghastly face grins back at me from the moon-limned shadows.
That voice, like a grave opening: 'I said I would come, Frankenstein.'

xoptx Attack it.

xoptx Attack him.

xoptx See to Elizabeth.

I have some sense that time has passed. Someone is shaking me by the arm. 'Sir, oh sir, what has happened?'
It's the innkeeper's wife. I sit up, wits still scrambled. The blood on my shirt is my own. There are faces in the doorway: a gaggle of servants in their nightgowns, all staring in horror at -
'Elizabeth!' I run to her side, but I can see it's too late. She has been broken and flung across the bed like a doll. 'Oh God, Elizabeth. I thought the fiend meant to come for me, but you were itshis victim all along.'
The others come nervously further into the room. The innkeeper's wife turns from the shattered window with a gasp. 'There's somebody running across the lawn!'
'The murderer!' a man's voice yells. 'Quickly - after him!'

xoptx Go and help them hunt the monster down.

xoptx Stay here with Elizabeth's body.

I patrol the grounds of the inn, examining every bush and wall in case my adversary is lying in wait. There is no trace of it. Perhaps - is it too much to hope that it has him. Perhaps - is it too much to hope that he has had a change of heart?met with a fatal accident?
I see a light at one of the upper windows. Strange I didn't notice it before. Then I am running back across the lawn, shouting for Elizabeth over the crescendo of the storm, as my pounding heart informs my brain that danger is near.
I come upon it at the foot of the wall. Itshim at the foot of the wall. His ghastly face grins back at me through the lancing rain. Above, our bedroom window is open, the curtains billowing wetly like the gasping of an injured lung.

xoptx Attack the monster.

xoptx Elizabeth's safety must be your first concern.

A shrill scream pierces the night. On the upper floor, a lighted window that was curtained a moment before. I am already pounding across the grass calling Elizabeth's name. The scream is cut off, suddenly, horribly, and my blood freezes.
I come upon it at the foot of the wall. Itshim at the foot of the wall. His ghastly face grins back at me from the deep shadows. In the stillness, I feel all hope bleeding out of me as if from a fatal wound.

xoptx Attack the monster.

xoptx Elizabeth's safety must be your first concern.

I spend some time walking up and down the passages of the house, inspecting every corner where my adversary might be hiding. I can find no trace of it. him. Perhaps it isn't coming. It might have - Perhaps he isn't coming. He might have - A scream! The moment I hear it, the whole truth rushes into my mind. The pistol falls from nerveless fingers. My every muscle and fibre feels stretched to immobility.
How can I have been so blind? ItHe never meant to come for me.
She screams again. There can be no doubt - it is Elizabeth. I throw myself up the stairs. That second scream is so shrill and fearful, a chilling sound. But more terrible by far is the way it is suddenly cut off.
I hesitate at the door. Dead silence within. I can feel the blood trickling in my veins and tingling in the extremities of my limbs. With an effort I force myself to break the paralysis, and I rush into the room.
Elizabeth is lying across the bed. I start to say her name, but what emerges from my lips is only a whimper. I can already see from the angle of her neck that she is dead.
A momentary cloud of darkness. Merely a flutter, like the sweeping of an angel's wing. I must have passed out. I am collapsed beside the bed with my arms lying across the limp white form of my wife.
The innkeeper's wife is shaking me. 'Sir, oh sir, what has happened?'
'She has been slain,' I say, weeping. 'I thought the fiend meant to come for me, but she was itshis victim all along.'
Others are pressing into the room. A man in a nightgown - one of the waiters who served us earlier - turns from the open window. 'There's somebody running across the lawn!'I run to the open window in time to see a huge, distorted figure lope at incredible speed across the lawn. A moment later, ithe has plunged into the lake.
'Oh!' comes a keening wail from behind me. The innkeeper's wife is standing in the doorway, with a host of others crowding behind her, craning their necks to see into the room.
'The murderer is outside!' I shout at them. 'Quickly - after it!'him!'

xoptx You must help to hunt the monster down.

xoptx Stay here with Elizabeth.

I have the hip flask. It isn't expecting that. Braced to dodge a pistol ball, it only succeeds in avoiding part of the flung acid. I have the satisfaction of drawing from it a guttural grunt of pain, but then itI have the hip flask. He isn't expecting that. Braced to dodge a pistol ball, he only succeeds in avoiding part of the flung acid. I have the satisfaction of drawing from him a guttural grunt of pain, but then he takes a powerful step forward and it is as though a boom has smacked me across the chest. I find myself lying stretched out in the mud with on the grass with itshis monstrous form looming over me. The stench of seared flesh hangs between us.
'That's the second time you've tried to burn me,' 'I don't think you have quite got the knack of killing, creator,' ithe hisses through clenched teeth.I launch myself at it. If fury and desperation could carry the fight, I would prevail - and indeed, despite its great strength, for a moment we grapple as equals. We are face to face. But I feel myself weakening as it grows stronger. With a cry I fall back, and it throws me to the ground.I launch myself at him. If fury and desperation could carry the fight, I would prevail - and indeed, despite his great strength, for a moment we grapple as equals. We are face to face. But I feel myself weakening as he grows stronger. With a cry I fall back, and he throws me to the ground.
Then kill me now,' I say. 'Why don't you kill me? What use is it to prolong this torture?'
'What else is there?' ithe says with a dry laugh. 'You're all I have. And, now, I'm all you have.'
Elizabeth!

xoptx Run to her.

xoptx Call for help.

The monster's laughter follows me as I dash inside.
I thrust my head indoors and scream for help. A servant is already there with a candle in her hand. 'Oh, sir,' she says, putting her hand to her mouth. 'Your poor wife.'
'Fetch some men with guns,' I snap back. 'The killer is outside!'
The innkeeper is behind her on the stairs. I have to shake him to get him to listen, but eventually he rounds up two or three men with muskets. As they are pulling overcoats and boats on over their nightgowns, the innkeeper's wife comes hesitantly down the stairs. 'It's Madame Frankenstein...' she says.
'Murdered, yes.' I cherish my knot of cold fury. 'And I mean to catch the murderer this very night.'

Then what I feared is true. I caught the monster on its way out. The deed ithis way out. The deed he came to do is done. Forgetting all thought of pursuit, I rush past the frightened girl.
The landing at the top of the stairs is filled with milling servants. I push my way through. The door of our room is open. Somebody is already inside. The innkeeper's wife. She looks up at me as I cross to the bedside. Brushing her aside, I stare down at the limp, broken body of my wife. I watch my fingers fumbling to find a pulse while my inner voice of calm, the steely rational self, quietly pronounces her already dead.
I need no more than a glance to see she is dead.
I run to the window, the pistol already in my hand. The monster stands below, arms spread as though to acknowledge praise for its despicable act. Instead of praise, I give it a bullet, but the shot thuds into the soft earth - it moved like lightning. I catch sight of it again, bounding down the shore. An instant later ithis despicable act. Instead of praise, I give him a bullet, but the shot thuds into the soft earth - he moved like lightning. I catch sight of him again, bounding down the shore. An instant later he has vanished into the water.I drag some of the others with me to the window. 'There!' The fiend is running swiftly down to the shore, and before our eyes ithe plunges into the lake.
The innkeeper turns to me with a look of disbelief. 'It didn't look human.'
'It isn't. It's a wild beast. Fetch as many men as you can, and tell them to come armed. I want to see its bleeding carcass hanging from a post by dawn.'I embrace her, shower hot kisses on her closed eyelids, anoint her face with tears. But it is no use. The Elizabeth I loved and cherished is gone, erased from existence by the fiend's grasp that still shows in the dented whiteness of her throat.

xoptx You must help to hunt him down.

xoptx You must help to hunt it down.

xoptx Stay here with your wife.

We take boats out onto the lake. Nets are cast, but in vain. After several hours the moon is close to setting and we are forced to return. I see from the sullen faces that most of my companions believe it to be a wild goose chase. After we have landed, a man who I am later told is one of the town councillors takes it upon himself to organize search parties to scour the woods and vineyards by torchlight.
I don't accompany them. I am exhausted, and robbed of my resolve by the knowledge that the monster could lie for an hour on the lake bed, or cling to the freezing heights of one of the surrounding mountains.
Returning to the inn, I cannot make myself climb the stairs to our - to my room. A film seems to descend over my vision. I feel hot and feverish. I sink onto a couch. The fire has gone out in the grate and a chill creeps deep into my bones, but I do not stir. The cup of life is poisoned. I taste the dregs and wonder why death is so unmerciful as to leave me untouched.
Hardly conscious, yet not sleeping, I lie waiting for the dawn of a terrible day, and my eyes restlessly wander the room, as if to seek something I have lost.

Search parties are sent out to scour the lake and surrounding woods. I don't go with them. I am exhausted, and robbed of any hope by the knowledge that the monster could lie for an hour on the lake bed, or cling to the freezing heights of one of the surrounding mountains.
I drive them all from the room. I feel hot and feverish, though the fire has gone out and cold is creeping into the room from the open window. I lie down beside Elizabeth on what was to have been our marriage bed. The cup of life with which we toasted each other is poisoned now. Why is death so unmerciful as to leave me untouched? Why cannot this be the bier on which we two are laid to rest together?
Hardly conscious, yet not sleeping, I await the dawn of a terrible day, and my eyes restlessly wander the room, as if to seek something I have lost.

END OF PART FIVE


PART SIX
I seek no sympathies, nor need;
The thorns which I have reap'd are of the tree
I planted: they have torn me, and I bleed:
I should have known what fruit would spring from such a seed.

CHAPTER ONE
Wandering Ministers of Vengeance

I have hired rowers for the return trip because there is still a strong wind from the west. The fish too are unchanged, slipping through the net of rippled light just as they were when Elizabeth pointed them outyesterday. How can so monumental a change have taken place and reality takes no notice?
The bracing tang of the lake air. The autumnal patchwork of the shores. The watery dawn, with the white sun a shivering droplet balanced on the grey teeth of the mountains.
